If this angle is beta, then the opposite side is a - x, and the hypotenuse is what was in the denominator over there. So this side is sine of beta. And so what this condition is telling us is that alpha = beta. Which is the hidden symmetry in the problem. I don't know if you can actually see it when I show you this thing. But, no matter how I tilt it, actually the two angles from the horizontal are the same. In the middle it's kind of obvious that that should be the case. But on the sides it's not obvious that that's what's happening. Now, this has even, so that's a symmetry, if you like, of the situation. These two angles are equal. But there's something more to be said. If you do a forced diagram for this, what you'll discover is that the tension on the two lines is the same. Which means that when you build something which is hanging like this, it will involve the least stress. If you hang something very heavy, and one side carries twice as much load as the other, then you have twice as much chance of its falling and breaking. If they each hold the same strength, then you've distributed the load in a much more balanced way. So this is a kind of a balance condition, and it's very typical of minimization problems. And fortunately, there are nice solutions which distribute the weight reasonably well. That's certainly the principal of suspension bridges. So yeah, one more question. We're going to now talk about something else. So our next topic is Newton's method. Which is one of the greatest applications of calculus. And I'm going to describe it here for you. And we'll illustrate it on an example, which is solving the equation x^2 = 5. We're going to find the square root of 5. Now, you can actually solve any equation this way. Any equation that you understand, you can solve this way, essentially. So even though I'm doing it for the square root of 5, which is something that you can figure out on your calculator, in fact this is really at the heart of many of the ways in which calculators work. So, the first thing is to make this problem a little bit more abstract. We're going to set f ( x ) = x^2 - 5. And then we're going to solve f (x) = 0. So this is the sort of standard form for solving such a. So you take some either complicated or simple function of x, linear functions are boring, quadratic functions are already interesting. And cubic functions, as I've said a few times, you don't even have formulas for solving. So this would be the only method you have for solving them numerically. So here's how it works. So the idea, I'll plot this function. Here's the function, it's a parabola, y = x^2 - 5. It dips below 0, sorry, it should be centered, but anyway. And now the idea here is to start with a guess. And square root of 5 is pretty close to the square root of 4, which is 2. So my first guess is going to be 2, here. So start with initial guess. So that's our first guess. And now, what we're going to do is we're going to pretend that the function is linear. That's all we're going to do. And then if the function were linear, we're going to try to find where the is. So if the function is linear, what we'll use is we'll plot the point where 2 is on, that is, the point (2, f ( 2 )), and then we're going to draw the tangent line here. And this is going to be our new guess. x = x, which I'll call x1. So the idea here is that this point may be somewhat far from where it crosses, but this point will be a little closer. And now we're going to do this over and over again. And see how fast it gets to the place we're aiming for. So we have to work out what the formulas are. And that's the strategy. So now, the first step here is, we have our guess, we have our tangent line. Which has the formula y - y0 = m(x - x0). So that's the general form for a tangent line. And now, I have to tell you what x1 is. In terms of this tangent line. x1 is the x intercept. The tangent line, if you look over here at the diagram, the tangent line is the orange line. Where that crosses the axis, that's where I want to put x1. So that's the x intercept. Now, how do you find the x intercept? You find it by setting y = 0. That horizontal line is y = 0. So now let's solve. And what we get here is - y divided by - sorry, -y0 / m = x1 - x0. And now I can get a formula for x1. So x1 = x0 - (y0 / m). So it's f' (x0). And this is the whole story. This is the formula which will enable us to calculate basically any root. I'm going to repeat this formula, so I'm going to tell you again what Newton's method is, and put a little more colorful box around it. So Newton's method involves the following formula. In order to get the n + 1st point, that's our better and better guess, I'm going to take the nth one and then I'm going to plug in this formula. So f ( xn ) / f' ( xn ). So this is the basic formula, and if you like, this is the idea of just repeating what I had before. No you can't take her money now. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV3/MT/monmt3.cha = = = Title: Convince Your Parents to Make a Will: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: A will is a legal document that outlines an individual's wishes regarding the distribution of their assets after death. It ensures that your parents' intentions are carried out and can prevent family disputes. This guide aims to help you effectively communicate with your parents about the importance of creating a will and provide them with necessary resources. Remember, patience and understanding are essential throughout this process. Step 1: Research and Understand the Importance of Wills Before discussing the topic with your parents, educate yourself on why having a will is crucial. Key reasons include: - Avoiding intestacy laws (which distribute assets based on government regulations) - Ensuring designated heirs receive specific possessions or amounts - Appointing guardians for minor children - Minimizing estate taxes and probate costs Step 2: Choose the Right Time and Place Find a quiet, comfortable setting where both you and your parents feel at ease. Schedule a time when there won't be any distractions or interruptions. Be mindful of their emotions and approach the conversation with sensitivity. Step 3: Start the Discussion with Empathy Begin by acknowledging that talking about end-of-life matters can be difficult but important. Express your love and concern for their wellbeing while emphasizing the need for proper planning. You might say something like, "I know we don't like thinking about it, but I want to make sure our family is protected no matter what happens." Step 4: Present Facts and Statistics Use clear facts and statistics to support your argument. For example, explain that over half of American adults do not have a will, leaving their estates subject to state laws. Share stories from friends or relatives who experienced complications due to lack of a will. Step 5: Address Common Misconceptions Address common misconceptions about wills, such as: - Only wealthy people need wills (not true - everyone benefits from having a will) - Creating a will is expensive (many online services offer affordable options) - Wills become invalid upon marriage or divorce (they usually must be updated, but they do not automatically expire) Step 6: Offer Assistance with the Process Assure your parents that you will help them through every step, whether it's finding an attorney, researching online options, or simply providing emotional support. Encourage them to involve other siblings or family members if needed. Step 7: Provide Resources and Recommendations Offer reputable sources for creating a will, including local attorneys specializing in estate planning, online legal services, or community workshops. If possible, accompany them to meetings with lawyers or assist in filling out forms. Step 8: Follow Up Regularly Periodically check in with your parents about their progress. If they haven't yet created a will, ask if there's anything preventing them from doing so and address those concerns. Remind them that making a will brings peace of mind and reduces future stress for loved ones. Key Tips and Guidelines: - Respect your parents' decisions even if they initially decline to create a will; continue expressing your concerns without pressuring them. - Emphasize the potential consequences of dying intestate (without a valid will). - Highlight the importance of updating their will regularly, especially after major life events like marriages, divorces, births, adoptions, or significant asset changes. - Consider involving additional family members or professionals to ensure all perspectives are considered. - Maintain confidentiality regarding sensitive information shared during these conversations. [22 sec; noises, S drinking his apple juice; burps; 2 more sec] Title: How to Give Selamectin to Cats with Parasites Selamectin is a topical medication used to treat and prevent parasitic infestations in cats. It is effective against various pests such as fleas, ear mites, heartworms, hookworms, and roundworms. This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on administering selamectin to your cat properly and safely. **Step 1: Consult Your Veterinarian** Before starting any new medication, consult your veterinarian first. They can confirm if your cat has a parasite infection requiring treatment with selamectin or recommend alternative treatments based on your pet's medical history. *Key Tip*: Always follow your vet's advice regarding dosage and frequency of application. Never exceed the recommended dose without consulting them. **Step 2: Purchase the Correct Product** Ensure that you buy a product containing only selamectin or a combination of selamectin and other FDA-approved ingredients for felines. Brands like Revolution carry this active ingredient. Be cautious about counterfeit products; always purchase from reputable sources. *Guideline*: Check the expiration date before buying. Using outdated medications may reduce effectiveness or even harm your cat. **Step 3: Prepare the Medication** Read the manufacturer's instructions carefully before applying the medicine. Generally, selamectin comes in premeasured tubes designed for easy application directly onto the skin. *Tip*: Make sure you understand whether the entire contents of the tube need to be applied at once or divided over multiple applications. **Step 4: Choose the Right Time and Place** Choose a quiet time when your cat is calm and relaxed. A comfortable room where they usually spend their leisure time would work best. *Guideline*: If possible, have another person assist you during the administration process. One can hold the cat gently while the other applies the medication. **Step 5: Hold Your Cat Properly** Approach your cat slowly so as not to startle them. Position yourself behind them so they cannot see what you are doing. Gently lift their tail and steady their body with one hand. Use your other hand to apply the medication. *Key Tip*: Avoid handling your cat too roughly as it could cause stress and potentially lead to injury. **Step 6: Apply the Medicine** Part the fur between the shoulder blades until you reach the skin. Squeeze the entire contents of the applicator directly onto the skin. Try to avoid getting any medication on your hands. *GuideLine*: Immediately after application, rub the area thoroughly to ensure the solution spreads across the skin surface and gets absorbed quickly. **Step 7: Monitor Your Cat Post-Application** Monitor your cat post-application for any signs of discomfort or adverse reactions. These might include excessive scratching, biting, or licking at the application site. *Tip*: If your cat seems unusually distressed or shows severe side effects, contact your vet immediately. **Step 8: Maintain Regular Treatment Schedule** Adhere strictly to the prescribed schedule advised by your vet. For ongoing prevention, monthly treatments are typically required. *Guideline*: Set reminders for future applications to maintain consistency. Remember, treating your cat for parasites involves more than just using medication. Regular grooming, cleaning bedding, vacuuming carpets, and preventing exposure to infected animals also play crucial roles in managing parasitic infestations effectively. [the sound of a siren can be heard through the window] Elizabethan English did not sound like modern English as it is spoken in England no Cockney, no Uppah Clahss refinement. It was an earthy, vigorous speech. Here are some of the characteristic sounds: SHORT A: As in the words want or father. Always pronounced with the flat-sounding A we use in words like WAX or CAT or FLAT, never with an ah-sound. LONG A: As in the words make or take. Sounded as a short E, so they come out mek and tek. Think of the way the name of the River Thames is pronounced. LONG I: As in the words die or ice, also where Y occurs sounded as long I as in my or try. Sounded as uh-ee, a long drawn-out sound. This is the quintessential Peasant noise. Die becomes duh-ee, my becomes muh-ee. But the short I sound (as in sir, girl, pin) is unchanged, exactly like what we use today. SHORT O: As in the words love or dove. Sounded like a long U or double O, the way we pronounce book or cook. All you need is luv, as a wise man once said. SHORT U: As in the words cup or run. Sounded like a long U or double O also, so they come out coop and roon. EA: As in the words head, bread, or dead. These are given a long A pronunciation, which makes them sound very American Country: haid, braid, daid. OU AND OW: As in the words house, about, down. Do like the Canadians! Pronounce them with an uh-oo dipthong. Huh-oose, abuh-oot, duh-oon. AI AND AY: As in the words maid and day. Its a flat A followed by that peasanty long I, so its sounded maa-eed and daa-ee. H AND R: These consonants are always pronounced. Never drop the H, as modern Cockneys do. Its Head, not ed, Here, not ere. The letter R is pronounced with all the glory of a pirate on the high seas: fatherrrr and ratherrrr and herrre, not fathah or rathah or heah. Avoid the Scots burred R, though, unless youre playing a Scot. THEE AND THOU: These are the informal pronouns. They are used to anyone who is your equal and intimate, such as a spouse or good friend, and also to anyone below you in station, such as a servant or child. They are also used when delivering an insult; or in prayer, because the Almighty is presumed to be your intimate. Thee and thou are not the same word, by the way. One is the subject, the other the object of the sentence, as in: I say to thee, I gave it unto thee as opposed to Thou hast a fair face, Thou wilt run away, What hast thou done? THY AND THINE are the informal equivalents of you and yours. YOU is the formal pronoun, used to address strangers and anyone above you in rank. It is also used as a sign of respect to ones parents or elders. THE WORD YE: Ye is a pronoun, the plural of you or thou, used to address several people at once: as in O come, all ye faithful. It is not ye olde-fashioned way of saying the. We tend to assume it is, because we misread old signs that have an old symbol for TH that looks like a crooked letter Y, but it isnt. Theres no ye olde anything. SHALL WE CONJUGATE? Notice that those old-fashioned eth and est endings only go on the ends of verbs, and are only correct with the informal and plural pronouns. HELLO OR GOODBYE: To greet someone, say Good Morrow or Good Day or Well met! Or the very old-fashioned God ye good den. How now is used for "How are you doing?" To bid them goodbye, say Fare thee well or God keep thee. If youre going to be right back, say anon, which means "in a minute" or "See you soon." TO A MAN: Use Sir, or Master, or My Lord. Master is used where we would use Mister. Goodman can be used to a peasant, Father or Gaffer to an older man. A little boy might be addressed as Young Master, or by Little Lad, or by Sirrah; but Sirrah means rascal, rogue or knave, too. TO A WOMAN: Use Madam, or Mistress, or My Lady. Mistress is used exactly as we use Ms now, courteous address to a married or unmarried woman. Goodwife can be used to a peasant, Mother or Gammer to an older lady. A little girl can be addressed as Little Maid or Little Lady. YES AND NO: Try Aye and Nay, or Yea and Nay. PLEASE, THANK YOU AND MORE: For Please try Prithee, Pray, I pray you, or An it please thee. For Thank You try I thank you, I thank thee, or Many Thanks. The response would be simply You are most welcome, or Thou art welcome. To say Excuse me, try I cry you mercy, or I crave your pardon. EXCITEMENT: Where youd say, "Wow!" try Marry! or Faith! or even Swounds! This last is short for Gods Wounds and refers to the five wounds of Christ. Where youd say, "No kidding?" try Forsooth! or Is it even so? To express disbelief slightly more forcefully, try Go to! or Thou liest! And to express dismay, mild anger, or disappointment, try Fie! or Alack! or Alackaday! or Alas! Or Out upon it/him/her /them! METAPHORS AND SIMILES: Everyone drew on the Bible: Strong as Samson, Wicked as Herod. Also widely known were the Robin Hood and King Arthur stories. Upper-middle-class persons and nobles might sprinkle their speech with references to the Greek and Roman classics: Beauteous as Venus, Bright as Phoebus Apollo. Years of experience have proven that the very best sources for language and grammar are the King James Version of the Bible and the plays of William Shakespeare, which are available everywhere. Other writers from the period, such as Christopher Marlowe, are also excellent to read if you want a sense of how the language is used. Very few Shakespeare plays are produced using the sounds of Elizabethan; most of them use a modern stage English accent. However, believe it or not, a basic Pirate accent is close to correct and works very well for our purposes. And its fun! [puts the silver magnet ball in between the two magnet balls that are on the washers] Learn more physics! How can you see light travel? - Tony Tse (age 10) E. R. Taylor, S.F., CA, United States An important point in being able to see something is that light it has to bounce off of something before going to our eye. Think of a regular light bulb. It sends out a lot of light in all directions when it is turned on. If you look right into the light (don't so this because it hurts your eyes), it is bright because a lot of light is going straight into our eyes. But when you look elsewhere, you don't see the stream of light going everywhere. In order for us to see that light, it has to bounce off something first, that's how it get's a specific color. If you look at your hand, you are seeing the light that is bouncing off your hand and going into your eyes. All this is possible because light moves all over the place and bounces around. If it didn't move, we wouldn't be able to see anything. In case you're curious, light moves pretty fast. It moves at about 1 billion kilometers per hour or about 670 million miles an hour. To make it easier to picture this, in less than 5 seconds, light can travel from the Earth to the moon and back again! (published on 10/22/2007) Follow-up on this answer. Title: How to Get TetherPro to Work with Older Digital Cameras Introduction: Tethering your digital camera to your computer can be incredibly useful, especially when shooting in a studio or other controlled environment. It allows you to view photos immediately after they're taken, make adjustments on-the-fly, and quickly transfer files to post-production software. However, if you have an older digital camera, you might find that many modern tethering solutions don't support your device. This guide will walk you through setting up TetherPro, specifically designed for compatibility with a wide range of cameras, both old and new. Step 1: Check Compatibility Before proceeding, ensure that your camera is listed as compatible with TetherPro. Visit the manufacturer's website and consult their supported devices list. If your model isn't there, unfortunately, TetherPro may not work for you. Key Tip: Some manufacturers provide firmware updates which add tethering capabilities. Be sure to check this too! Step 2: Download & Install Software Download the latest version of TetherPro from its official source. Run the installer file once downloaded; most systems require administrative rights to perform installation. Follow all prompts during setup, ensuring anti-virus software doesn't flag any components. Guideline: Always choose 'Custom Installation,' giving you control over what gets installed. Uncheck unnecessary options like browser toolbars. Step 3: Connect Camera via USB Cable Connect your camera to your computer using the appropriate USB cable (usually provided by the camera manufacturer). Newer cameras typically use mini or microUSB cables while older ones might need a full-size USB connector. Make sure the connection is secure at both ends. Key Point: Using cheap or poor quality cables could lead to unreliable connections or even damage to your equipment. Invest in good quality cables. Step 4: Launch TetherPro Launch TetherPro on your PC. Depending upon your system settings, you may see a prompt asking permission to access your hardware. Grant these permissions as needed. Step 5: Select Your Camera Model From the main menu of TetherPro, select your specific camera model from the dropdown menu. If your camera model isn't showing up, double-check Step 1 - compatibility issues are often due to unsupported models. Step 6: Set Up Shooting Preferences Now configure your shooting preferences within TetherPro. These include capture settings, naming conventions, storage locations, etc., depending on your needs and the features offered by your camera. Step 7: Start Live View If available, initiate live view mode within TetherPro. This provides real-time feedback about framing, focus, exposure, etc., directly onto your monitor screen. Step 8: Begin Shooting With everything set up, start taking pictures. As soon as you hit the shutter button on your camera, the captured image should appear almost instantly on your computer screen thanks to TetherPro's fast data transfer rates. Conclusion: While getting TetherPro working with older digital cameras requires several steps, following them closely ensures seamless integration between your gear and computer. Remember always to confirm compatibility before purchasing or attempting to connect anything. Happy shooting! At last he got the rest of the beans planted and went home. This means that each time something is compressed with it, some information is lost. This information can not be recovered. When encoding to MP3 converting some audio data to it), the encoder is smart. The human ear can not hear certain sounds if they are masked by other sounds. This means that when encoding certain sound samples, some sounds can be left out as they will not be heard. This makes it possible to compress audio by a factor of about 5, without noticeable change to the sound of the music. With slight audible changes to the sound, compression factors of about 10 are possible. Having seen Jermyn drive away an hour before, he was in no fear: he walked at once to the Fox and Hounds and asked to speak to Mr. Johnson. A brief interview, in which Christian ascertained that he had before him the Johnson mentioned by the bill-sticker, issued in the appointment of a longer one at a later hour; and before they left Duffield they had come not exactly to a mutual understanding, but to an exchange of information mutually welcome. Christian had been very cautious in the commencement, only intimating that he knew something important which some chance hints had induced him to think might be interesting to Mr. Johnson, but that this entirely depended on how far he had a common interest with Mr. Jermyn. Johnson replied that he had much business in which that gentleman was not concerned, but that to a certain extent they had a common interest. Probably then, Christian observed, the affairs of the Transome estate were part of the business in which Mr. Jermyn and Mr. Johnson might be understood to represent each other, in which case he need not detain Mr. Johnson? You think that we're making you sleep. Keeping Track and Displaying Data Last year, children represented and compared data using tally marks. Begin with this concept to reinforce the idea that each tally mark stands for one piece of data that is collected. The connection between tally marks and summarizing data can then be shown visually. Prerequisite Skills and Concepts: Children should have basic counting skills and be able to skip-count by fives. Children should be able to make tally marks and interpret data. Write the months of the year in large letters on the chalkboard. - Say: Today, we will make a tally chart of the birthdays in our class. It will show the month of each person's birthday. I need a volunteer to record the tally marks. Choose a volunteer to write one tally mark next to the month that is announced by each student. - Ask: In which month is your birthday? Ask each child the same question. As the volunteer records the tallies, children should realize that each student is receiving their own tally mark on the board. This is crucial to understanding that each tally mark represents an individual's birthday. - Ask: How can we show tally marks so they will be easier to count later? Have volunteers offer ways to show tallies for easier counting. Lead children to recommend that every fifth tally be a slash through the first four existing tallies. This offers an easy method for skip-counting by fives. Continue questioning each student until all birthday months are recorded. Allow time for the class to study the tally chart. - Ask: How many students are present today? Please count the total number of students in class today. Children should respond with the correct number of students in the class. - Ask: How many tally marks should be on the board? Children should realize that the number of tally marks should be the same as the number of students in the class. If the numbers do not match, find and correct the problem. - Ask: How did you count the tallies? Children should realize they can skip-count by fives for the tallies with diagonals. Then they can count up by ones for the single tallies left on the board. - Ask: What conclusions can you draw just by looking at the tally chart on the board? Allow enough time for all children to draw at least one conclusion. Write each conclusion on the board. Conclusions can include the following: most popular month for birthdays, least popular month for birthdays, months with no birthdays, comparison of tallies for 2 or more months Make a copy of the tally chart on the board. You may use it later to demonstrate how to create a bar graph or line plot from existing data. He wanted to play with it, but he remembered what his mom had told him. She said real knives are not for playing. They can hurt people. Tim put the real knife back and went to find his toy knife. Title: How to Care for a Weepul A weepul is a small, furry toy with beads inside that allow it to move its arms and legs when shaken or tossed. These toys are often used as promotional items at events and can provide hours of entertainment. However, they do require some basic care to keep them looking and moving their best. Here's a comprehensive guide on how to care for your weepul: **Step 1: Handling Your Weepul** When handling your weepul, hold it gently by the body or the head. Avoid grabbing its limbs too tightly as this could cause damage to the internal beads and strings. Regularly washing your hands before playing with your weepul will also help prevent dirt buildup. *Key Tip:* Be mindful of where you leave your weepul after playtime. Keeping it away from pets and young children can prevent accidental harm or destruction. **Step 2: Cleaning Your Weepul** Weepuls aren't designed for extensive water exposure, so spot cleaning is usually sufficient. Use a damp cloth lightly moistened with warm water to wipe down any dirty areas. Gently rub until clean; do not scrub harshly as this may remove color from the fabric. For more thorough cleaning, you can use a mild detergent diluted in water (about one teaspoon per cup). Dip the cloth into this solution, squeeze out excess liquid, then carefully blot stained spots. Afterward, rinse the area with another damp cloth dipped only in plain water. Remember never to submerge a weepul in water or put it in a washing machine. This could ruin the inner workings and exterior appearance. *Guideline:* Always let your weepul air dry completely before playing with it again. Never use heat sources like hairdryers or radiators to speed up the process, as these could melt the plastic parts. **Step 3: Storing Your Weepul** Proper storage ensures your weepul stays dust-free and maintains its shape. Store it in a cool, dry place, preferably inside a breathable bag or box. If possible, avoid stacking heavy objects on top of the storage container, which might flatten your weepul over time. *Key Tip:* Consider placing silica gel packets in the storage container to absorb moisture and prevent mold growth. **Step 4: Fixing Loose Limbs** If your weepul's limb becomes loose due to broken strings, you can attempt to fix it yourself using craft glue suitable for fabrics. First, locate the ends of the severed string(s) within the limb opening. Apply a tiny amount of glue onto the end of the string, then reinsert it back into the original hole or slot. Hold the limb steady until the adhesive sets. Please note that repair success depends on the extent of damage. Severe cases might necessitate professional assistance or replacement. *Guideline:* Refrain from excessive movement of the repaired limb until fully dried to ensure proper bonding. By following these steps and maintaining good habits, your weepul should remain happy and healthy for many plays to come! Okay well 'at's not too bad then. Delete Browsing History in Mozilla Firefox Mozilla Firefox is a popular web browser that stores your browsing history to help you quickly access previously visited websites. However, there might be instances when you want to delete this information for privacy reasons or free up storage space. This tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting your browsing history in Mozilla Firefox, providing in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines along the way. **Step 1: Open Mozilla Firefox** To begin, launch Mozilla Firefox on your computer by clicking its icon from the desktop or application menu. **Step 2: Access the Library window** Click on the "Library" button (three horizontal lines) located at the top right corner of the Firefox interface. A dropdown menu appears; click on "History." Alternatively, press `Ctrl + H` on Windows or `Cmd + Shift + H` on macOS to open the History panel directly. The library window provides an overview of your browsing activities, including bookmarks, downloads, history, and more. ![Accessing the Library Window](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ad/Clear-Your-Browser's-Cache-in-Firefox-Step-3.jpg/aid4586709-v4- Ya because you might slide and get hurt. Example: It's sticky. It's shiny. But what is earwax, anyway - and where does it come from? Earwax is made in the outer ear canal. This is the area between the fleshy part of the ear on the outside of your head (the part you can see) and the middle ear. The skin in the outer ear canal has special glands that produce earwax. The fancy name for this waxy stuff is cerumen (say: suh-roo-mun). After the wax is produced, it slowly makes its way through the outer ear canal to the opening of the ear. Then it either falls out or is removed when you wash. In most people, the outer ear canal makes earwax all the time, so the canal always has enough wax in it. So why do we need wax? Earwax has several important jobs. First, it protects and moisturizes the skin of ear canal, preventing dry, itchy ears. Second, it contains special chemicals that fight off infections that could hurt the skin inside the ear canal. Finally, it acts as a shield between the outside world and the eardrum. When dust, dirt, and other things enter your ear, the earwax traps them so they can't travel any further. If you want to get rid of earwax, here's what you need to do: nothing! Most kids don't need to do anything special to remove earwax. If you wash your hair regularly, this is enough to keep your ears clean. You can wipe the outside of your ear with a washcloth but don't use a cotton swab, your finger, or anything else to poke around inside your ear to remove earwax. Your ear canal and eardrum are very delicate, and you may hurt them or cause bleeding by trying to get rid of wax this way. Poking around in your ear can also push and pack the wax in further. In some kids, one or both ear canals make extra earwax. If this sounds like you, tell an adult. Doctors often can prescribe special medicines that are placed in the ear to get rid of extra wax. Long jump is a sport in which, the athlete who jumps the longest by legal means, wins. The athlete begins running from his or her starting position and after attaining sufficient velocity, jumps, landing in a sand pit, which is fitted with distance markers. There is a foul line, in the run-up area, that the athlete has to be aware of; jumping from beyond this line results in a 'foul jump'. Simple Linking Verbs "is" and "are" by Amy Miller Sonntag, M.A., CCC-SLP Expected Outcome: The person will use the linking verbs "is" and "are" with -ing verbs. Purpose: to learn to say the linking verbs "is" and "are" and expand length of utterance. Target Language Skill: Use the linking verbs "is" and "are" and use them with -ing verbs 1. The person will say the word "is" appropriately in a sentence. 2. The person will say the word "are" appropriately in a sentence. 3. The person will say the word "is" + an -ing verb. 4. The person will say the word "are" + an -ing verb. 5. The person will say the word a sentence with a "noun + linking verb + -ing verb." 6. The person will say the word a sentence with a "noun + linking verb + -ing verb + noun." 1. Model and prompt the linking verbs on the device. 2. Model using "is" and "are" in simple sentences. 3. Use the "Stage 4 - Is and Are Linking Verbs" activities to practice saying "is" and "are." 4. Praise the client when you hear them use the words "is" and "are" appropriately. You could even have a reward chart that is marked each time the word is used and after 10 correct usages a larger prize is earned. 5. During spontaneous speech model for the client when "is" and "are" could be used appropriately. 1. Read the Power Point book together. The first time through, ask the person to read just the linking verbs. Remember to practice the pronoun-verb phrases in Unity that save keystrokes. 2. Read the Power Point book again, this time ask the person to read as many words as possible. Model and teach other words. 3. Change the names of the people in the story to familiar names. Read the story to someone else. 1. Connect the device to a computer with either a USB or a Blue Tooth adapter. Open a word document and as the person to practice writing the target vocabulary words. 2. Help the person to make up a story using "is" and "are". Provide only as much help as necessary. 3. In a word document, insert graphics of familiar people. Ask the person to write captions for the pictures. Encourage using "is" and "are" in the captions. Save and print for sharing. 1. Model and teach pronoun-verb phrases that use "is" and "are", such as "he is" and "we are". Ask the person to make up sentences with these phrases. 2. Find other reading books that have "is" and "are" in sentences. Read the books together. Here's an idea that will help your child improve his note-taking skills. With your child, create sheets of paper with note- taking prompts on them. You will need four different sheets of paper: One to use before the lecture to help your child identify what he already knows about the topic. Two to use during the lecture to focus on main points. A fourth for after the lecture to help him summarize and remember the information. On the pre-lecture sheet, have your child write these two questions: What is today's topic? What do I already know about this topic? For note-taking during the lecture, have your child create three sections: Describe the main points the teacher makes. Include details as they're being discussed. Have five to seven numbers on the two sheets. Add space after each number for writing. Summarize. Tell how the main ideas are related. List new vocabulary. On the after-lecture sheet, ask your child to list five main points of the lecture and describe each point. Title: Draw a Simple House Introduction: Drawing can be a fun and rewarding hobby that allows you to express your creativity. This tutorial will guide you through the process of drawing a simple house using basic shapes and techniques. By following these steps, you'll create a charming little house with windows, doors, and a pitched roof. Let's get started! Step 1: Sketch an outline Using a light pencil (2H or HB), draw a horizontal rectangle as the base of your house. Then sketch two vertical rectangles at either end of the first one; these will serve as the side walls. Lastly, add another smaller rectangle overlapping one of the larger ones to form the front door. Make sure all lines are light so they can be erased later. Key tip: Keep proportions consistent for a balanced composition. A common ratio is 3:2:1 for length-width-height when creating the main body of the house. Step 2: Define the window openings On both sides of the door, lightly sketch two squares or rectangles above the base line - these will represent the windows. Remember, keep them symmetrical and ensure their size fits within the wall dimensions. Guideline: The height of the window should ideally take up about half the total wall height. Step 3: Add architectural details To give your house some character, include features like shingles, bricks, or siding by dividing the roof and outer walls into small sections using short parallel lines. For added realism, angle the lines slightly upward towards the peak of the roof. Step 4: Create perspective Give your house depth by adding perspective. To do this, gradually make the top edges of the side walls converge towards a vanishing point just beyond the center of the house. Also, gently curve the bottom edge of the base rectangle to suggest a slight downward slope away from viewer. Step 5: Refine the outlines Now go over your initial sketches with darker but still relatively light strokes, making any necessary adjustments as you proceed. Be careful not to press too hard; we want to maintain the option of correcting mistakes later if needed. Step 6: Block in solid colors Grab a medium graphite pencil (like B) and fill in areas between the outlines with uniform tone. Shade the entire background behind the house as well to provide contrast and set it apart visually. Step 7: Render shadows and highlights Identify where sunlight would hit the surface of the house based on its orientation relative to the sun. Darken those exposed surfaces accordingly while leaving highlight areas untouched or barely touched. Soften harsh transitions with blending stumps or tortillons for smooth gradients. Step 8: Highlight textures Use cross-hatching or stippling techniques to emphasize specific materials such as wood grain, brick patterns, or glass reflections on the windows. Vary pressure, direction, and spacing of marks to convey different textures effectively. Step 9: Final touches Examine your artwork carefully for consistency and accuracy. Correct any discrepancies, reinforce weak lines, enhance details, or deepen shadows where needed. Don't forget to sign your masterpiece once satisfied! Conclusion: Congratulations! You have successfully drawn a simple yet appealing house using traditional drawing techniques. Practice regularly and experiment with various styles, perspectives, lighting conditions, and environments to further develop your skills. Happy drawing! It couldn't be burglars, you know." [laughs] and he jumped up and he said Title: Create a Harmless Virus Using Notepad Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through creating a harmless virus using Notepad. This is purely educational and should never be used maliciously. The term "virus" can have negative connotations, but this project will result in a simple script that does nothing more than display a message box when opened. It's important to note that real viruses are illegal and unethical. **Disclaimer:** This article is intended for educational purposes only. Any actions or outcomes resulting from following the steps outlined below are solely your responsibility. Misuse of information presented here can lead to legal consequences. Step 1: Opening Notepad ---------------------- Click the Windows Start button (or press `Win`), type "Notepad," then click on the Notepad app icon. A new blank document will open. Step 2: Writing the Code ------------------------ In the newly opened Notepad window, write the following code exactly as shown: ```vbnet @echo off msg * /time:60 Hello, World! pause exit ``` Let's examine what each line does: - **@echo off**: Prevents command prompt echoing, making the output cleaner by hiding unnecessary text. - **msg \* /time:60 Hello, World!**: Displays a popup message with the title 'Microsoft Windows User Message' and the content 'Hello, World!' for 60 seconds. Replace 'Hello, World!' with any custom message you want to show up. - **pause**: Pauses execution until the user presses any key. This allows users to read the message before the batch file closes automatically. - **exit**: Closes the Command Prompt window once the pause has been released. Step 3: Saving the File ----------------------- After writing the code, go to "File" > "Save As." Choose a location where you want to save your file, give it a name ending in ".bat" (e.g., "hello\_world.bat"). Make sure "All Files" is selected under "Save as Type". Click "Save." Remember, giving files executable names may cause confusion later. Always choose something unique yet descriptive enough so you know its purpose. Step 4: Running the Batch File ------------------------------- Navigate to the saved location via File Explorer, right-click the created file, select "Run as administrator." You'll see a Command Prompt flash briefly followed by your custom message appearing in a popup dialog. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Be cautious about distributing such scripts even if they're meant to be harmless. People might misinterpret them as actual threats. - Never include harmful commands in these types of scripts. They could potentially damage systems or data. - Respect others' privacy and computers; do not run any scripts without explicit permission. I got a lot of bread XXX red and black. The opposite of polytheism is monotheism, which is the belief in only one god, practiced by the monotheistic religions of Judaism, Christianity, Islam, Sikhism, etc. Title: How to Grip a Golf Club Like a Pro Introduction: A proper golf grip is crucial for controlling your shots and hitting them straight. It's one of the fundamentals that every golfer should master. This tutorial will guide you through the steps to grip a golf club correctly, with an explanation of why each step is important and what benefits it brings. By following these guidelines, you can improve your game significantly. Step 1: Choose the Right Size Golf Glove Before gripping the club, make sure you have the right size glove. A well-fitted glove provides better control over the club and reduces blistering or discomfort during swings. Visit a local pro shop where professionals can help measure your hand size and recommend suitable gloves. Step 2: Position Your Hands on the Club Place both hands on the golf club so they are close together near the end of the shaft (for irons) or just below the logo (on woods). The distance between your hands depends on personal preference but generally keeps fingers from overlapping too much while still maintaining contact. Key Tip: Avoid holding the club too far down the grip as this restricts movement and limits power transfer at impact. Step 3: Interlock or Overlap Your Fingers There are two common ways to interlock or overlap your pinky finger of your right hand (for right-handed golfers) under the index finger of your left hand - the interlocking grip and the overlapping grip (also known as Vardon grip). Both methods provide stability and ensure consistent hand placement. Interlocking Grip: Link your pinky finger of your right hand and your index finger of your left hand by hooking them together like links in a chain. This type of grip works best for those with smaller hands or who struggle to maintain a secure connection using the overlapping method. Overlapping Grip: Place the pinky finger of your right hand directly onto the gap between the index and middle fingers of your left hand. Many professional golfers prefer this method because it creates more surface area contact between the hands and enhances control. Guideline: Whichever option you choose, aim for creating a solid link between your hands without applying excessive pressure. Step 4: Adjust Hand Pressure Maintain light yet firm pressure throughout your entire grip. Too tight a hold results in tension buildup which affects swing speed and accuracy; conversely, a loose grip leads to loss of control. Imagine squeezing lemons gently - neither crushing nor dropping them. Step 5: Check Alignment Ensure your thumbs point towards the target line. For most players, the 'V' shape formed by thumb and forefinger on each hand should be pointing roughly toward the right shoulder (for righties) or left shoulder (for lefties). Correct alignment ensures correct swing path, promoting accurate shot making. Key Tips: - Keep your wrists firm but flexible. - Maintain equal pressure across all fingers. - Practice regularly to reinforce muscle memory. Conclusion: With regular practice and patience, a good golf grip becomes second nature, enabling you to focus on other aspects of your swing such as posture, alignment, tempo, etc. Remember, there's no single perfect way to grip a golf club; find what feels comfortable and natural to you. As always, seeking advice from experienced instructors or coaches can accelerate learning progression. Happy golfing! I don't know what you're saying if your mouth is full of milk. View Now Mars - Dry Ice and Dunes We tend to think of Mars as a dead planet where all the exciting geologic activity happened billions of years ago. But information from the Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter shows us that Mars is still a very dynamic place. Mars has a permanent ice cap made up of water ice at its north pole. This pole is ringed by sand dunes, which we're looking straight down on. These dunes are made of small bits of basalt, dark volcanic rock similar to the rocks found around the Hawaiian volcanoes. In the fall and winter, the temperatures are so cold that 30 percent of the Martian carbon dioxide atmosphere freezes onto the ground, forming a seasonal polar cap. The ground gets covered with a bright layer of seasonal carbon dioxide ice, or "dry ice," that's about one to two feet thick. When we flip between images taken at different times in the spring, we can see the dry ice cracks form under the sun's heat as the solid carbon dioxide turns into gas. The gas beneath the ice escapes, carrying dark sand and dust that move, as we can see, down the steep sides of the dunes. We are now looking at different parts of the vast field of dunes. These are called Barchan dunes. At the start of this time-lapse, the dunes are covered with seasonal dry ice so everything is roughly the same color. The dark streaks and splotches are sand from the dune. There are small splotches of sand at the crest of the dune. Then we start seeing the ice crack. Sand is pushed to the top of the ice layer by the escaping gas outlining the cracks. As time goes on, the cracks widen and fresh bright frost condenses in the vicinity. Now, we see sand coming from the crest of the dune and sliding down the steep dune slopes. A ferocious wind has picked up, blowing sand and dust across the dune. Until the dark sand dune is free of seasonal ice. Let's watch that whole sequence again. We start with ice-covered dunes in the early Spring. Some areas of the ice rupture and crack, allowing sand and dust to escape along with the escaping gas. The gas from the dry ice destabilizes the slopes, reshaping the dunes. This activity happens every Spring in the vast dune fields of the Martian polar regions. These are not the sort of events that would naturally occurs on Earth. Mars may look Earth-like, but in some ways it is a very different planet from our own. View Now Coop is a Cupid. He was sent to Phoebe by the Elders to help her find love. He realizes that he loves her and he wants to be with her. Phoebe does not feel the same way until the series finale. You like to bang on the Shoji door. a and me and b BUH. [as he hooks clothes pins on the line] [CHI is struggling trying to get a man in the helicopter] She enjoyed the spoons and the food. Harmonics in music are notes which are produced in a special way. They are notes which are produced as part of the "harmonic series". In physics a harmonic is a wave which is added to the basic fundamental wave. In this article we are talking about sound waves, and we can understand it clearly by looking at the strings of a musical instrument. When a violinist plays a note on a violin string the string starts to vibrate very fast. This vibration makes the air vibrate and the sound waves travel to our ear so that we can hear it. If the note was absolutely pure the string would move like a sine wave. Sine waves can only be made electronically and they sound very boring to us. The note played on the violin string makes the string vibrate in a very complicated way. There is the basic note (the fundamental), but added to that are lots of other little notes that all add up to a sound in a special way that tells us that it is a violin playing and not a clarinet or a human voice. The higher the note the faster the string vibrates. An A above middle C (the violinist's A string) vibrates at 440Hz (440 times per second). This is the "fundamental" or "first harmonic". The second harmonic is vibrating twice as fast (ratio 2:1): 880Hz. This gives an A an octave higher. The third harmonic will give a ratio 3:2. This will be an E (an octave and a fifth above the fundamental). The higher the harmonic the quieter it is, but the ratio is always a whole number (not a fraction). Every note that we hear on an instrument is really a combination of several notes or "harmonics", even although we may not realize that we are hearing more than one note at a time. Play the lowest C on the piano. Now find the next C which is an octave higher. Press this key very slowly so that it does not sound and hold it down. While holding it down play the bottom C again making it loud and very short. The C that is being held silently will now sound. This is because the strings of that C are vibrating a little because it is a harmonic of the low C (they can vibrate because the damper is off the string while the note is being held down). The same can be done holding the next G down, then the next C, then the E. The higher the note the fainter (quieter) the harmonics become. The musical example below shows the notes of a harmonic series in musical notation. To hear the notes of a harmonic series click here: clicking here. Playing harmonics on instruments [change] Musicians sometimes need to play harmonics on their instruments. In musical notation this is shown by placing a little circle above the note. A violinist can place his or her finger very lightly on a string so that it divides the string into half. He will hear a harmonic (the note an octave higher than the open string). By placing his fingers in other places he can get more harmonics, e.g. by touching the string a quarter of the way down he gets the next harmonic. "Artificial harmonics" can be played by stopping a string with the finger in the usual way (so that the string is now shorter) and placing the little finger farther up the string to get a harmonic of the stopped note. Artificial harmonics are written with diamond-shaped note heads. They are very hard to play well. Harpists can play harmonics with their left hand by stopping the string with the side of the hand (near the little finger) and plucking with the thumb or finger. Up to 3 notes can be played by the left hand. They can play harmonics with the right hand by stopping the string with the upper knuckle of the second finger and plucking with the thumb. Only one harmonic note can be played by the right hand. Harmonics on the harp sound very beautiful. Players of woodwind and brass instruments play many of their notes by blowing slightly stronger (overblowing) to get a higher series of notes. Instruments such as the recorder can play chords by making several harmonics sound together, but this is extremely difficult to do well and only found in modern music for virtuoso players. On April 18, 1977, Carter delivered a televised speech declaring that the U.S. energy crisis during the 1970s was like war. He supported energy conservation by all Americans and added solar water heating panels on the White House. He wore sweaters because he turned down the heat in the White House. On August 4, 1977, Carter signed the Department of Energy Organization Act of 1977, forming the Department of Energy, the first new cabinet position in eleven years. During the signing ceremony, Carter said the current "crisis of energy shortages" made him create the Department of Energy. At the start of a September 1977 news conference, Carter said that the House of Representatives had "adopted almost all" of the energy proposal. The following month, on October 13, Carter stated he believed in the Senate's ability to pass the energy reform bill and said that "the most important domestic issue that we will face while I am in office" was the energy criss. Tem jin was born in 1162 into an influential family who were part of a Mongol tribe near Burkhan Khaldun mountain and the Onon and Kherlen Rivers in modern-day Mongolia, not far from the current capital Ulaanbaatar. The Secret History of the Mongols reports that Tem jin was born with a blood clot grasped in his fist, a sign that he was destined to become a great leader. He was the second-oldest son of his father Yes khei, a minor tribal chief of the Kiyad and an ally of Ong Khan of the Kerait tribe, and the oldest son of his mother Hoelun. According to the Secret History, Tem jin was named after a Tatar chieftain whom his father had just captured. The name also suggests that they may have been descended from a family of blacksmiths. If necessary, convert the file using media conversion software like Audacity, Freemake Video Converter, or CloudConvert. Title: How to Choose Vegan Beer Introduction: More people are choosing a vegan lifestyle due to ethical, environmental, or health reasons. When it comes to beer, many brands may not be entirely vegan-friendly due to fining agents used during the brewing process. This tutorial will guide you through selecting vegan beer options with clear steps, helpful explanations, and useful tips. Step 1: Understand Why Some Beers Aren't Vegan During the production of beer, fining agents are often added to clarify the liquid by removing suspended particles. Commonly used fining agents include isinglass (from fish bladders), gelatin (derived from animal bones), casein (a milk protein), and chitosan (made from crustacean shells). These animal products make some beers non-vegan. Key Tip: Look out for labels indicating that the beer is vegetarian or vegan-friendly. If these aren't available, research the brand and its brewing methods. Step 2: Research Popular Vegan Beer Brands There are numerous vegan beer brands available worldwide. Here are a few popular ones: - Brewdog - Guinness (switched to using a plant-based fining agent) - Heineken - Corona Extra - Amstel - Stella Artois - Beck's - Carlsberg - Budweiser (excludes North American varieties) Key Guideline: Always double-check websites or contact customer service as formulas can change over time. Step 3: Identify Local Craft Breweries Offering Vegan Options Many craft breweries now offer vegan choices. Check their websites or social media pages for information about specific beers being produced without animal products. You might also find local vegan festivals where such breweries showcase their vegan-friendly drinks. Step 4: Use Online Resources To Find Vegan Beer Choices Several online resources provide extensive lists of vegan beers based on user submissions and independent investigations. Examples include Barnivore ([www.barnivore.com](http://www.barnivore.com)) and TheVeganBeerGuide (theveganbeerguide.com). Such platforms help ensure your chosen beer adheres to vegan standards. Key Tip: Remember that even if one beer from a particular brand is deemed vegan, others within the same line might not be. So always verify individual beers before consuming them. Step 5: Learn About Certified Vegan Labels Some companies seek certification from organizations like Vegetarian Society or Vegan Action. Products bearing these certifications guarantee they meet strict vegan standards throughout manufacturing processes. However, keep in mind that smaller breweries may not have this label but still produce vegan beer. Step 6: Attend Tastings And Events Focused On Vegan Beer Joining events centered around vegan alcoholic beverages allows direct interaction with producers who can answer questions regarding ingredients and practices. Plus, tastings give an opportunity to sample various types before purchasing larger quantities. Conclusion: By following these six steps, you'll become proficient at identifying and choosing vegan beer options. Being informed about potential hidden animal products ensures your consumption aligns with your personal beliefs and values while enjoying great-tasting beer. " Put me in the zoo a book of colors. Title: Create a Terrarium for Your Pacman Frog: A Comprehensive Step-by-step Guide Introduction: Pacman frogs (Ceratophrys spp.) are popular pets due to their unique appearance and easygoing nature. They require a specific environment to thrive, which can be created using a terrarium. This guide will walk you through creating an ideal habitat for your Pacman frog, ensuring its health and happiness. I. Choosing the Right Enclosure A. Selecting a suitable terrarium is crucial for your Pacman frog's wellbeing. Choose a tank with the following dimensions: 1. For adult Pacman frogs: 20 gallons (75L) or larger 2. For juveniles: 10 gallons (38L) B. Opt for a front-opening tank if possible, as this makes maintenance easier. C. Ensure that the enclosure has a secure lid to prevent escapes. II. Substrate Selection and Preparation A. The substrate provides a surface for the frog to move around and dig while also helping maintain humidity levels. Suitable options include: 1. Map Your Food Maps, in all shapes, sizes, and themes, are a common fourth grade social studies topic. Kids love the textures and shapes of physical maps, the intricate symbols of product maps, and the rainbow colors of climate charts. But it can be another matter all together when it comes to locating themselves within that context. Here's a fun family activity that builds this awareness, while also addressing the pressing environmental issue of our "global footprint." And if you've got a picky eater around, it just might get him to try some new foods, too! What You Need: - 10-20 foods that your child eats often over a week. Try to include a range of categories, from produce and meat to milk to cereals and snacks. - Wall size map of the world - Ball of thin colored yarn - Push pins or repositionable tape (available at stationery or art supply stores) - Small sticky notes What You Do: - Explain to your child that you are going to figure out how far your food travels to get to you. Start by putting up the world map, and marking your home town on it with a colored marker. - Invite your child to explore the kitchen with you, and pull out your 10-20 common food items. Keep them in their containers, or leave stickers on them, for this activity-you want to find out where they're from! - Now, one by one, "map" your food: stretch your yarn from your house to the country or state, or even city, where your food was grown or made. Label each string with a post-it. - This being fourth grade, your child is studying map legends and learning to figure distance ratios. So take out a plain piece of paper and start a list: for each food, use the map legend to figure how many miles it came, assuming it came "as the crow flies." (It may not have, but this will still give us a useful ballpark). If a product came from within 50 miles, it may be too hard to be exact; just give it an average number of, say 20. And if it came from your back yard, of course, you can savor writing a nice round "0"! - When you're done, you should have a dramatic map with lines of varying lengths. Leave it up for the week and talk it over. Are there any foods you might choose that are closer to home? Tally your first total of miles...and then, for the next couple of weeks, see if you can beat your score. What is it like to "eat local," and how can you do it more often? Title: How to Convert a Microsoft Word Document to a Portable Document Format (PDF) **Introduction:** This tutorial will guide you through the process of converting your Microsoft Word document into a Portable Document Format (PDF). This is particularly useful when you want to share or publish documents that maintain their original formatting regardless of the device used to open them. The steps provided are applicable for users working with Microsoft Office 2016, but similar procedures can be followed for other versions of MS Office as well. **Step 1: Open Your Word Document** Start by launching Microsoft Word and opening the document you wish to convert into a PDF file. You can do this by clicking on 'File' from the top left corner of the screen, then selecting 'Open'. Navigate to where your document is saved, highlight it, and click 'Open'. **Step 2: Prepare Your Document** Before conversion, ensure all elements within your document are arranged properly. Check spelling, grammar, page layouts, tables, images, etc., since these features may appear differently once converted into a PDF format due to varying software interpretations. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Review headers and footers carefully because they might shift position during the conversion process. - If your document contains hyperlinks, test them after conversion to confirm functionality. - Remember to remove any tracked changes or comments if necessary before converting. **Step 3: Save As PDF** After preparing your document, go back to the 'File' tab and choose 'Save As', which allows you to save your current work under another name or location while keeping the original intact. A dialog box should pop up asking you to specify a new filename and select a location to save it. **Step 4: Choose Destination Folder** Navigate to the folder where you would like to store the converted version of your document using the directory tree displayed on the left side of the dialog box. Once selected, enter a suitable name for your new PDF file in the 'File Name' field at the bottom of the dialog box. **Step 5: Select File Type** On the right side of the dialog box, locate the 'Save as type' dropdown menu. From here, select 'PDF (*.pdf)' as shown in the label next to the option. It ensures that your file gets saved in the correct format. ![Screenshot demonstrating saving as a PDF](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/b/bd/Convert-a-Word-Document-to-PDF-Step-7.jpg/aid98603-v4- Overall, the Earth is about 1.59% aluminium by mass. In the Earth's crust, aluminium is the most abundant metallic element by mass (8.23%). It is also the third most abundant of all elements in the Earth's crust. A lot of silicates in the Earth's crust contain aluminium. But, the Earth's mantle is only 2.38% aluminium by mass. Aluminium also occurs in seawater at a concentration of 2 g/kg. Although in pinyin, "Shaanxi" is spelled exactly the same as "Shanxi", the only difference in how the two words are pronounced is that the first syllable in "Shanxi" is said using the first tone in Mandarin, while the first syllable in "Shaanxi" is said with the third tone. Since tone markers are not usually used to show how to pronounce the two different names, the first word in "Shaanxi" is spelled using the Gwoyeu Romatzyh method instead, which indicates tones by adding extra letters to a syllable. Lily and Tom are friends. They like to play with toys and eat snacks. One day, they go to Lily's house after school. Lily's mom gives them tuna sandwiches and apple juice. They are happy and say thank you. They take their sandwiches and juice to Lily's room. They see many toys on the floor. They see dolls, cars, blocks, and books. They want to play with everything. They forget about their sandwiches and juice. They leave them on the table. They play for a long time. They make stories with the dolls and cars. They build towers with the blocks. They look at the pictures in the books. They have fun and laugh. Then, they hear a noise. They turn around and see Lily's cat. The cat is on the table. The cat is eating their sandwiches and juice. The cat likes tuna. The cat does not like apple juice. The cat spills the juice on the floor. Lily and Tom are surprised. They say, "Oh no! The cat ate our sandwiches and juice! We forgot to eat them! We were too busy playing!" Lily's mom hears them. She comes to Lily's room. She sees the cat, the sandwiches, the juice, and the mess. She is not angry. She is kind. She says, "Don't worry, kids. It's okay. The cat was hungry too. I can make you more sandwiches and juice. But first, let's clean up the mess together." Lily and Tom say, "Okay, mom. We are sorry. As a guide to the relative physical scale of the Milky Way, if the Solar System out to the orbit of Pluto were reduced to the size of a US quarter (about an inch or 25 mm in diameter), the Milky Way would have a diameter of 2,000 kilometers. Rationale: Children need to be able to recognize phonemes and see that letters in the alphabet represents these sounds. Before learning to read one must be able to read and blend phonemes. My goal is to help students locate s=/s/ in the beginning, middle, and end of words. By the end of the lesson, my students should be able to recognize /s/ in spoken words, written words, and use it in writing. Materials: note card (for tongue twister), picture page (hand drawn with square, circle, snake, sun, moon, and six), primary paper/pencil, S-card 1. Introduce- "Boys and girls, we will discover what sounds Sam the silly snake says when slithering through the sand. Can you tell me what sound a snake makes? Very good!" Make the sound a few times and have them feel the way their mouth moves (teeth together and tongue hissing and the back of their teeth). 2. Let's say a tongue twister together: "Sam said he was sorry he put salt in Sally's sandwich." Have the students repeat several times. "Now let's pretend we are snakes and stretch out each /s/ sound." (Repeat twister putting emphasis on S's). Review: "What sound does s make?" 3. Give them a picture page with various words some containing the letter s. Have each student draw and S on the ones containing the s phoneme (square, circle, snake, sun, moon, six). Now listen for the /s/ sound in words I call out. If you hear /s/ then hold up your S-card (sit, stand, reach, star, finish, start, run, swim, win, loose). Great job you used the /s/ phoneme perfectly. 4. Write the letter S on primary paper, Model starting at just below the roof making a little c the sit on the fence. Then go down the sidewalk and make a curve in the opposite direction. Now have the student practice a line of S's. Wonderful! 5. I will read them a paragraph and have the students hold up the S-card every time they hear a word with the letter s. " Six students went to school on Sunday morning. The class was empty. Silly kids forgot it was a day off, so they went swimming. The sun was out and Sally got a sunburn. So she went home." 6. As an assessment have each student think of three words containing the letter S and share them with the class. Repeat the words as a group after the individual reads them. [spoken from a distance; MOT seems to be tending to fussing infant] Title: Discovering Patterns in Pictures with a Special Computer Program! Hello young explorers! Today, we're going to learn about a fun and exciting way computers can find patterns in pictures all by themselves. We'll call our special computer program a "sparse autoencoder." First, let's imagine we have a big bunch of small 8x8 picture pieces (we call them "patches") taken from different photos. These patchies could be anything - toys, plants, or even funny faces! Our goal is to teach our sparse autoencoder to recognize important features within those patchies, like lines, corners, or shapes. Now, let's see how our sparse autoencoder does its magic: **Step 1: Making our Patchy Training Set** Just like when we're learning something new, our sparse autoencoder needs practice too! So, we give it a collection of patchies to study - this is called a "training set." Our program looks at each patchie, picks out interesting details, and remembers them for later. To create our training set, here's what we do: 1. Choose one of our ten cool images. It could be a picture of cats, dogs, cars, or flowers! 2. From that image, cut out tiny 8x8 squares at random spots. That's our patchy! 3. Repeat steps 1 and 2 until we have enough patchies for our sparse autoencoder to learn from. Next time, we'll tell you how our sparse autoencoder uses this training set to discover neat things about the world around us! Stay tuned for more pattern-finding adventures! Pascal Worked Example: Area of a Square Write a program to calculate the area of a square. Inputs: the length of a side of the square. Thought: Only need one value as this is a square. Thought: What type of values can be entered? This needs to be clarified with the client. For the moment we will assume that this can be any Real value. Calculation: The area of a square is equal to the square of the length of one of its sides. Thought: Calculating the square of a number sounds like something that is likely to exist in the library of functions associated with the language. We could calculate this value as area := length * length, however it will be easier if we use the built in functions. Searching the rtl.pdf that comes with the Free Pascal Compiler we can find the function Sqr (Sqr (1061) Calculate the square of a value). This is a simple problem and its algorithm can be kept in your mind, however it is good practice to write this down. In pseudo code: - Print program header informing user of the intent of the program. - Ask user to enter the length of a side of the square. - Read the value entered by the user and store in length. - Calculate area and store. - Print area to console. Thought: Do we need to calculate the area of just one square or does the user want to be able to calculate the area of a number of squares? This needs to be clarified with the user. For this exercise we will assume that the client just want to print the area of a single square, so the program can end after the steps above. Implement program skeleton: Add the variables identified as input. Add an additional variable to store the area. As the length is a real value, the area will also be a real value. Implement the steps of the algorithm. This can be done in the order that they are defined. Run the program and test it to ensure that it works correctly. Thought: are the prompts what the user intended? You should show the program to the client and verify these details with them. For a simple program this is not a big problem but it is something that you need to keep in mind. Important Points: Notice the layout of the program... Divide the logic areas in the code into separate areas and indent things inside of compound statements. The expression area:10:2 is used to format the output to 10 spaces, with two decimal places. Photo: &y (flickr) If you've ever had to dig a hole and then fill it back in, you may have noticed a mysterious thing: there was always a little dirt left over. This can often be seen when construction crews dig down to get at sewer pipes or electric cables. But how can there be more dirt going in than there was coming out? The answer is that the amount of soil itself remains the same; what has changed is the arrangement of the individual grains. To see how this happens, think of a sugar bowl. If every crystal of sugar is evenly spaced, the ratio of crystals to empty space will be 60 - 40. That's true no matter how big the bowl: 40% of the inside will always be empty. Now put a spoon in and smooth the surface of the sugar: all those evenly-spaced crystals will be packed down into an uneven arrangement, filling the empty spaces. The result is still a 60 - 40 ratio, only now we have a lower level of sugar, with the empty space moved up top. It's the same with soil. Rainwater and passing feet press the soil down so that the grains lie closely together with little empty space, called "pore space." When the shovel pulls the soil up and scatters it in a big pile, the soil grains fall into a pattern closer to the way they were before being packed down. Once the hole is refilled, all you have to do to make it level is get rid of that pore space - which can easily be done with the back of a shovel. He started to look for some food. He was hidin' in the toy box. L. L. Zamenhof created Esperanto. He grew up in Bia ystok, a town that was in the Russian Empire, but is now in Poland. People in Bia ystok spoke many languages. Zamenhof saw conflicts between individual ethnic groups living there (Russians, Poles, Germans and Jews). He thought that lack of a common language caused these conflicts, so he began creating a language people could share and use internationally. He thought this language should be different from national languages. He wanted it to be culturally neutral and easy-to-learn. He thought people should learn it along with national languages and use Esperanto for communication between people with different native languages. Sara and Ben were playing in the park. They saw many flowers and birds and bugs. They heard a stream and followed it. They found a big rock with a hole in it. "Look, Sara! A cave!" Ben said. "Let's go inside!" Sara was scared. She saw a lot of mist coming out of the hole. She smelled something terrible. She heard a growl. "Ben, I don't want to go in there!" Sara said. "It's too dark and scary! Maybe there is a monster!" He wished he had listened to Sara. Sara heard Ben scream. She was very worried. She wanted to help him. She ran into the hole. She saw the monster. It was a big bear. It was angry and hungry. It had Ben in its mouth. Sara was brave. She picked up a stick and hit the bear. She yelled at it. She made it drop Ben. She grabbed Ben's hand and ran out of the cave.
