Title: Find the Sun Using the Crescent Moon Introduction: Finding the sun using the crescent moon is an ancient technique that has been used by sailors, explorers, and nature enthusiasts for centuries. This method relies on observing the position of the crescent moon relative to the horizon and can help you locate the sun's approximate location even when it is hidden from view. By understanding this process, you will enhance your ability to navigate using natural clues and gain a deeper appreciation for celestial navigation. Step 1: Observe the Crescent Moon Look for a thin crescent moon low in the western sky just after sunset or before sunrise. The thinner the crescent, the more accurate your results will be. A thicker crescent may still provide useful information but could result in less precise measurements. Key Tip: Wait until at least two days have passed since the new moon phase for the crescent moon to become visible. Step 2: Identify the Direction of the Horns Determine which direction the 'horns' of the crescent are pointing. These horns refer to the ends of the curved line formed by the moon's illuminated edge. They always point towards the east (before sunrise) or west (just after sunset). Explanation: As the moon orbits around Earth, its phases change due to differing angles between the sun, earth, and moon. When only partially lit, the crescent shape forms with the dark side facing either the sun or away from it, depending on whether it appears during dawn or dusk. Step 3: Locate the Sun's Position Draw an imaginary line connecting the horns of the crescent moon. Extend this line beyond the moon until you reach the horizon. The sun should be roughly in the opposite direction along the horizon from where the extended line points. Guideline: If the moon's horns point upwards, imagine drawing a horizontal line across the top of the curve instead. Then proceed as described above. Explanation: Since the moon reflects sunlight, the angle between the sun, earth, and moon dictates where we see the reflected light. Therefore, if we know the orientation of the moon relative to the sun, we can estimate the sun's position based on our observation. Step 4: Confirm Your Observation Wait about 30 minutes after observing the crescent moon's alignment. During this time, check whether there are any noticeable changes in shadow lengths or directions. Additionally, observe if any nearby objects cast longer shadows compared to their immediate vicinity. Both these signs indicate that the sun has risen or set closer to the indicated direction found in Step 3. Key Tips: - Be patient - wait for clear skies without cloud cover to ensure accuracy. - Practice often to improve your skills and confidence. - Remember that atmospheric conditions might affect visibility and precision. Conclusion: By mastering the art of finding the sun using the crescent moon, you join countless others who have harnessed the power of celestial navigation throughout history. While modern technology provides numerous alternatives for determining direction, relying on natural indicators fosters a deep connection with the environment and promotes self-reliant survival skills. Happy navigating! There are many events in "Ramadan", Islam's holiest month. During "Ramadan", Muslims must fast during the day. Mosques organise "iftar" dinners after sunset. These are done after the fourth required prayer of the day. Part of the food is given by members of the community, which creates nightly potluck dinners. The community contribution to these dinners is required. For this reason, mosques with smaller communities may not be able to hold the "iftar" dinners daily. He found a space helmet in the drain board in the kitchen and a pair of spare boots on the mat by the front door. Title: Discovering Shapes and Patterns in the World Around Us! Have you ever played with building blocks or tried to create shapes using sticks and clay? If so, you've already started exploring a fascinating area of mathematics called geometry! Today, we will dive into a fun puzzle involving cubes and smaller shapes called simplices. Let's get started! First, let's understand what these words mean: 1. Cube: You probably know this one already! A cube looks like a square box. In math, it has six identical squares as its faces, and all edges are equal in length. Imagine a Rubik's cube without colors - just a 3D grid! 2. Simplex: Don't worry; you don't need to memorize this term! Just think of a simplex as a simple shape with straight lines. A point is a 0-simplex, a line segment is a 1-simplex, a triangle is a 2-simplex, and a tetrahedron (four triangles forming a pyramid) is a 3-simplex. Now, here comes the exciting part: Can we break down a big cube into tiny simplex shapes while ensuring no two simplexes overlap? Yes, we can! And your job is to do it using the fewest possible simplexes. For example, imagine a small 1-cube (a square). We can divide it into two non-overlapping triangles (or 2-simplexes), as shown below: ``` +---+---+ |\ | |\ | | \| \| | +---+---+ ``` Now try doing the same thing with a 2-cube (a cube). As you saw in the snippet earlier, it takes five 3-simplexes (tetrahedrons) to decompose a cube optimally. See if you can find a way to cut it up like that! What happens when we go to higher dimensions, like a 3-cube or even larger cubes? That's where things start getting tricky! The goal is still to chop up the cube into as few simplices as possible, but finding those divisions can become quite challenging. So grab some paper, scissors, and tape, and begin experimenting! Cut out different sized cubes and discover ways to break them apart into various simplex shapes. Encourage friends and family members to join in and exchange ideas. Remember, there's no single correct solution; it's all about exploration and discovery. Have fun learning together! The Signoria (Town Council) of the City of Florence wanted two large frescos painted on the walls of the most important room of the Signoria Palace. Michelangelo was to paint "The Battle of Cascina" and Leonardo was to paint "The Battle of Anghiari". Leonardo began the project by studying and drawing the faces of angry men and fighting horses. These drawings can still be seen in his notebooks. But unfortunately, this was to be another failure for Leonardo. When he painted the picture on the wall, instead of using fresco, he mixed the paints with oil. The paint would not dry. Leonardo lit some fires to dry it, and the painting melted. Peter Paul Rubens drew a copy of the middle part. After a time, the town council covered it up and got somebody else to paint the wall. Michelangelo did not finish his painting either, because the Pope called him to Rome. Discussing religion with anyone can be a sensitive topic, but it is especially important to approach discussions about Mormonism with respect and understanding. Here's a guide to help you have productive and meaningful conversations about Mormonism: **Step 1: Educate Yourself About Mormon Beliefs** Before discussing religion with a Mormon, take the time to learn about their beliefs. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS), commonly known as Mormonism, has unique doctrines that differ from traditional Christianity. Familiarize yourself with basic Mormon concepts such as the Book of Mormon, Joseph Smith, prophets, temples, and baptisms for the dead. This will allow you to ask informed questions and engage in more substantive dialogue. Key Tip: Avoid making assumptions or stereotypes based on limited knowledge or media portrayals. **Step 2: Find Common Ground** Begin by acknowledging shared values or commonalities between your belief systems. For example, both Christians and Mormons believe in God, Jesus Christ, and the importance of faith and family. Building upon this foundation can create an atmosphere of mutual respect and openness. Guideline: Show genuine interest in learning about their perspective without judgment or criticism. **Step 3: Ask Open-Ended Questions** Instead of asking yes/no questions, frame your queries in ways that encourage thoughtful responses. Examples include: * Can you explain how your faith impacts your daily life? * How does the concept of continuing revelation influence modern Mormon practices? * What role does the Book of Mormon play in your spiritual journey? By doing so, you demonstrate a willingness to understand their viewpoint while also fostering deeper conversation. Key Tip: Be patient and let them share their thoughts fully before responding. **Step 4: Share Your Perspective Respectfully** When sharing your own beliefs, remember to be considerate and avoid confrontational language. Use "I" statements rather than "you" statements to express your opinions without sounding accusatory. For instance, say "I believe..." instead of "You believe wrongly because..." Guideline: Recognize that religious beliefs are deeply personal and should be treated with reverence. **Step 5: Encourage Dialogue** Aim for a two-way conversation where both parties listen and respond to one another. Paraphrase what they said to ensure accurate understanding and show that you value their input. Additionally, invite them to clarify any points you found confusing or unclear. Key Tip: Active listening is crucial in building trust and fostering continued discussion. **Step 6: Address Differences Politely** It's likely that you'll encounter differences in doctrine or interpretation. When addressing these distinctions, focus on the issues themselves rather than attacking the person. Remember, disagreement doesn't necessarily mean disrespect. Guideline: Strive for understanding over persuasion; aim to broaden perspectives instead of changing minds. **Step 7: Agree to Disagree If Necessary** If after sincere effort, you still cannot reach consensus, agree to disagree amicably. Acknowledge the areas where you diverge and reaffirm your appreciation for their willingness to discuss openly. Key Tip: Maintaining positive relationships often surpasses the need for complete agreement. In conclusion, engaging in religious discourse with a Mormon requires patience, empathy, and knowledge. By following these steps, you can facilitate constructive dialogues that promote interfaith understanding and respect. In another counting example, Sally and Bill have 2 children. Sally and Bill get 3 more children. Sally and Bill have added three children to their two children, and now have five children. This one is April and that one is Sneaker. These he made his family, and constituted himself their father and chief. He abode with them, and cared for them as a parent. He had schools for the children; the more advanced he put to trades and employments; he set up a hospital for the sick; and for all he had the priestly ministrations of his own Christ-like heart. As Ferdinand stood beside the king, his eye glanced at a large and splendid mirror. His matted hair, his bloodshot eyes, his haggard looks, and torn and mean dress, derogated from the nobility of his appearance; and still less did he appear like the magnificent Count Eboli, when, to his utter confusion and astonishment, his counterfeit stood beside him. He was perfect in all the outward signs that denoted high birth; and so like him whom he represented, that it would have been impossible to discern one from the other apart. The same chestnut hair clustered on his brow; the sweet and animated hazel eyes were the same; the one voice was the echo of the other. Because the bad pig tries to blow a house down. New York was the capital of the United States under the Articles of Confederation from 1785 to 1788. When the US Constitution was made, it stayed as the capital from 1789 until 1790. In 1789, the first President of the United States, George Washington, was inaugurated; the first United States Congress and the Supreme Court of the United States each met for the first time, and the United States Bill of Rights was written, all at Federal Hall on Wall Street. By 1790, New York grew bigger than Philadelphia, so it become the biggest city in the United States. By the end of 1790, because of the Residence Act, Philadelphia became the new capital. 's that look like your good night moon book? to the Finish Growing Independence and Fluency By: Karla Hollis To become fluent readers, children need to learn how to read faster, smoother, and more expressively. Fluency refers to a student's ability to read words accurately and automatically. In this lesson, students will learn how to read quickly, smoothly, and expressively in order to gain fluency. It has been proven that the more students read the more their reading skills will improve. Students will gain fluency through repeated readings, timed readings, and one-minute reads. Stopwatch for each pair of students Pencil for each student Copy of the book Go, Dog. Go! for each student Race Track progress chart for each student Die cut car for each student List of comprehension questions for each student 1. What size are the dogs in the book? 2. What color are the dogs in the book? 3. Where are the dogs going? 4. What had did the dog like at the end of the book? Crayons for each student Construction paper for each student 1."Today we are going to learn how to be a fluent reader by reading words correctly as fast as we can." Explain to the students what it means to be a fluent reader and the steps they must take to become a fluent reader. "The word fluency means having the ability to read words correctly, without hesitation, and quickly. Once you become a fluent reader, you will enjoy reading more because you will understand what you are reading." 2. Model for the students how to read with fluency. "First, I am going to read you a sentence without fluency." Write on the board the following sentence: The dog ran up a hill. "The d-o-g r-a-n u-p the h-i-ll. Now I am going to read the sentence as a fluent reader. The dog ran up the hill. Did you hear the difference between reading with fluency and reading without fluency? Listen as I read the sentence once again. The dog ran up the hill. This time I read the sentence faster because it was not the first time I had read these words. The first two times I read the sentence gave me practice and helped me read the sentence fluently the third time." 3. "The story you will be reading to practice becoming a fluent reader is Go, Dog. Go! As you read you do not want to skip any words or read them incorrectly but read every word correctly while reading at a fast pace." Model reading Go, Do. Go! aloud as a fluent reader for the students. 4. Remind students to cross check if they do not automatically recognize a word during their reading. "Do not forget that cross checking is a tool that fluent readers use to make sense of the sentences that they read and to read more successfully. If you do not automatically recognize a word cover-up part of the word to make it easier to sound out. Once you have determined the pronunciation of the word, go back and reread the sentence to see if the word makes sense in the sentence. If the word does not make sense in the sentence, you can change your guess to a word that fits the sentence. If you and your partner cannot figure out how to pronounce a word correctly, come ask me and I will help you figure it out." 5. "Now that you have heard me read the book as a fluent reader, you are going to practice reading fluently with a partner." Divide the students up into groups of two and give each student a copy of the book. "Once you are seated next to your partner, pick out your six favorite pages in the book that you will read to each other." 6. "To keep record of our progress in becoming a fluent reader, each of you will have your own "race track" progress chart." Give each student a "traffic light" progress chart and a die cut car. The "race track" progress chart is simply a piece of construction paper with a race track on it. Also, give each pair of students a stopwatch. Explain to the students how to use the progress chart. "The goal of this activity is to see how fast you can read the pages you have chosen in order to move your car from the red light to the green light on the chart. You will each take turns reading the six pages to your partner. While you read, your partner will use the stopwatch to keep the time. After you have read the six pages you will stop and write down your time on a piece of paper and place the car on the red light. Then you will read the same pages again and if your time has improved, you will move the car to the yellow light and so on. The car only moves if you increase your speed. You will continue reading the six pages until your car reaches the end of the track and you can "finish!" The students should take turns reading to one another. 7. Observe the students participation in the repeated readings by walking around the classroom and listening to their fluency develop with each repeated reading. 8. After each student has read their favorite pages enough times to reach the finish line on the "race track" progress chart, the students will respond to questions to check for understanding and comprehension. 1. What size dogs are in the book? 2. What color are the dogs in the book? 3. Where are the dogs going? 4. What hat did the dog like at the end of the book? 9. Pass out a piece of construction paper, box of crayons, and a pencil to each student. "Now everyone is going to draw and color a picture of their favorite dog in the book. Underneath your picture write a sentence explaining what your dog is doing in the picture. We will display each of your pictures and sentences on our reading bulletin board!" 10. "As you draw and color your picture, I am going to call you up to my desk one at a time so that you can prove to me how smoothly and quickly you can read your favorite pages in the book Go, Dog. Go!" 11. In order to assess each student's reading fluency, let them individually complete a one-minute read by rereading a few pages from Go, Dog. Go! aloud to the teacher. A one-minute read is a fluency checking activity in which a student reads aloud to the teacher for one minute which is timed by a stopwatch. The teacher has a progress chart for each student. It is the students' goal to move from the beginning point on the progress chart up to the ending point on the progress chart. If the student reads more fluently, the object will move up the progress chart. While the student is reading the book, note reading miscues. Also collect each student's "race track" progress chart to assess how quickly the student read six pages with his or her partner. 12. After each student has completed a one-minute read with the teacher, review the meaning of a fluent reader with the class and give the students their homework assignment. "Today we have learned how to become a fluent reader. At home tonight I want you to reread Go, Dog. Go! to a parent or older sibling so that they can enjoy the book while you show them how to read fluently." Holly Kubik. Extending displays can be incredibly useful when working with multiple windows or applications at once, increasing productivity by providing more screen real estate. This guide will walk you through extending your display from a Lenovo Ideapad G505s laptop to a Compaq Presario CQ56 101sa laptop. **Prerequisites:** - Both laptops must support external display connections via HDMI or VGA ports (check your user manuals). - A compatible cable to connect both laptops: either an HDMI-to-HDMI cable if both laptops have HDMI ports, or a VGA-to-VGA cable plus an audio cable if only one has an HDMI port. **Step 1: Check Display Settings on Lenovo Ideapad G505s** Ensure that the Lenovo Ideapad G505s is set up to extend its display rather than mirroring it. To do this: 1. Press `Win + P` keys together. 2. Select 'Extend' option from the list presented. If 'Second Screen Only' is selected instead, change it back to 'Extend'. 3. Click 'Apply', then 'Keep Changes'. This ensures any extended desktop activity appears on both screens while maintaining separate workspaces. **Step 2: Connecting Laptops With Appropriate Cable** Connect your Lenovo Ideapad G505s and Compaq Presario CQ56 101sa using the appropriate cable based on their available ports. Make sure all devices are powered off before connecting them. Once connected, power on both machines. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - For best results, use high-quality cables designed for video transmission to avoid signal loss or degradation. - Avoid stretching or bending the cable excessively as it could damage the connection or affect picture quality. **Step 3: Detect Second Monitor on Compaq Presario CQ56 101sa** Now we need to configure the Compaq Presario CQ56 101sa to recognize the secondary monitor (Lenovo Ideapad G505s): 1. Right-click anywhere on the desktop and select 'Display settings' from the context menu. _Or_ 1. Open Control Panel > Hardware and Sound > Display > Adjust resolution. 2. Under 'Multiple displays,' click 'Detect.' The system should now identify the second monitor. 3. Choose 'Extend these displays' under 'Multiple displays' dropdown menu. 4. Set the orientation and resolution according to preference and comfort level. 5. Confirm changes made by clicking 'Apply' followed by 'OK'. By following these steps correctly, you should now successfully extend your primary display (Compaq Presario CQ56 101sa) onto your secondary display (Lenovo Ideapad G505s), effectively doubling your workspace! **Troubleshooting Common Issues:** - **No Signal / Black Screen**: Try restarting both computers after ensuring proper physical connection. Update graphics drivers if necessary. - **Distorted Image**: Replace low-quality cables; ensure there's no excessive bend or stretch along the length of the cable. - **Color Differences Between Screens**: Calibrate color settings individually per monitor until satisfactory balance achieved. Although much of Cambodia is heavily forested, the central lowland region is covered with rice paddies, fields of dry crops such as corn (maize) and tobacco, tracts of tall grass and reeds, and thinly wooded areas. Savanna grassland occur in the plains, with the grasses reaching a height of 5 feet (1.5 metres). In the eastern highlands the high plateaus are covered with grasses and deciduous forests. Broad-leaved evergreen forests grow in the mountainous areas to the north, with trees 100 feet (30 metres) high emerging from thick undergrowths of vines, rattans, palms, bamboos, and assorted woody and herbaceous ground plants. Title: How to Change Word Document to Text Format Introduction: Converting a Word document to text format is a useful skill that can help you save time and ensure compatibility across different platforms or devices. This tutorial will guide you through the process of changing a Word document to plain text using Microsoft Word's built-in features. By following these steps, you'll be able to preserve your content while removing any formatting elements, such as font styles, colors, or layout options. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Always remember to save a backup copy of your original Word document before starting this conversion process. * Be aware that converting from Word to text may result in some loss of information like tables, images, charts, and other rich media. * Keep an eye out for special characters (e.g., curly quotes) that might require manual adjustment after conversion. Step 1: Open Your Word Document Start by launching Microsoft Word and opening the file you want to convert to text format. Make sure all necessary edits have been completed prior to initiating the conversion process. Step 2: Access the Save As Dialogue Box Once your document is open, click on the 'File' tab located at the top left corner of the screen. From there, select the 'Save As' option to proceed with exporting your document into a new format. Step 3: Choose Plain Text File Type In the Save As dialogue box, look for the 'Save as type:' dropdown menu. Click on it and choose 'Plain Text (*.txt)' as your preferred file format. This selection ensures that only unformatted text will be saved without any additional styling attributes. Step 4: Name Your New File Enter a descriptive name for your converted text file under the 'File name:' field. It's essential to provide a distinct title so you can quickly identify the new file among other documents within your folder or directory. Step 5: Select Encoding Option Before finalizing the conversion, take note of the encoding settings available below the 'Save as type:' dropdown menu. Depending on your specific needs, you may need to modify the default setting. For instance, if your document contains non-English characters or symbols, consider selecting UTF-8 encoding to maintain their integrity during the conversion process. Step 6: Confirm Conversion Settings Review all chosen settings one last time before clicking the 'Save' button. Double-check that the correct location has been selected for storing the new file and make any required changes accordingly. Step 7: Manage Special Characters (Optional) After completing the conversion, review your text file carefully for any inconsistencies caused by character substitution (e.g., straight quotation marks instead of curved ones). Address those issues manually if needed. Step 8: Verify Successful Conversion Open your newly created text file using a simple text editor like Notepad or another basic word processor to confirm successful conversion. Check whether the text appears accurately and no crucial data points are missing due to formatting limitations inherent to plain text files. Conclusion: By following this comprehensive tutorial, users should now feel confident when faced with the task of changing a Word document to a plain text format. Remember always to back up important files, pay attention to encoding options, manage special characters post-conversion, and verify the accuracy of the output. With practice, mastery over this technique will become second nature, making cross-platform collaboration more seamless than ever before. He stayed inside for more than two weeks. Title: How to Create Cute Curl Short Hair at Home Creating cute curls in short hair can be challenging but with the right tools and techniques, you can achieve salon-worthy results at home. This tutorial will guide you through the process of creating beautiful curls in your short hair using a heated styling tool such as a curling iron or wand. **Things You'll Need:** * A heat protectant spray * A comb * Clips or hair ties (for sectioning) * Curling iron or wand with a barrel size between 0.5 inches to 1 inch * Hairspray (optional) **Step 1: Preparation** Before starting, make sure that your hair is clean, dry, and tangle-free. Apply a generous amount of heat protectant spray throughout your hair from roots to ends. Heat protectants help prevent damage caused by high temperatures, keeping your hair healthy and shiny. **Step 2: Section Your Hair** Divide your hair into sections to ensure even distribution of curls and avoid missing any strands. Use clips or hair ties to separate the top half of your hair, leaving the bottom layer exposed. **Key Tip**: The smaller the sections, the tighter the curls; larger sections result in looser waves. Adjust based on your preferred curl pattern. **Step 3: Start Curling** Take one small section of hair from the bottom layer. Hold the curling iron vertically (the handle pointing downwards), wrap the ends around the barrel, then slowly twist the rest of the section up towards the scalp. Ensure that all parts of the section touch the hot surface while wrapping. **Guideline**: Keep the tension consistent while wrapping the hair around the barrel. Too tight might give an unnatural look, too loose may not hold the curl well. **Step 4: Release the Curl** After holding the curl on the iron for about 8-10 seconds depending on your hair type and thickness, gently release the curl by unwinding it from the barrel. Let the curl cool down completely before touching it again to allow it set properly. **Step 5: Repeat the Process** Continue steps three and four until all sections are curled. Once finished with the first layer, move onto the next layers, always remembering to let each curl cool completely before moving on. **Step 6: Final Touches** Once every strand has been styled, run your fingers lightly through your curls to break them apart slightly if needed. For added volume, flip your head over and shake your hair out. Optionally, finish off with hairspray for longer lasting curls. **Maintenance Tips**: \* Avoid brushing your curls after styling as this can cause frizziness and straighten out the curls. Instead, use your fingers to detangle and style. \* Sleep with your hair protected either by braiding it loosely or putting it up in a pineapple bun to maintain the shape and definition of the curls overnight. Remember practice makes perfect! Don't worry if your first few attempts don't turn out exactly how you want them to. With patience and persistence, you'll soon master these techniques and enjoy fabulous curly hairstyles anytime! Get out o' there get out o' there. Then perhaps you'd haft ago to the hospital. [CHI is telling about the book she is reading MOT is sitting off to the side and not interacting] An exponent is a number that tells how many times the base number is used as a factor. For example, 34 indicates that the base number 3 is used as a factor 4 times. To determine the value of 34, multiply 3*3*3*3 which would give the result 81. Exponents are written as a superscript number (e.g. 34) or preceded by the caret (^) symbol (e.g. 3^4). Some facts about exponents: - Zero raised to any power is zero (e.g. 05 = 0) - One raised to any power is one (e.g. 15 = 1) - Any number raised to the zero power is one (e.g. 70 = 1) - Any number raised to the first power is that number (e.g. 71 = 7) oh, did you have those at your birthday? Title: How to Help a Sick, Pregnant, or Elderly Person Safely Exit a Vehicle Helping a sick, pregnant, or elderly person out of a car can be a delicate task that requires patience, care, and attention to detail. By following these steps, you can ensure a safe and smooth exit from the vehicle while providing necessary support and assistance. **Step 1: Prepare the Environment** Before assisting the individual out of the car, make sure the area around the vehicle is clear of any obstacles or hazards such as uneven surfaces, potholes, or slippery patches. This will help prevent accidents and reduce the risk of injury during the process. *Key tip*: If possible, park the car in a well-lit area with minimal traffic to create a safer environment for both parties involved. **Step 2: Communicate Clearly** Ask the person if they need assistance getting out of the car and discuss your plan before taking action. Clear communication ensures mutual understanding and cooperation throughout the process, reducing anxiety and confusion. *Guideline*: Always respect their wishes; never force help upon someone who prefers to manage independently unless there's an immediate danger present. **Step 3: Position the Assistive Device (If Available)** If using a transfer board or other assistive device, place it securely between the car seat and the destination surface (e.g., wheelchair, walker). Ensure it spans the gap evenly and provides stable support for the transition. *Key tip*: Familiarize yourself with the proper usage of the specific device according to manufacturer instructions or professional guidance. **Step 4: Support Their Stability** Stand at the side of the door, facing the person. Place one hand under their arm pit and another on their back for additional support. Maintain good posture and keep your core engaged to provide steady assistance without straining yourself. *Guideline*: Adjust your position based on the person's needs - for example, supporting them from the front if they have limited mobility in their arms. **Step 5: Gradual Movement** Guide the person slowly and gently towards the edge of the seat. Encourage them to swing their legs out first, followed by their body. Once their feet are firmly planted on the ground, ask them to lean forward slightly so you can slide your hands beneath their armpits for further support. *Key tip*: Move at a pace comfortable for the individual, allowing ample time for adjustments and transitions. **Step 6: Lift Together** On the count of three, simultaneously lift the person upwards while guiding them towards the assistive device or final destination surface. Keep your knees bent and spine straight to protect your back from strain. *Guideline*: Distribute weight evenly between both parties involved in the lifting process, avoiding jerky movements or sudden shifts that could cause imbalance. **Step 7: Transfer onto Destination Surface** Once the person is upright and stabilized, help them shift their weight onto the assistive device or final destination surface. Ensure they are seated comfortably and safely before releasing your hold. *Key tip*: Double-check that all belts, brakes, or locks are secured properly once the transition is complete. By following these steps, you can help a sick, pregnant, or elderly person exit a car safely and confidently. Remember to always prioritize open communication, patient guidance, and careful handling throughout the entire process. Are you going to cook it and then feed it to the babies? Title: How to Locate Your Garage Door Opener Model Number Introduction: Knowing your garage door opener model number is essential when you need to order replacement parts or seek technical support from the manufacturer. This guide will help you find this information quickly and easily, regardless of the brand or type of opener you have. Key Tips and Guidelines: 1. Gather necessary tools: Before starting, ensure that you have a stepladder, flashlight (if working in a dimly lit area), and a pen or paper to write down the model number once found. 2. Safety first: Always prioritize safety when dealing with any mechanical components. Turn off power supply to the garage door opener before proceeding. 3. Consult owner's manual: If available, refer to your owner's manual as it may provide specific guidance on locating the model number. Step-by-step Instructions: Step 1: Access the Back Cover of the Motor Housing Unit The motor housing unit is typically mounted on the ceiling of your garage. To access its back cover, position your ladder securely under the unit and climb up carefully. Use your flashlight if needed to illuminate the area. Once stable, locate the screws holding the cover in place - usually four evenly spaced around the perimeter. Unscrew them using a suitable screwdriver. Please note that some models might require a hex key instead. Carefully remove the cover, setting it aside without disconnecting any wires attached. Why this step matters: By exposing the interior of the motor housing unit, you can see various labels containing important information such as the model number. Step 2: Identify Label(s) Inside the Motor Housing Unit After removing the cover, look inside the motor housing unit for one or more labels affixed by the manufacturer. These could be stickers or printed directly onto the metal surface. They often contain details about the product including serial number, manufacturing date, voltage requirements, etc. What to expect: The label should clearly display the model number using alphanumeric characters. It might also include additional codes like 'DC' indicating whether the device uses direct current electricity. Step 3: Record the Model Number Once located, jot down the model number accurately onto your piece of paper or take a clear photo with your smartphone for future reference. Make sure there are no errors while recording since incorrect information could lead to compatibility issues when purchasing replacements. Proper documentation ensures that you won't forget the exact model number later on, which saves time and effort during shopping or seeking assistance. Step 4: Reassemble the Motor Housing Unit Now that you've recorded the required info, replace the cover securely onto the motor housing unit. Screw it back into place making sure they're properly tightened but not overly so as to damage the threads. Restore power supply only after everything is reconnected and secured. Reassembly prevents potential accidents due to exposed electrical components and returns your setup to normal operation condition. Conclusion: Finding your garage door opener model number doesn't have to be daunting anymore. With these straightforward steps, you now possess the knowledge to confidently identify this critical detail whenever needed. Remember always to put safety first, consult manuals where possible, document meticulously, and maintain vigilance throughout the process. Happy tinkering! An oligarchy is a government ruled by a small group of powerful people. These people may spread power equally or not equally. More so a different version of a monarchy, where everyone makes decisions together instead of one person making them all or telling people what to do, such as in a Dictatorship. An oligarchy is different from a true democracy because very few people are given the chance to change things. An oligarchy does not have to be hereditary or passed down from father to son. An oligarchy does not have one clear ruler, but several powerful people. Some past examples of oligarchy are the former Union of Soviet Socialist Republics and Apartheid South Africa. A fictional example is the dystopian society of "Oceania" in the book Nineteen Eighty-Four. Some critics of representative democracy think of the United States as an oligarchy. This view is shared by anarchists. An oligarchy may have a leader in the ruling group. Title: How to Draw Animations Introduction: Drawing animations is an exciting way to bring your art to life! By breaking down movements into individual frames, you can create the illusion of motion. This tutorial will guide you through the process, from sketching basic shapes to creating complex scenes. We'll cover various techniques and best practices so that you can develop your own animation style. Let's get started! Step 1: Planning Your Animation Before drawing, plan out what action or scene you want to animate. Consider factors such as characters, backgrounds, objects, and camera angles. Sketch rough thumbnails to visualize these elements and determine their interactions within the frame. Key tip: Keep movement simple at first; complicated actions may lead to confusion during the drawing phase. Step 2: Setting Up Frames Divide a sheet of paper or digitally create columns representing each frame. Decide on the number of frames needed based on the complexity of the motion and its speed. Generally, faster motions require more frames while slower ones need fewer. Guideline: A good starting point for beginners is 12-24 frames per second (fps) when working with hand-drawn animations. Step 3: Key Poses Identify crucial poses throughout the sequence - known as 'keyframes.' These depict significant changes in position, expression, or posture. Begin by illustrating these keys accurately, ensuring proper proportions and perspective. Key Tip: Focus on capturing the essence of the character and their emotions rather than fine details. You can refine them later. Step 4: Breakdown Poses In between keyframes, add breakdown poses to gradually transition from one pose to another. While still simplified, they should show intermediate stages of the motion. Think of these as stepping stones guiding viewers through the story. Guideline: Space out breakpoints evenly across the timeline for smooth transitions. Step 5: Inbetweens Now fill in the gaps with additional drawings called 'inbetweeners.' They help complete the motion cycle by making subtle adjustments to previous poses. As you progress, reduce the differences between consecutive frames until the final image appears almost identical to the initial one. Key Tip: Pay close attention to timing - too many in-betweens slow down the pace, whereas too few make the action feel abrupt. Step 6: Refining Details Once satisfied with the overall flow, revisit earlier sketches to enhance details like facial expressions, clothing wrinkles, or environmental textures. Be consistent with shading and line quality throughout the series to maintain continuity. Guideline: Use reference materials if necessary but avoid copying exactly; strive for originality and personal touch. Step 7: Testing & Adjustment Scan or photograph all completed frames and compile them using video editing software. Playback the sequence to assess fluidity and pacing. If any part seems off, return to the respective frame(s), modify accordingly, then repeat testing until satisfactory results are achieved. Key Tip: Seek feedback from others for objective opinions about improvements needed. Conclusion: Congratulations! You now have the foundation required to draw compelling animations. Remember, practice makes perfect - keep experimenting with different ideas and styles to hone your skills further. Happy drawing! They look so much the same only they got big nose here [short laugh]. Find a List of Branded Keywords Search in Google Analytics Follow this detailed guide to find a list of branded keywords search within your Google Analytics account. Understanding these terms can help you better understand how users are finding your website and what content is most relevant to them. Step 1: Sign in to Your Google Analytics Account To begin, navigate to the Google Analytics homepage (analytics.google.com) and enter your login credentials. If you have multiple accounts or properties associated with your login, select the appropriate one from the drop-down menus at the top of the page. Step 2: Navigate to the Acquisition Section Once logged in, look for the "Acquisition" option in the left-hand menu. This will take you to an overview that displays information about where your traffic is coming from. Key Tip: Make sure you're viewing data for the correct date range by checking the upper right corner of the screen. Adjust as needed using the drop-down calendar if necessary. Step 3: Select "Organic Search" under All Traffic Underneath the Acquisition section, click on "All Traffic." A submenu should appear; here, choose "Channels," then click on "Organic Search." This will display all organic search traffic during the selected time period. Step 4: Click on "Secondary Dimension" Button On the Organic Search Overview page, locate the button labeled "Secondary Dimension." It may be found just above the graph showing your organic search traffic trends. Key Guideline: Keep in mind that we want to filter our results specifically for branded keyword searches, so selecting a secondary dimension will allow us to add another layer of categorization to our analysis. Step 5: Type "Keyword" into the Search Bar and Select "Keyword (not provided)" Option After clicking the "Secondary Dimension" button, a text box will appear asking you to input your preferred secondary dimension. Type "keyword" into the search bar, and then scroll down until you see "Keyword (not provided)." Check the checkbox next to this option. Step 6: Analyze Data for Relevant Terms Now that you have filtered your organic search data according to branded keywords, review the table below the graph for any recognizable terms related to your brand or company name. These could include product names, service descriptions, or other identifiers unique to your business. Key Takeaway: Remember that some queries might still fall under "(not provided)," especially since Google encrypts many search terms. However, analyzing the available data can still provide valuable insights into user behavior and preferences regarding your brand. By following these steps, you should now have successfully identified a list of branded keywords searched in relation to your site through Google Analytics! Good now finish the rest of it. Lesson Plan Three: Sense of Hearing and The Ear Lesson Topic/Subject: The ear/Science and Grade Level: 1 /2 Teacher: Michelle Curtiss I decided to incorporate three different activities in one lesson because of the importance of each activity. As I have mentioned before, the five senses are only a small section of the body unit; therefore, only one or two days are provided for lessons and activities for each sense. This particular lesson will be taught over a two-day period because of the depth of each - Students will be able to use their sense of hearing to observe the sounds at school. Applications. - Students will be able to label parts of the ear. - Students will be able to write their own version of the "Ears Hear" poem by following the example provided. - The book The Listening Walk by Paul - Eye Gate Media filmstrip on the ear - Projector and tape player - 24 copies of the ear worksheet - 24 copies of the poem "Ears Hear" - Blank paper Part I: This can be taught with a whole group or in small groups with an assistant. - Read The Listening Walk by Paul - Tell students that we will go on our own listening walk. Reinforce the idea of a listening walk: to be as quiet as possible to hear all the sounds around us at school. Have students remember what sounds they hear, so that when we return to the classroom we can make a list. - Take students on a five to ten minute walk around the school. Remind students to stay really quiet. - Return to class and start a list of sounds that we heard on our walk. - Discuss why or how we hear all of these - Tell students that we will be watching a filmstrip on the ear. Tell them to find three things that they did not know to share after the filmstrip. - Watch filmstrip. - Ask students what are some things they learned from the filmstrip. Write down ideas and thoughts on a chart in front of class. - Pass out ear worksheet to label as a group. Pass out clipboards and pencils. Put ear poster in front of class for all students to see. - Label ear worksheet together. Ask students first if they can remember part. Then discuss function of part. Write down the ear parts name on worksheet. Wait for students to do the same on their worksheet. - Quickly review main parts of the - Read the poem "Ears Hear." - Discuss how certain animals or objects make noises: bees buzz. - Ask students to brainstorm ideas of other sound words. Record ideas on chalkboard. - Handout copies of "Ears Hear" poem and paper for students to try writing their own "Ears Hear" - Tell students that they may work independently or in a group to write their own "sounds" - Circulate room to help students get started with the poems and help with spelling. - Share poems. Evaluation of student - Did all the students participate in the listening walk and carefully listen? - Were the students able to label the ear with guided help? - Did the students create their own "Ears Hear" poem with guidance? What to do - Write the letter c on the board; make it at least a foot tall. Alternatively, use a letter card large enough for the whole group to see easily. - This letter has two sounds; let's review them. Anyone: what are the two sounds? Good: /c/ g(as in cat) and /s/ (as in sat). - Look for students who are not saying the sounds. Ask them: What are the sounds? Look for students who are making the wrong sounds and model the sounds for them until they have them right. Well done everyone. - We use the /c/ sound for this letter at the start of words like car, cold, clean and we use the /s/ sound for this letter at the start of words like city, circus, cycle. - Write 12 letters on the board: 4 of the letters should be c and they should be interspersed with 8 other letters dissimilar in appearance to c, such as T and v. - When I point to the letter we just learned, say both of its sounds: /c/, /s/. When I point to any other letter, you have to stay quiet. My turn first. Point to a series of letters and either say the sounds or make a performance of saying nothing, as appropriate. - Your turn. Ready? Point to letters randomly, holding on each one for a few seconds. - If a student says the sound for one of the other letters (not c), point to c and say: You only need to make a sound for this letter. When I point to any other letter, stay quiet. Ready? Look for individuals who are saying nothing when you point to c. Have those students try letters individually until they have it (but don't call only on struggling students). Keep going until everyone has it. How much fun does teaching about rocks sound? About as much fun as teaching about soil! Thanks to Reading A-Z, an Aims science project, and our iPod Touches, we managed to learn a lot about rocks and enjoy ourselves at the same time. Oh, thank Heaven! We began with the question: What makes rocks break? Reading A - Z has this great little book about rocks. It is a level C book, but it is totally appropriate for the lesson. |Screen shot of the book.| Readinga-z.com is awesome! I love their leveled readers. This book is great for building background knowledge and learning the proper vocabulary on the subject. Many of my kids have never seen a "mountain" mountain (landfills don't count) in their lives, so reading the word boulder was brand new for them. Then, we matched the vocabulary words with the proper sentences and added them to the anchor chart. On to the science project... The project is called Ice Breakers and it is from an Aims activity book. First, we discuss how rocks can break in nature. The kids speculate how rocks break to make boulders, stones, pebbles, sand, and dust. When one of them finally brings up water, I asked them how would water break the rocks? Do you think it will break if it was frozen? We fill out the prediction chart: For the project, you will need water balloons, plaster of paris, and the freezer. I prepared the water balloons ahead of time. I called the kids up one at a time and had them help me mix the plaster of paris and add it to their model rock. We let the rocks dry in the classroom. Then, we put them in the freezer for two days. After the two days, I took the water balloons out and the kids were amazed to see that the plaster had cracked off and the balloons had broken. We concluded the activity by using our iPod Touches. The kids had to verbalize the activity and explain what they had learned using the app Sonic Pics. The app has them add pictures, text, and their voices to create a little video project (like a power point.) *I'll have to add one of their projects in another post. Then, for some writing fun, we wrote about what we would build with a bunch of rocks. They were cute and enjoyed thinking about how we use rocks. Despite positive responses to its early presentations and support from a number of hardware and software makers, Windows 1.0 was received poorly by critics. Critics felt Windows 1.0 did not meet their expectations. In particular, they felt that Windows 1.0 put too much emphasis on mouse input at a time when mouse use was not yet widespread; not providing enough resources for new users; and for performance issues, especially on systems with lower computer hardware specifications. Despite these criticisms, Windows 1.0 was an important milestone for Microsoft, as it introduced the Microsoft Windows line. Windows 1.0 was declared obsolete and Microsoft stopped providing support and updates for the system on December 31, 2001. He knows you know just bird in general. Like Mars, Like Earth Who are NASA's Earth and Space Science Explorers? The middle school students who track weather to study its effect on bursting tree buds. And the scientist studying black holes in distant galaxies. But also the teacher whose class shares Earth science data with students around the world. And the engineer who designs robotic instruments to probe hard-to-reach planets. All of these people are Earth Explorers, Space Science Explorers or both. The Earth Explorers and Space Science Explorers series features NASA explorers, young and old, with many backgrounds and interests. Rocks can be fun to collect. They come in many shapes and sizes. Some are gray and some are very colorful. They can be very smooth or very rough. All rocks are made of minerals. The color and feel of a rock depend on the minerals it contains. Did you know that clues about the past are hidden in rocks? Some rocks are millions of years old. Over time, wind and water can wear down a rock. Earthquakes and volcanoes also change rocks. The minerals in a rock can tell about its history. They can also tell about the history of the entire Earth. Phil Christensen has a large collection of rocks. He has big rocks and small rocks. He has bumpy rocks and shiny rocks. In all, he has more than 8,000 rocks. That's right -- 8,000! How did he get so many rocks? Phil has liked rocks since he was a kid. He's also always wanted to know more about Mars. Mars is a rocky planet much like Earth. When he was young, Phil spent hours looking at Mars through his telescope. Now, Phil is a scientist for NASA. Scientists are always asking questions. One question Phil has is, "Are rocks on Earth like rocks on Mars?" The first step to finding an answer was to collect rocks from around the world. But how would he do it? Visiting all the places he would need to go would take forever. Instead of collecting the rocks himself, Phil thought of a neat idea. He would ask kids everywhere to send him rocks. That idea was the start of the Rock Around the World project. Students have sent in more than 8,000 rocks. Phil and other scientists study every rock. They use special tools to discover what minerals are in each one. Studying rocks from Earth is the easier part of Phil's project. Learning about rocks on Mars is more difficult. To do this, Phil helped create a special camera that was launched into space. The camera is called THEMIS. It circles Mars and takes pictures of rocks there. The pictures can be used to figure out what kinds of minerals are in Martian rocks. They also give clues about whether there is water on Mars. Scientists learn a lot by comparing rocks from Earth and Mars. They learn how Earth and Mars are different, but they learn how the planets are alike, too. Mars is the planet most like Earth. Humans may even go there someday. Studying Mars "can tell us a lot about the planet ... and what humans have to be prepared for when they get there," Phil said. And the two heroes went through a ceremony, very binding in those days, which was called the Oath of Brotherhood. It meant that they must remain as faithful to each other as though they were really brothers, and that should one prove false to his vow the other would have the right to kill him. Then the two entered the boat and went away down the Rhine, Siegfried to take Gunther's shape, with the aid of the Tarnhelm, and go through the fire to win the maiden; Gunther to wait on the banks of the Rhine until Siegfried brought him the bride, and then took his own shape once more. And he has his own drawer of sort of kid safe kitchen implements. Power functions are functions where y = x^n where "n" is any real constant number. When "n" is a positive integer, we have two possible scenarios of an integer power function. When "n" is odd, the function passes through the origin, (1,1) and (-1,-1). Also, as the exponent increases, the function becomes steeper. When "n" is even, the function passes through the origin, (1,1) and (-1,1). These functions are symmetric about the origin. I want to talk about power functions. I specifically want to talk about power functions where the exponent is a positive integer. We can divide these into two cases. First, the odd power functions, which are Y equals X to the N where N is an odd number. 1, 3, 5, et cetera, and there are the even power functions where Y equals X to the N and N is an even number, 2, 4, 6, et cetera, let's look at Geometer Sketch Pad to get an idea for what these functions look like. Okay. Here we're in Geometer Sketch Pad. We're looking at odd power functions right now. You can see I've got graphed Y equals X, Y equals X cubed Y equals X to the fifth and Y equals X to the seventh. They're color-coded so you can tell which is which and I can change the power of this guy so we can look at other examples of power functions X to the 9th and X to the 11th, et cetera. Notice that they all seem to have things in common. One of them is that they all pass through the origin. They all pass through the point 1,1 All pass through the point negative 1, Negative 1. They all have an increasing tendency. They go up from left to right. You'll also notice that as the power goes up, as we go from X to X cubed to X to the 5th, X to the 11th and so on, that the graph gets closer and closer to the X axis between 0 and 1. It's kind of sucked down into the X axis. But as we zoom out, you can see that there's the opposite behavior. The higher the exponent is, the faster the function increases. So this is X to the 11th right here. This is X to the 5th. X cubed. And X. And you can see that X to the 11th increases really fast. And if I increase to an even higher exponent you can see it's faster still. Now let's look at even power functions. Here the even power function is X squared, X to the 4th, X to the 6th and X to the 8th. Like the odd power functions they all pass through the origin. They all pass through 1, 1, but these graphs also pass through negative 1, 1, and they are not increasing functions. In fact, they're symmetric about the Y axis. Now, like the odd power functions, the higher the power is, the more the graph hugs the X axis between 0 and 1. You can see that's happening here with Y equals X to the 8th. If I increase the power, that's even more noticeable, X to the 10th. X to the 11th, X to the 12th and so on. Let's see what happens when we zoom out. Again, like the odd power functions, the higher the power, the faster the increase when X is greater than 1. So this is X to the 12th. This is X to the 6th. X to the 4th and X squared. So just a brief overview. You can see that this graph is symmetric with respect to the Y axis. What kind of symmetry do the odd functions have? Let's take a look back at them. These guys are symmetric about the origin. We say they have 180 degrees of symmetry with respect to the origins. You can rotate them 180 degrees and you'll get the same graph. Another way you can look at it is you can reflect them across the X axis and then across the Y axis, and you'll get the exact same graph. Okay. Let's review what we've learned. So about odd functions. We've learned that, first of all, let's talk about the domain. You can see that these functions are going to be defined for all real numbers. There's no special reason why they wouldn't be. So the domain is going to be all real numbers. From negative infinity to infinity. Also the graphs pass through 0, 0, and 1, 1. Graphs include 0, 0 and 1, 1. And for the moment I'm not going to list negative 1, negative 1 because so far what I've written also goes for even power functions. The domain is all real numbers for these guys. And they also pass through 0, 0 and 1, 1. So I'm going to focus on things on properties that they both have. And speaking of properties that they both have, if you look at the right side of either kind of power function, the right side, the end goes up, the end behavior is as X goes to infinity Y goes to infinity. Let me write that down. The right end goes up. Whoops. One letter ahead. End goes up. Now, properties that are specific to the odd and even ones, the odd power functions are symmetric with respect to the origin. Let me write that down. Symmetric with respect to -- whoops -- respect to the origin. Okay. Remember, that kind of symmetry is where you can rotate the graph 180 degrees and you'd get the same picture. Also, the range the all real numbers. You can see it looking at the graph that these are going to go up to infinity and down to negative infinity. So you're going to hit every possible Y value here. Now, let's take a look at the even functions. Here we have symmetry with respect to the Y axis. So symmetric with respect to, I'll abbreviate, the Y axis. And we also have -- we don't have the same range here. We can't get negative numbers out of this function. When you're raising to an even power, negatives become positive. So the range only includes the non-negative numbers. you can get 0. And you can get positive numbers but you can't get negatives. So just to recap, all of these power functions, I'm speaking specifically about power functions where the exponent is a positive integer. All of them have a domain, all real numbers. All of them pass through the point 0, 0 and 1, 1. All of them have the property that the right end goes up. Now, if you want to know what the left end does you look at the symmetry, when a graph is symmetric with respect to the origin, the left end goes down. The range is all real numbers. With even functions, they're symmetric with respect to the Y axis. So the left end does the same thing the right end does. It goes up to infinity and the range is the set of non-negative numbers. We'll be needing to understand power functions really well when we start setting polynomial functions, because it turns out that the end behavior of polynomial functions is determined by the end behavior of power functions. Title: How to Change Your Address on Google Maps on PC or Mac Google Maps is a widely-used navigation tool that provides maps, directions, traffic updates, and location information. If you've moved to a new address, updating your home location on Google Maps ensures accurate search results and routing. This guide will walk you through changing your address on Google Maps using a computer (PC or Mac). **Key Tips**: 1. Make sure you are logged into your Google account before starting this process. 2. You can only change the home address associated with your personal Google account. 3. Changes may take several hours to appear due to caching mechanisms. 4. Be cautious when sharing your home address publicly; review privacy settings as needed. 5. For business addresses, contact the business owner or manager to update their listing. ### Step 1: Open Google Maps in a web browser Visit <https://www.google.com/maps> and ensure you're signed in with the correct Google account. Using an up-to-date browser like Chrome, Firefox, Safari, or Edge facilitates seamless interactions. Accessing Google Maps via a desktop environment offers better control than mobile devices. ### Step 2: Navigate to 'Your Places' section Click on the hamburger menu (three horizontal lines) located at the top left corner of the screen. From the dropdown list, select 'Your places'. A panel displaying two tabs - 'MAPPED LOCATIONS' & 'LABELLED' - should be visible now. ### Step 3: Go to the 'LABELED' tab Switch to the second tab labelled 'LABELLED'. Here, you can see various labels such as 'Home', 'Work', 'School', etc., along with any custom ones created by you. The purpose of these labels is to make frequently visited locations quickly accessible within Google Maps. ### Step 4: Edit Home Location Locate the label named 'Home'. Click on the edit button next to it which looks like a pencil icon. Upon clicking, a dialog box pops up asking for confirmation about editing your home address. Confirm by pressing 'OK'. ### Step 5: Update your home address In the text field provided under 'Address', type in your new residential address accurately. As you start typing, Google suggests auto-complete options based on its geocoding database. Selecting one saves time and reduces potential errors. Double check all fields including street name, house number, city, state, and zip code before saving changes. ### Step 6: Save Changes Once satisfied with the entered data, click on the blue 'Save' button situated at the bottom right corner of the dialog box. After hitting save, you might notice a loading spinner indicating processing of updated information. Once completed, a success message appears confirming the modification. ### Step 7: Verify Updated Information To verify if the operation was successful, navigate back to the main map view. Perform a search using terms like "my home" or simply zoom into the area where your home resides. Ideally, the red pin should mark the exact spot of your newly inputted address. If the new address doesn't show immediately, wait a few hours considering possible cache delays. However, if issues persist even after significant waiting periods, consider reaching out to Google Support for assistance. Remember, keeping your digital footprint current helps maintain efficient online services usage while ensuring safety and accuracy! The position of something is where that thing is in space and time. Usually people use Cartesian coordinates to write down a position. So a scientist might say that the balloon was at "x", "y", "z" when it popped at a certain time. [.7 sec, then D and G speak at once, sorting out that S said 'Grandaddy'] The average velocity of an object is its change in position divided by the total amount of time taken. When calculating the average velocity, only the times and positions at the starting and ending points are taken into account. The average velocity is different from the instantaneous velocity, and the two are many times not the same number. I want to talk about average velocity. Let's start by doing an example. A pumpkin is catapulted into the air, time T as in seconds and the height of the pumpkin F of T is feet and it's given by values on this table. I've got values for T equals 0 to 8. Now I want to calculate some changes in position. And get used to this symbol in calculus, the delta means change in. So delta position means change in position. Over the interval from 0 to 1. So the change of position from on the interval of 0 to 1 would be F of 1 minus F of 0. Now, F of 1 is 118. F of 0 is 4. And so I'd get 114. But remember the units are feet. So this would be in feet. So that means that the pumpkin over that first second increases in height 114 feet. Now let's look at the interval from 1 to 4. The change in position F of 4 minus F of 1. Also remember it's final position minus initial position when you're calculating a change in position. F of 4 is 268. And again F of 1 is 118. So you just subtract. And you get 150 feet. And finally from 4 to 6, you get F of 6 minus F of 4. F of 6, 208. That's the final position. Minus the initial position. F of 4, 268. And you get negative 60. And what does that mean? That means that over this interval from T. Equals 4 to T equals 6 the pumpkin has dropped 60 feet. What does this have to do with average velocity? Average velocity over an interval from A to B is defined as the change in position divided by the change in time. And so what we were calculating just now were changes in position. If you divide that by the change in time, the length of the interval, you get the average velocity.Let's compute some average velocities. First, for the interval of 1 to 4 we would have average velocity is F of 4 minus F of 1 over 4 minus 1. Now we already calculated F of 4 minus F of 1. It's back here. 150 feet. So this would be on top 150 feet and on bottom 3 seconds. So this is going to be 50 feet per second. Okay. And how about on the interval of 5 to 7? Average velocity is F of 7. Remember, final position minus initial. Minus F of 5 over 7 minus 5. F of 7 going back to the table is 130. F of 5, 254. So we have 130 minus 254 over 7 minus -- actually, I'll just write 2. 7 minus 5 is 2. Now, this numerator, 130 minus 254 is negative 124. And that's in feet. Over two seconds. And that's going to give me minus 62 feet per second. What does it mean that the average velocity is negative? It just means that the pumpkin's probably on its way down on this interval of time. So average velocity is change in position over change in time. And remember change in position is final position minus initial position. I guess it's a little too cool for bathing suits huh. The Structure of Birds' Feathers The most important difference that distinguishes birds from other creatures is surely their ability to fly. Birds can fly by means of their feathers, which in fact have a very special structure. A bird feather is a light structure that has lift force and can easily regain its original shape. If we examine a bird's feather under a microscope, we see a marvelous creation. In the middle part of the feather is a hard central shaft with a series of paired branches called barbs on either side. These barbs, whose lengths and softness vary, enable the bird to fly in the air. As we examine the subject in further detail, we encounter even more interesting structures. Minute hairs called "barbules" project from each barb. Barbules cannot be seen with the naked eye. On these barbules are little hooks. The barbules are interlocked by these hooks, just like a zipper. The hooks are clamped together like the two rows of a zipper. The barbules held together by hooks are so close to each other that not even smoke can pass through them. If these hooks are somehow unlocked, it is enough for the bird to shake itself or to put its feathers in order with its beak, to make the feathers return to their previous state. In order to survive, birds must always keep their feathers clean, neat and ready for use. For the care of their feathers, they use oil sacs at the bottom of their tails. They take some oil with their beak, and then clean and polish their feathers. In swimming birds, this oil prevents water from reaching their skin when they are in the water or in the rain. In addition, birds can prevent loss of body heat by ruffling their feathers in cold weather. In hot weather, on the other hand, they keep their body cool by smoothing their feathers. Each group of feathers in a particular part of the body has particular functions. For example the down feathers and the feathers that lie along the wings and tail have different structures. Large tail feathers are used for steering as well as for braking. As the wing feathers are spread wide while flying, the surface area is expanded and lift force increased. When the bird flaps its wing downwards, these feathers come closer and prevent air passing through. As the wings move up, the feathers open wide and permit air to pass through them. In order to maintain their ability to fly, birds shed their feathers at certain times of the year. Large torn or worn out feathers that do not function properly any more are renewed quickly. This detailed structure exists in the feathers of every bird. All of them have the features that enable birds to fly. One who reflects on what is explained here and uses his intellect will notice the spectacular attributes in birds and will realize that these are Allah's creation. Just thinking about the attributes of birds alone is enough to understand that Allah is almighty. It is stated in the Qur'an that believers reflect on the creation by Allah: The kingdom of the heavens and earth belongs to Allah. Allah has power over all things. In the creation of the heavens and the earth, and the alternation of night and day, there are Signs for people with intelligence: those who remember Allah, standing, sitting and lying on their sides, and reflect on the creation of the heavens and the earth (and say): 'Our Lord, You have not created this for nothing. Glory be to You! So safeguard us from the punishment of the Fire. (Surah Al 'Imran: 189-191) [7.6 sec click, silence, D sniffs twice] " "Oh, I say, let's go! Such fun, Billy! I know you'll like it. The Main (pronounced in German like the English word "mine") is a river in Germany, 524 km in length, and one of the bigger tributaries of the Rhine river. Its average discharge at Frankfurt East Harbour is 190 cubic metres/second. Okay, hang on one second, and I'll come and help you, okay? Title: Creating Secure Passwords using Letters and Numbers Hey kids! Today, we're going to learn about creating secure passwords using both letters and numbers. But before we do that, let's play a little game. Imagine you have 26 different colored alphabet blocks and 10 different colored number blocks. Your task is to create a line of 5 blocks with at least one number block and at least two letter blocks. Can you make many different lines following this rule? Sure, you can arrange the blocks in many ways! That's exactly what we'll explore now - counting all those unique arrangements or "passwords." First, remember that order matters here since ABAAB and BAAAA are considered different combinations even when they contain the same letters and numbers. So, our passwords should allow for different arrangements too. To account for that, we multiply by 5! (read as five factorial), which means calculating 5 x 4 x 3 x 2 x 1 = 120. This gives us the total number of distinct arrangements for any given combination of 5 blocks. Now, think of three types of valid lines based on the hint provided earlier: 1. Four letter blocks and one number block. We need to choose 4 letters out of 26 available ones and pick just one number block from 10 options. The formula for choosing r items from n choices is called "combinations," written as C(n,r). In our case, we calculate C(26,4) * C(10,1). 2. Three letter blocks and two number blocks. Similarly, we find the combinations for picking 3 letters from 26 (C(26,3)) and selecting 2 numbers out of 10 (C(10,2)). 3. Two letter blocks and three number blocks. Finally, we determine the combinations for having 2 letters picked from 26 (C(26,2)) and obtaining 3 numbers chosen from 10 (C(10,3)). To finish, add together these calculated values and then multiply their sum by 5! (total number of distinct arrangements). And voila, you'll have the final answer to how many potential secure passwords you could build! Have fun trying various combinations while understanding the importance of strong and creative passwords! God is beyond limitation. His strength is infinite. If He chooses to create a mountain that is too heavy for Him to lift he would simultaneously become strong enough to lift it (which actually means he's not omnipotent, because if he can gradually become stronger, then he can't be all powerful).
