Title: How to Highlight Text in a PDF Document PDF (Portable Document Format) is a popular file format used for sharing documents across various platforms without worrying about formatting issues. One common practice when reading or reviewing PDFs is highlighting text to emphasize important information or make it easier to locate later. Unfortunately, unlike word processing software such as Microsoft Word, Adobe Acrobat Reader DC does not have built-in tools to add highlights directly within the free version. However, there are several methods you can employ using both online and offline solutions. This article will guide you through these methods with clear instructions and helpful tips along the way. **Method 1: Using Adobe Acrobat Pro DC (Paid Service)** Adobe Acrobat Pro DC offers an extensive range of features including the ability to edit and annotate PDF files. Here's how you can utilize this tool to highlight text: Step 1: **Open your PDF document in Adobe Acrobat Pro DC.** To begin, launch Adobe Acrobat Pro DC on your computer. Open the application, then click "File" > "Open" to select the PDF file you wish to modify. Step 2: **Navigate to the 'Comment' pane.** Once your document is open, look at the right side panel. If it isn't visible, press Ctrl+Shift+A (Windows) or Command+Shift+A (Mac). Click on the "Comment" icon if it isn't already selected. Step 3: **Choose the highlighter tool.** Under the "Comments" section, click on the "Highlight" button represented by a pen nib over highlighted text. You'll find different colors available; choose one that suits your needs. Step 4: **Select the text to be highlighted.** Click and drag your cursor over the text you want to highlight. Release the mouse button once all desired text is covered. The chosen color should now appear over the specified text indicating successful highlighting. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Remember to save your changes before closing the program. Any unsaved modifications won't be applied. - Experiment with different colored highlighters to categorize types of content (e.g., yellow for main ideas, pink for supporting details). **Method 2: Using Online Tools** There are numerous web-based applications designed specifically for editing and annotating PDFs, many of which provide free basic functionalities. We'll demonstrate using Sejda, but other reliable options include Smallpdf and PDFescape. Step 1: **Go to Sejda's website.** Visit <https://www.sejda.com/> from any web browser. Scroll down until you see "Edit PDF" under the "Tools" header, then click "Start". Your birthday's a long way off Sarah. Like I remember mister brown can move... "It's a way to introduce animals we can study and learn about." That sounded exciting! Margaret decided to try her own animal trap. She got a box and placed it outside, putting some food inside. Boiling water is a fundamental skill that is useful for many recipes and household tasks. This tutorial will provide you with an in-depth guide on how to boil water and cook, ensuring clarity, practicality, and ease of application. **Things You'll Need:** * A pot or kettle * Water * Stove or hot plate * Optional: Spices, salt, or other flavor enhancers * Optional: Food to be cooked (e.g., pasta, vegetables) ## Step 1: Choose Your Vessel Select a suitable pot or kettle made from materials like stainless steel, aluminum, copper, or enameled cast iron. These materials are good conductors of heat and can safely bring water to a boil without cracking or leaching harmful chemicals into the water. The size of your vessel should accommodate the amount of water you plan to boil while leaving enough room for adequate water circulation during heating. ## Step 2: Fill the Pot With Cold Water Always start with cold water as it ensures even heating throughout the process. Use filtered or tap water depending upon your preference and local water quality standards. Avoid filling the pot to its maximum capacity; instead, leave some space near the top to prevent accidental spills when the water begins to boil rapidly. ## Step 3: Add Flavor Enhancers (Optional) If desired, add ingredients such as salt, herbs, or spices to enhance the taste of the water. Keep in mind that adding these elements may slightly alter the boiling point due to increased solute concentration. However, this effect is minimal unless large quantities of seasonings are used. ## Step 4: Place the Pot on the Stovetop or Hot Plate Position the pot containing water onto a burner or hot plate. Turn the heat setting to medium or high, depending on your stove's power output and desired speed of reaching a boil. Lower settings might require more time but consume less energy, whereas higher settings quickly reach boiling temperatures at the cost of greater electricity usage. ## Step 5: Monitor the Temperature Increase Wait patiently for the water to warm up gradually until it reaches its boiling point. During this stage, observe bubbles forming around the edges of the pot - known as nucleation sites - which indicate rising temperature levels. As the water continues warming, more nucleation sites appear, eventually leading to full-scale boiling characterized by rapid bubble formation across the entire surface area of the liquid. ## Key Tips and Guidelines: * Never leave unattended boiling water, especially if children or pets are present, since overheated pots pose risks of scalding accidents and potential fires. * When cooking food items like pasta or vegetables, ensure they are fully submerged underwater before bringing them to a boil. Once boiled, maintain consistent heat levels to avoid uneven cooking results caused by fluctuating temperatures. * If using an electric kettle, always fill it below the marked MAX line and never pour water directly from the kettle after boiling, as it retains extremely high temperatures capable of causing severe burns. * To cool down boiled water rapidly, transfer it into smaller containers or immerse the original container in ice baths, promoting faster heat dissipation through increased surface areas exposed to colder environments. By following these steps and guidelines, you're now equipped with the knowledge required to efficiently boil water and prepare meals confidently. Happy cooking! Be very gentle with it is will break. Choosing the right tech deck brand can be an exciting yet daunting task due to the variety of options available in the market. This guide will help you navigate through the process with ease by breaking down the steps involved in selecting the perfect tech deck brand that suits your preferences and skill level. ### Step 1: Determine Your Skill Level Before diving into different brands, assess your current skateboarding skills. Are you just starting out or have some experience under your belt? Knowing this information is crucial as certain brands cater more towards beginners while others focus on advanced tricks and techniques. If you're new to tech decks, consider choosing a beginner-friendly option; if you've got experience, explore those designed for intermediate to expert users. Key Tip: Don't rush into buying a high-end model without considering whether it matches your abilities. It could lead to frustration rather than enjoyment. ### Step 2: Research Various Brands There are numerous tech deck brands offering unique features and benefits. Some popular ones include: 1. **Tech Deck**: The original fingerboard company, they offer various models ranging from basic sets to professional ones. Their products often come with authentic graphics licensed from real skateboard companies. 2. **Flip Sky Skates**: Known for their durable construction and smooth riding experience, these are great for both beginners and experienced riders. 3. **Blackriver Ramps**: While primarily known for their quality ramp sets, they also produce top-notch tech decks characterized by precision manufacturing and intricate details. 4. **DKNG Fingerboards**: Offering handcrafted wooden boards, DKNG focuses on creating realistic replicas of iconic skateboards. However, they tend to be pricier due to their craftsmanship. 5. **Yomega**: Yomega provides affordable starter kits which make them ideal for those looking to try out fingerboarding without investing heavily upfront. Guideline: Remember, there isn't necessarily one 'best' brand - what works best depends largely on personal preference and needs. ### Step 3: Compare Prices Prices vary significantly between brands and individual products within the same brand. Make sure to compare prices across multiple sources (online retailers, local stores) before making a decision. Keep in mind that cheaper doesn't always mean inferior quality, nor does expensive equate to superior performance. Balance cost with value based on your requirements. Key Tip: Lookout for sales or discounts that might allow you to get a better board at a lower price. ### Step 4: Check Reviews & Ratings And um they wanted to go down to earth. The Season of Advent, which begins on Sunday about four weeks before Christmas Day, is celebrated by the Catholic and Anglican Churches, as well as others. It is a time for people to prepare themselves for two different things: for the coming of the baby Jesus and Christmas, and for the second coming of Jesus, when he shall rule over all the Earth in peace. Not all Christian people remember Advent. Some people use it as a time of fasting, study, meditation and prayer. The following experiment is a fun and easy way to see the effects of gravitropism on a plant These are the materials needed to make your own upside down plant: -Small bean plant Pour water over sponges until they are moist. Take small potted plant out of pot and remove much of the soil. (We used a small bean plant that we planted in a plastic cup.) Sandwich the root ball of the plant between two sponges. Tie the string around the sponges lengthwise and widthwise. Then hang the plant upside down in a sunny window with a tray under it to catch excess water. Keep the sponges watered and in a couple of days the plant will begin to turn around and grow upright!! To see gravitropism in action press play and watch the plant grow upwards. Title: How to Change Your Desktop Background in Windows Windows operating system allows users to personalize their desktop background with ease. This tutorial will guide you through the process of changing your desktop background in Windows, providing detailed explanations for each step along with helpful tips and guidelines. **Step 1: Accessing Personalization Settings** To start, click on the Start button (Windows icon) located at the bottom left corner of your screen or press the Windows key on your keyboard. Once the Start Menu appears, look for the gear icon labeled as "Settings" and click on it. This action will open the main settings window where you can modify various aspects of your Windows environment. Key Tip: If you're using an older version of Windows like Windows 7, right-click anywhere on your desktop and select "Personalize" from the context menu that pops up. **Step 2: Navigating to Background Options** In the Settings window, scroll down until you find the "Personalization" category. Click on this option to proceed. A new page should appear displaying different customizable features related to your computer's appearance. Among these options, locate and click on "Background". Guideline: Remember that depending on your Windows version, some categories might be named differently but they still serve similar functions. **Step 3: Choosing a New Image** Upon clicking "Background", you'll see three primary options: Picture, Solid Color, and Slideshow. For choosing a single image, select 'Picture'. Now, under 'Choose your picture', browse through the preloaded images provided by Microsoft or click on "Browse" to choose an image file from your local storage. Helpful Tips: - The ideal resolution for a desktop wallpaper is dependent on your monitor's native resolution. However, higher resolutions usually provide better quality. - JPEG format is commonly used due to its compatibility and smaller size compared to other formats such as PNG or BMP. **Step 4: Setting Up Position Preferences** After selecting your preferred image, notice the "Choose a fit" dropdown box just below the 'Choose your picture' section. Here, you can adjust how the selected image aligns with your desktop. Five choices are available - Fill, Fit, Stretch, Tile, and Center. Experiment with these to get the best view of your chosen photo. Understanding Each Option: - Fill: Resizes the image to fill the entire screen while maintaining aspect ratio; parts of the image may be cut off if they don't match perfectly. - Fit: Displays the whole image without distortion but there might be empty spaces around the edges. - Stretch: Distorts the image to completely cover the screen which often results in poor visual quality. - Tile: Repeats the image across the screen both horizontally and vertically. - Center: Places the image at the center of the screen leaving blank space surrounding it. **Step 5: Saving Changes** Once you have made all necessary adjustments, simply close the Personalization window. Any changes made automatically save and become immediately visible as your new desktop background. Remember, you can always revert back to default settings by following these steps again and either selecting one of the preset backgrounds or setting the color to solid black/white. Enjoy personalizing your workspace! Rich in vitamins A, C, and K, dandelion greens also contain calcium, iron, and potassium. This guide will walk you through identifying, harvesting, preparing, and cooking dandelion greens at home. **Step 1: Identify and Harvest Dandelion Greens** Identification: Look for bright yellow flowers (dandelion blossoms) growing close to the ground with multiple sets of jagged-edged, serrated leaves radiating from the base. The leaves should have a distinct notch where they meet the stem. Mathematicians use graphs that "plot themselves", without taking measurements. For example, the formula "x = y" says that the value of x will always be equal to the value of y. If you plot this formula as a graph, with x as the horizontal axis and y as the vertical axis, you will get a graph that looks like a straight line angled at precisely 45 degrees. The use of these graphs forms a branch of mathematics called analytic geometry. That's relatively good food for you all the time. Caffeine comes from other plants as well. It is in guarana, yerba mat , cacao, and some plants used to make tea. The plants use caffeine as a "pesticide". This is a chemical that kills insects if they eat the plant. It is the way the plant protects itself. It's really kind of a simple book for you Chi but let's just do our work and get it over with. Waves might look like chaos (top), but there is a pattern to them (bottom). Click on image for full size Top image courtesy of NASA; bottom image courtesy of NOAA Ocean waves are usually formed by wind. Large waves will form if the wind is strong, if it blows over a large distance, and if it blows for a long time. Waves may look chaotic when they approach a beach -crashing and full of sea foam. However, waves do have organization to them, especially in open water when they are not crashing. A wave's highest point is called its crest. The low point between two waves is called a trough. The vertical distance between crest and trough is called the wave height. The distance between two waves is called the wavelength and it is usually measured either from one crest to the next or from one trough to the next. The time it takes for waves to pass is called the wave period. Why is it important to make all these wave measurements? Sailors rely on reports of the size of waves when planning travel. Surfers rely on reports of the size of waves when searching for places to surf. Waves have an impact on currents at the coast and beach erosion too. Not all waves coming into a coast are the same size. Some are larger than average and others are smaller than average. About once every hour a wave comes by that is about double the size of the others. There are other types of large waves that are very rare such as rogue waves, which are extremely large, often travel alone and not in the direction of the other waves. A tsunami is also a powerful wave that is not caused by wind. It has such a long wavelength that it is not visible until it gets near shore. Waves that travel very long distances - far from the winds that formed them - are called swells. Swells tend to be large waves with flatter crests and are very regular in shape and size. When over deep water, the energy in a wave moves forward, but the water does not. The water moves up and down in circles. As waves move into shallow water, they act differently. The energy in the wave runs into the shallow seafloor. This causes the wave to steepen and eventually to topple over or break. Shop Windows to the Universe Science Store! The Summer 2010 issue of The Earth Scientist , available in our online store , includes articles on rivers and snow, classroom planetariums, satellites and oceanography, hands-on astronomy, and global warming. This movement creates different pressures in the atmosphere which creates the winds around the globe. Since the Earth spins,...more Ocean waves often move towards a beach at an angle. This moves water along the coast in a longshore current. Longshore currents grow stronger when the waves come towards the beach at a large angle. [tries to flip page before MOT finishes page] Title: How to Draw the Flag of Japan Introduction: The flag of Japan is a simple yet iconic design that has represented the country since the late 19th century. Consisting of a white field with a red circle at its center, this flag is known as the Nissh ki ( , "sun-mark flag") or Hinomaru ( , "circle of the sun"). This tutorial will guide you through the process of drawing the Japanese flag step by step. By following these clear and easy-to-understand instructions, even beginners can create an accurate representation of one of the world's most recognizable national flags. Katie is in the bathtub splashing in the water with both hands. Her father sits on the floor next to the tub making sure she is safe. "Katie, Katie," he says as he picks up a washcloth. "Are you ready to play our special game?" Katie looks up and sees her father's smiling face. She smiles at him and laughs. He says, "Let's play Peek-a-Boo,"; and puts a washcloth in front of his face. Katie reaches out and pats the top of his head. Her father says, "Peek-a-Boo, Katie, I can't see you." He lowers the washcloth so his eyes are no longer covered. Katie squeals with delight. He covers his eyes again and says, "Peek-a-Boo, Katie, I still can't see you." Katie's father holds the washcloth out toward her, saying, "Your turn, Katie." She takes the washcloth from his hand and puts it in front of her face. Her father says, "Where's Katie?" Katie drops the washcloth in the water and splashes with her hands. She babbles to her father, "Dadadada. Babababa." He says, "I think you're saying that you're tired of playing Peek-a-Boo. Let's play with your sponges." Like many young babies, Katie is learning about language: - She knows that it's fun to play with another person. - She looks up when her father says her name. - She smiles when her father smiles at her. Katie's father helps her learn about language: - He talks with her during a daily activity - bathtime. - He says her name again and again so that she will learn to recognize it. - He takes several turns in their game - then encourages her to take a turn. - He responds to her babbles as if he knows what she is saying. I USTA have tickets to go back. Hello young learners! Today, we're going to explore the world of vectors and their spans through a fun interactive tool called "Visualizing the Span of Two Vectors." This tool will help us understand how different vectors relate to each other in three-dimensional space (that's just a fancy way of saying we'll be working with things that have length, width, and height). Let's dive into this exciting adventure together! Imagine holding two magic wands - one in each hand. Each wand has its unique magical properties; think of them like special abilities or traits. Now let's say I ask you to create new spells by combining these wands in various ways - maybe twirling them around or tapping them against each other while casting your spell. These combined movements represent something called a 'linear combination.' In mathematics, when we talk about a linear combination of two vectors (our imaginary wands), we simply multiply each vector by a number (which could be any real value) and then add those results together. In our case, if we had Vector u and Vector v, a linear combination would look something like this: 1 * u + 2 * v, where 1 and 2 are our numbers. Now, imagine collecting all the possible outcomes from every single spell you ever created using both wands simultaneously. That collection represents what mathematicians call the 'span' of the two vectors. It forms a sort of magical playground containing infinite possibilities formed by combining your enchanted tools! Our interactive applet allows us to see this concept visually. We start by placing two vectors (wands) within a 3D space. By clicking on "Plot some linear combinations," we generate several random combinations based on our initial vectors. You can rotate and move the scene to observe these fascinating creations! When you click "Show span{u, v}", you'll witness the entire magical realm constructed by the interplay between your two wands. Every line, curve, and shape inside this boundary stems from intriguing interactions among countless linear combinations. Finally, we introduce a third vector 'w'. When we set w equal to u + v, we discover whether this newcomer resides within the existing magical domain. But don't stop there! Set 'w' to other vectors too. Sometimes, you may find that some vectors cannot be reached through combinations of your original pair. And that's perfectly fine because not everything can be explained solely through the interaction of two elements. Variety makes our universe more interesting, after all! So go ahead, experiment, and enjoy exploring the mesmerizing world hidden behind these magical wands - I mean vectors. Remember, math is everywhere, even in places where we least expect it, like in the whimsical realm of wizards and vectors! Come on you only have about three bites left. Vilnius is the capital city of Lithuania. It is also the largest city in that country. Vilnius is the largest city and the capital of Lithuania, with a population of 553,904 (850,700 together with Vilnius County) as of December 2005. It is the seat of the Vilnius city municipality and of the Vilnius district municipality. It is also the capital of Vilnius County. Vilnius lies 312 kilometres (194 mi) from the Baltic Sea and Klaip da, the chief Lithuanian seaport. Clara Brandon's simple history may be briefly summed up. She was the only child of a Mr. Frederick Brandon, who, a widower in the second year of his marriage, had since principally resided at the "Elms," a handsome mansion and grounds which he had leased of the uncle of the late Sir Harry Compton. Catching a runaway rabbit can be a challenging but rewarding task. With patience, persistence, and the right approach, you can successfully catch the rabbit and ensure its safety. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you accomplish this goal: **Step 1: Observe the Rabbit's Behavior** Start by observing the rabbit from a distance. Take note of its patterns, favorite hiding spots, and general behavior. This will give you valuable insights into the best ways to approach catching it. Key tip: Remain calm and avoid making sudden movements or loud noises, as this may scare the rabbit away. **Step 2: Create a Safe Environment** Ensure that the area where you plan to catch the rabbit is safe and free from potential hazards such as roads, predators, or poisonous plants. If possible, choose an enclosed space like a fenced yard or a room indoors. Guideline: A controlled environment makes it easier to monitor the rabbit's movement and reduces the risk of harm coming to the animal. **Step 3: Gather Necessary Tools and Equipment** You'll need some basic tools to increase your chances of success. Consider using a lightweight towel or blanket, gloves, a carrier or cage, water, and food (preferably fresh vegetables). These items will help you safely contain and transport the rabbit without causing stress or injury. **Step 4: Approach Slowly and Calmly** Begin approaching the rabbit slowly and calmly, avoiding direct eye contact which might make it feel threatened. Speak softly to reassure the rabbit and minimize its fear. As you get closer, move sideways rather than directly towards it, as this appears less threatening. **Step 5: Use Food as Bait** Offer the rabbit some enticing treats like carrots, leafy greens, or pellets. Once it starts eating, gently toss more food towards the container you intend to use for transportation. Over time, try moving the food closer to the container until the rabbit becomes comfortable entering it voluntarily. **Step 6: Contain the Rabbit** If the rabbit enters the container willingly, close it quickly yet carefully so as not to startle the animal. If necessary, use your towel or blanket to cover the rabbit before placing it in the container. Remember to handle the rabbit gently at all times to prevent unnecessary stress or injuries. **Step 7: Provide Water and Shelter** After capturing the rabbit, provide immediate access to clean drinking water. Offer shelter if needed, especially if the weather conditions are harsh. Keep the rabbit in a quiet, secure place while you decide what to do next. **Step 8: Check for Identification** Look for any identification tags or microchips that could indicate ownership. Contact local animal shelters, veterinary clinics, or rabbit rescues to report finding a loose rabbit and seek advice on further steps. **Step 9: Reunite with Owner (if applicable)** If the rabbit is identified, arrange for its return to its owner. If unable to locate the owner after reasonable efforts, consider keeping the rabbit yourself or rehoming it through reputable channels. Consult with experts about proper care and housing requirements. Remember, every rabbit is unique, and there's no one-size-fits-all solution when trying to catch a runaway bunny. Patience, understanding, and compassionate handling should always be your guiding principles throughout this process. Nintendo was formed on September 23, 1889. They originally were a company that made playing cards. Then it went on to making toys, but in the 1960s, they started doing other things, such as owning hotels. They began making video games only in the 1970s. In 1980, they came out with a hand-held game device called Game and Watch. Nintendo's first video game console was the Color TV Game. Famicom, Nintendo's second console, was first only released in Japan, but it was later released in some other areas, such as North America and Europe. When they released it in there, its name was changed to "Nintendo Entertainment System", or NES for short. They also released some popular stand-alone video games, with names like the arcade game "Donkey Kong", and NES games "Super Mario Bros.", "Duck Hunt", and "The Legend of Zelda". Later, the company made hand-helds such as the Game Boy, "DS", and "3DS" and home consoles such as Nintendo 64, GameCube, Wii, Wii U, and Switch. We're going to play with baby Article. Rationale: In order for children who are beginning readers to move on to reading fluently, they must learn many different things. One very helpful component of learning how to read is to know what a digraph is. A digraph is what is made when 2 or more letters with individual sounds are combined in order to make a new, single sound. This lesson will teach the ay = /A/ correspondence. This simply means that the 2 graphemes 'a' and 'y' are put together to form the digraph 'ay' to make the /A/ phoneme. After this lesson is over, the children should be able to hear and see words with the ay = /A/. Materials: Letters of the alphabet, Elkonin boxes, A Day with May, by Nat Gabriel (Reader's Digest Children's Publishing, Inc. 2000), primary paper, pencils, and chart paper. 1) Begin the lesson by making sure all the students know what a vowel is. Then explain to the students that by taking two vowels such as 'a' and 'y' and combining them, it makes not two sounds anymore, but one!! "Today I am going to teach you what sound is made when the vowels 'a' and 'y' are put side by side." 2) "When the letters 'a' and 'y' are beside each other, they make the ay = /A/ sound. I want you all to look up here at the chart while I read the tongue twister on it. Listen carefully for our ay = /A/ sound. May plays with Jay and Kay once a day. Now lets all say it together. What words did we hear that had our ay = /A/ sound? Yes, that's terrific!!! May, plays, Jay, Kay, and Day." 3) "Hopefully by now you will all be able to know when someone says a word with the ay = /A/ sound when they are talking, but just for practice, I am going to read some sentences to you (one at a time) and when I am done, I want you to raise your hands and tell me how many words I said with the ay = /A/ sound in them. Ok, here is the first sentence, second, third, forth, and fifth. A) May I say that your house is gray? (3) B) The play got better everyday. (2) C) The hen wants to lay her eggs. (1) D) Can Kay and Jay come over today? (3) E) I must say, May days are best to play. (4) Great Job!! You were all able to pick out the correct number of words just by listening to me!" 4) Now the class will do a letterbox lesson with ay = /A/ using Elkonin boxes and laminated letters of the alphabet. The lesson will be thoroughly explained before the children start. "Now that we all know how to do a letterbox lesson, let's begin. First I am only going to tell you to spell some words with only 2 letterboxes and then we will work our way up to 3 and 4 letterboxes. The first word I want you to spell is 'say'. Using 2 letterboxes, place the letters where they belong. When you are all done, put your hands in your lap and I will show you my letterboxes so you can check and see if you got yours right." If they got it right, we will move on to some more but if not, I will stop and explain why I put 's' in the first letterbox and 'ay' in the second. We will continue our letterbox lesson with the words: day, may, play, say, jay, lay, hay, and pay. When the lesson is over, we will gather together the words we spelled and will place them in a column on a piece of chart paper then practice reading them before the children put away their letterbox materials. 5) "Now that you all seem to have a pretty good grasp on our new vowel correspondence, I am going to read you a book, and together we will try to see how many words we recognize that have the ay = /A/ in them. Then we will do a fun activity with those words. In the book we are about to read called, A Day with May, there are 2 words in the title that make our sound because they have the letters 'a' and 'y' side by side. Now listen carefully as I read the title one more time. By a show of hands, who can tell me what 2 words have the ay = /A/ sound? That's right!! Day and May!! Very good!! Now here is a tricky question. What word in the title has our sound, but doesn't have the 'ay' in the word. That's right!! The word 'A'." Now the book will be read to the children and together we will pick out the ay = /A/ words. "You all did a fabulous job listening for our new sound in the book. Now let's add to our chart by placing our new list of words from the book to our list of words from our letterbox lesson." 6) In order to assess the children, primary paper and pencils will be handed out for a writing activity. "Okay children, I want you to look at the words on the chart we collected from our letterbox lesson and our book with the ay = /A/ sound. Pick one of those words and write it down on your paper. Once you are done doing that, write a message using your word with the ay = /A/ sound. When you are done, one by one you can share your message with the rest of the class." The papers will be collected when the children are finished sharing and they will be displayed on our ay = /A/ wall in the hall so the rest of the school can see what the children learned about and can see how smart they are. The Reading Genie Website: I See A Bee!! by Jennifer O'Meara LEADERS Handbook of Early Literacy Strategies and Activities : 20 minutes : Whole group This strategy provides students with a tool to help figure out unknown words or words that they do not recognize by sight. - Alphabet cards - Lists of familiar words to blend in an oral fashion - Use a fast-paced sing-along pattern. First, the letter's name is said aloud twice, then the sound of the letter is said aloud three times. - Once you have done this for each letter in the word, give all sounds for that word in a row and then signal for students to say the word. - Increase to longer words. - When the sounds and spelling are familiar, then you can move this activity into a written format. - Show "H" card, students say "H, H,/h/,/h/,/h/" - Repeat for "O" card. - Repeat for "T" card. - Say the sounds all in a row: /h/, /o/, /t/. - Give signal and students say "hot". - This oral practice goes really well. The students enjoy the song. Have students take turns leading the group. - Be aware, boredom may set in for the better readers. We know that all plants need water to survive, even bouquets of cut flowers and plants living in deserts. But have you ever thought about how water moves within the plant? In this activity, you'll put carnations in dyed water to figure out where the water goes. Where do you think the dyed water will travel, and what will this tell you about how the water moves in the cut flowers? Plants use water to keep their roots, stems, leaves and flowers healthy as well as prevent them from drying and wilting. The water is also used to carry dissolved nutrients throughout the plant. Most of the time, plants get their water from the ground. This means it has to transport the water from its roots up and throughout the rest of the plant. How does it do this? Water moves through the plant by means of capillary action. Capillary action occurs when the forces binding a liquid together (cohesion and surface tension) and the forces attracting that bound liquid to another surface (adhesion) are greater than the force of gravity. Through these binding and surface forces, the plant's stem basically sucks up water-almost like drinking through a straw! A simple way of observing capillary action is to take a teaspoon of water and gently pour it in a pool on a countertop. You'll notice that the water stays together in the pool, rather than flattening out across the countertop. (This happens because of cohesion and surface tension.) Now gently dip the corner of a paper towel in the pool of water. The water adheres to the paper and "climbs" up the paper towel. This is called capillary action. Measuring cup Glass cup or vase Blue or red food color Several white carnations (at least three). Tip: Fresher flowers work better than older ones Camera (optional) Measure a half cup of water and pour it into the glass or vase. Add 20 drops of food color to the water in the glass. With the help of an adult, use a knife to cut the bottom stem tips of several (at least three) white carnations at a 45-degree angle. Tip: Be sure not to use scissors, they will crush the stems, reducing their ability to absorb water. Also, shorter stems work better than longer ones. Place the carnations in the dyed water. As you do this, use the stems of the carnations to stir the water until the dye has fully dissolved. Observe the flowers immediately after you put them in the water. If you have a camera, take a picture of the flowers. Observe the flowers two, four, 24, 48 and 72 hours after you put them in the dyed water. Be sure to also observe their stems, especially the bumps where the leaves branch from the stem and it is lighter green (it may be easier to see the dye here). If you have a camera, take pictures of the flowers and stems at these time points. How did the flowers look after two hours? What about after four, 24, 48 and 72 hours? How did their appearance change over this time period? What does the flowers' change in appearance tell you about how water moves through them? Extra: In this activity, you used carnations, but do you think you'd see the same results with other flowers and plants? Try this activity with another white flower- a daisy, for instance-or a plant that is mostly stem, such as a stalk of celery. Extra: Try doing this activity again but use higher or lower concentrations of food color, such as one half, twice, four times or 10 times as much; be sure to mix each dye amount with the same amount of water. What happens if you increase or decrease the concentration of food color in the water? Extra: How would you make a multicolor carnation? Tip: You could try (1) leaving the flower for a day in one color of water and then putting it in another color of water for a second day or (2) splitting the end of the stem in two and immersing each half in a different color of water. Tutorial: How to Electroplate Nickel ===================================== Introduction ------------ Electroplating is an electromagnetic process that deposits a thin layer of metal onto the surface of another material through the reduction of cations of that metal by applying an electric current. This article will focus on electroplating nickel, which is commonly used to add a corrosion-resistant or decorative finish to various objects, such as jewelry, car parts, or electronic components. ### Safety Guidelines * Always wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE), including safety glasses, gloves, and lab coats or aprons. * Work in a well-ventilated area with proper fume extraction to avoid exposure to harmful vapors from plating solutions. * Use caution when handling chemicals and electrical equipment to prevent injury or damage to materials. * Follow local regulations regarding waste disposal for chemical solutions used during the electroplating process. Materials Needed ---------------- 1. **Nickel sulfate solution** (\[NiSO4 6H2O\]: at least 250 g/L) - provides the source of nickel ions for deposition. 2. **Boric acid** (\[H3BO3\]: 30-40 g/L) - acts as a buffer to maintain pH levels and stabilize the nickel plating process. 3. **Sodium lauryl sulfate** (SLS): 1-2 g/L - serves as a wetting agent to improve the distribution of nickel ions across the object being plated. 4. **Distilled water**: rinse agent between steps. 5. **Direct current power supply**: capable of delivering up to 5 amps per square foot of plating surface. 6. **Plating cell**: container designed to hold the plating solution and provide electrical contact points. 7. **Anode**: typically made of pure nickel, connected to the positive terminal of the DC power supply. 8. **Cathode**: where the nickel deposit occurs; usually the part you want to plate, connected to the negative terminal of the DC power supply. 9. **Alligator clips or other suitable connectors**: attach wires to the anode and cathode. 10. **Wire leads**: connect alligator clips to the power supply. 11. **Masking tape and plastic sheeting**: protect areas of the object you don't wish to plate. 1 And the mommy's going off into the house. Does she eat it with her mouth? And Raymond wanted a cheeseburger and a chocolate shake. " I put on my incredible hulk on. " yeah, because you never took me there. Home run! John Wilkes Booth (May 10, 1838 - April 26, 1865) was an American actor who shot and killed U.S. president Abraham Lincoln on April 14, 1865, in Washington, D.C. Lincoln died the next morning. Booth was born in Bel Air, Harford County, Maryland to English immigrant parents. He was a very well-known stage actor who supported the Confederacy during the American Civil War. He was angry with Lincoln for supporting voting rights for former slaves, and he hoped to rally the remaining Confederate troops to keep fighting the war, which was coming to an end. Booth was chased by United States soldiers and killed at a farm in Virginia 12 days after the assassination. Dressing stylish and cute in middle high school can be a fun and exciting way to express yourself and build confidence. By following these steps, you'll learn how to put together outfits that are both fashionable and age-appropriate. Step 1: Understand Your School's Dress Code Before you start planning your outfits, make sure you understand your school's dress code. Many schools have specific rules about what is and isn't allowed, so it's important to familiarize yourself with them to avoid any issues. Some common restrictions include no tank tops, short shorts, or clothing with offensive language or imagery. If you're unsure about something, don't hesitate to ask a teacher or administrator for clarification. Step 2: Take Inspiration from Fashion Bloggers and Magazines A great way to get ideas for outfits is by looking at fashion blogs and magazines. These resources often feature the latest trends and styles, which can serve as inspiration for your own looks. When browsing, pay attention to how different pieces are paired together and consider how you could recreate those combinations with items from your own closet. Step 3: Build a Versatile Wardrobe Having a versatile wardrobe will allow you to mix and match different pieces to create multiple outfits. Focus on building a foundation of basics such as t-shirts, jeans, skirts, and cardigans. From there, you can add in trendier pieces like statement jackets or patterned blouses. This approach will ensure that you always have something to wear, regardless of the occasion. Step 4: Accessorize Appropriately Accessories can take an outfit from good to great. However, it's important to keep things age-appropriate. Avoid anything too flashy or mature-looking, such as large hoop earrings or stiletto heels. Instead, opt for delicate jewelry, hair accessories, and comfortable shoes. Remember that less is usually more when it comes to accessorizing. Step 5: Consider Comfort and Practicality While it's important to look stylish, it's equally important to feel comfortable and confident in what you're wearing. Choose clothes that fit well and aren't overly tight or revealing. Additionally, think about the activities you'll be doing throughout the day - if you have gym class or plan on playing outside during recess, opt for sneakers and athleticwear instead of dresses and sandals. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Always prioritize comfort and practicality over fashion. There's no point in wearing an amazing outfit if you're constantly adjusting or feeling self-conscious. * Don't be afraid to experiment with different styles and trends. Fashion is all about personal expression, so find what works best for you and embrace it. * Invest in quality basics that will last longer than fast-fashion items. While cheaper clothes may seem appealing initially, they tend to fall apart quickly and won't provide the same level of longevity as higher-quality pieces. * Pay attention to fabric choices. Natural fibers like cotton and linen are breathable and comfortable, while synthetic materials can be hot and uncomfortable. * Keep your makeup and hairstyles simple and natural-looking. Save dramatic looks for special occasions or weekends when there's more flexibility in terms of dress codes. By following these steps and guidelines, you'll be able to dress stylishly and cutely in middle high school without compromising comfort or appropriateness. With a little practice and creativity, you'll soon develop your unique sense of style that reflects your personality and interests. The word Catholicism is often used to tell the difference between the beliefs of Catholic Christians and the beliefs of others called Protestant Christians. Catholic and Orthodox churches use church leaders, called bishops, to determine beliefs. Protestants, however, often use each member's own understanding of the Bible to determine beliefs. You can eat that when you come back. Come Up With a Manga Book Title =============================== Are you looking for a catchy and memorable title for your new manga book? A good title can grab potential readers' attention and give them an idea of what they can expect from your story. This wikiHow will guide you through the process of creating a captivating title that truly represents your work. ### Step 1: Understand Your Story *Estimated time needed: 30 minutes - 1 hour* Before crafting a title, take some time to understand the core elements of your manga. Consider the following questions: 1. What is the genre of your manga (e.g., action, romance, fantasy)? 2. Who are the main characters and what motivates them? 3. What themes or messages does your story convey? 4. Where and when does the story take place? 5. How would you describe the overall tone of your manga (lighthearted, dark, mysterious, etc.)? Understanding these aspects of your story will help you create a more fitting and meaningful title. Key tip: Jot down notes as you consider these questions, so you have ideas readily available during later steps. ### Step 2: Identify Key Elements Adam if you bring me my purse I'll see if I have one. Creating a Facebook account on your iPhone or iPad is an easy process that only takes a few minutes. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you get started: 1. **Open the App Store:** The first step is to download the Facebook app from the App Store. Tap on the App Store icon, which should be located on your home screen or in the app library. This will take you to the App Store where you can search for apps. 2. **Search for Facebook:** Once you are in the App Store, tap on the search icon (magnifying glass) at the bottom of the screen. Type "Facebook" into the search bar and hit enter. Look for the official Facebook app developed by Meta Platforms Inc., and make sure it has a blue background with a white 'f' logo. 3. **Install the Facebook App:** After finding the correct app, tap on the "Get" button next to the app name. You may need to authenticate this action using Face ID, Touch ID, or your Apple ID password. Wait for the installation to complete before moving onto the next step. 4. **Launch the Facebook App:** Locate the newly installed Facebook app on your device's home screen or app library and tap on its icon to open it. If prompted, allow Facebook to access your phone's features such as camera, microphone, and location services. These permissions enable certain functionalities within the app. 5. **Sign Up:** Upon opening the app, if you don't already have a Facebook account, you'll see a "Sign Up" option. Tap on it. Fill out all required fields including your full name, mobile number or email address, password, date of birth, and gender. Make sure to provide accurate information since some details cannot be changed later. 6. **Verify Your Email or Phone Number:** After filling out the sign-up form, Facebook sends a confirmation code either via text message or email depending on what method you chose during registration. Enter this verification code when asked to confirm your identity. 7. **Set up Profile Picture & Cover Photo:** Now that your account is active, customize your profile by adding a profile picture and cover photo. These pictures serve as visual identifiers so friends can recognize you more easily. To add these photos, tap on them within your profile page and choose between taking new ones or selecting existing ones from your photo album. 8. **Find Friends:** Next, start building your network by finding friends who are also on Facebook. You can do this manually by entering their names in the search bar provided at the top of the screen, or automatically through synchronizing contacts from your device. For automatic friend suggestions based on shared mutual connections, click on "People You May Know." 9. **Adjust Privacy Settings:** Before posting anything publicly, ensure your privacy settings align with your comfort level. Go to Menu > Settings & Privacy > Privacy Shortcuts > See More Privacy Settings. From here, manage various aspects like Who Can See My Stuff?, How People Find And Contact Me, etc. It's crucial to review these options thoroughly because they control who sees what you share online. 10. **Stay Safe Online:** Remember to practice safe browsing habits while using Facebook. Avoid clicking suspicious links, sharing sensitive personal info openly, and interacting with unknown profiles without caution. Regularly update your knowledge about security measures offered by Facebook too. By following these steps carefully, you've successfully created a Facebook account on your iPhone or iPad! Enjoy connecting with others, engaging with content, discovering businesses, and exploring groups relevant to your interests. The momentum equations are the equations that make it hard to solve problems in fluid dynamics. There are a number of different versions that include a number of different effects. The Navier-Stokes equations are momentum equations, and the Euler equations are the Navier-Stokes equations but with viscosity not included. There is one momentum equation in a 1D problem and three, one in each space direction, in 3D. Grizzly Bears live on the western side of North America where there are mountains and wide spaces. They are mostly located north of the United States, but some can be found within the lower 48 states. The males usually travel alone, but the females with their cubs. During the summer, however, 50 or 60 grizzlies gather at the falls of rushing rivers to catch salmons. From June to October, salmons swim up rivers from the Pacific Ocean to lay their eggs. At this time bears fatten up for the winter. They go where there is food. Grizzlies may fish in one river this month, but another the next month. They also travel to meadows where they find berries. When winter comes bears hibernate. They usually wait until the first snowfall before hiding in dens. Both males and females sleep for the winter. They don't wake up unless there are floods or if the days get warm. When days start to lengthen again, their biological clock tells them to get up. The grizzlies are brown. They have a keen nose to sniff the air to find food. There are 42 teeth. This includes sharp teeth for tearing meat, and flat molars for chewing plants. Each paw has 5 sharp claws. Grizzlies' furs have 2 sizes. Long hairs keep off insects and dirt while short hairs keep the bear warm. The brown color also camouflages them from their preys. Bears have a large hump behind the neck. This hump contains muscle to strengthen the two front legs. Underneath the skin is a 10-inch (25cm) thick layer of fat. They build up the fat during summer by feeding a lot. During hibernation they don't eat or drink but burn the fat as food. After each hibernation, they can lose up to 40% of their original weight. When they hibernated during winter, their whole body slows down and the temperature drops. The heart rate decreases from 50 beats per minute to about 8 beats/min. Their body temperature drops from 89 F (31.7 C) to just about 80 F (26.7 C). Since they are resting, grizzlies don't use up a lot of energy. After mating the females are ready to give birth during hibernation. The cubs are born in the dens. A mother usually has 2 cubs, rarely does she have 3 or 4. At birth the babies are only 1pound (.45kg) in weight and about 1 foot (30cm) in length. The cubs are blind and deaf. Their tiny bodies are covered with fine hair. They stay in the den with their mother for 3 months, until hibernation is over. During these 3 months they fatten up with their mother's nutritious milk. At soon as hibernation is over, they go in search of food. The young bears need nursing every 2 hours. By this time they are about 7 pounds (3.15kg). The mother is very protective of the cubs. She keeps them away from any potential dangers. When the cubs are a little more than a year old, they begin to feed on some solid food such as berries, nuts, roots, grasses, and meat. The mother teaches them how to survive in the wild. They learn how to hunt, and defend themselves. The cubs are ready to be on their own by the third year. The mother's milk starts to dry up. Her body is getting ready for the next pregnancy. The youngsters are on their own now. They will probably not see their mother any more. The cubs stay together for 2 or 3 years. By the time they are 6 or 7, grizzlies are ready to establish their territories. A grizzly usually lives up to an age of 20 in the wild. Grizzlies eat a variety of foods. They eat fish, especially salmons, young elks, deer, or caribou. Sometimes they eat carrion. They would steal it from coyotes, wolves, or other bears. Grizzlies also are plant eaters. They consume berries, roots, grass, and nuts. In 1975, the United States officially announced that Grizzly Bears were threatened. As population and tourism grow around bear country, more of their habitat is being taken away. With the loss of habitat, they are reduced to a small space. Their sources of food are also affected. When human lives are threatened, they would kill the bears. Others kill bears for the sport and the excitement. Still others hunt them down for their precious parts. Poachers kill bears for their paws, gallbladder, fur, and skin. They ship these parts to Asia where they are used as medicine. The most important thing, however, is to preserve their natural habitat. They chased the dog and got their toys back. They were happy and played together again. Most ants produce a new generation each year. During the species specific breeding period, new reproductives, winged males and females leave the colony in what is called a "nuptial flight". Typically, the males take flight before the females. Males then use visual cues to find a common mating ground, for example, a landmark such as a pine tree to which other males in the area converge. Males secrete a mating pheromone that females follow. Females of some species mate with just one male, but in some others they may mate with anywhere from one to ten or more different males. Mated females then seek a suitable place to begin a colony. There, they break off their wings and begin to lay and care for eggs. The females store the sperm they obtain during their nuptial flight to selectively fertilise future eggs. A Mobile Army Surgical Hospital (or M.A.S.H. or MASH) was a medical center used during wars, to care and fix injured soldiers who got hurt in the fighting. They were an alternative to field hospitals and real hospitals which were used in World War II. During the Korean War, thanks to these kinds of hospitals, soldiers had a 97% chance of surviving an injury. They were also used in the Vietnam War and the first Gulf War, but were gradually replaced by Combat Support Hospitals or CSHs which are used today. Joan of Arcadia was a CBS drama television series. Title: Exploring Special Functions - A Grade School Adventure! Have you ever wondered how mathematicians create and study new types of functions? In this adventure, we will explore a special kind of function and learn some cool techniques used by mathematicians! No need to worry about complex college subjects like electromagnetism or integration here; let's dive into something fun and accessible using concepts you already know! Imagine having two friends, Alice and Bob, who each have a collection of toys. They want to combine their toys so everyone has equal happiness. To do this, they assign numbers (scores) to their collections based on how happy they feel about them. Let's call these happiness scores 'functions'. Now, our goal is to understand if there's a way to fairly distribute all the toys between Alice and Bob while ensuring neither gets jealous of the other's share. In mathematical language, we are looking for a specific type of function called a 'quasi-convex' function. This fancy name means that no matter where Alice and Bob start combining their toy collections, every step along the way ensures that both remain equally happy or become happier. It also guarantees fairness since neither friend feels worse off than before when sharing toys. But wait! There's a catch. Mathematicians love finding unique examples without copying others', even when dealing with similar situations. So, imagine trying to discover whether there exists another friendly function just like ours - yet distinct enough not to be simply a mirror image through a growing glass (a 'monotone transformation'). Can you think of such a pair of friends and their magical happiness scoring system? Now, why did we add an extra rule requiring Alice and Bob's favorite toys to play a significant role? Well, sometimes these rules make problems more exciting and challenging, pushing us to dig deeper into understanding various properties of these functions. That's exactly what happened here! By adding this restriction, we ensure that the most cherished toys cannot be ignored during the distribution process. So, grab your thinking caps and embark on this thrilling journey full of twists, turns, and fascinating mathematical ideas! Remember, exploring questions and seeking answers is half the fun, so don't hesitate to ask questions and enjoy the ride! Come on let me see you do it. Mom XXX do you think these will stick together? You bumped your head on the sink, DI'n't you. Inyati, however, answered Amanzinina well, and said that I had nothing to do with this "bring near"; that it was made by white men, just as Caffres made assagies; that it was a thing which white men used everywhere; and that I, having seen men use it, knew what it was for, and that witchcraft had nothing to do with it. Now we want you to play ball you want to sit in the chair. Katie you height put a stewardess in there. Vocab In a Flash Make flash cards with words that give you problems. Write the word on one side, the definition on the back. Underneath the definition, write a sentence using the problem word in context. Hint: You're not going to learn the word if the sentence goes something like, "I don't know the word 'onerous'." Make sure the sentence gives clues about what the word means. For example: "Learning all these vocabulary words doesn't have to be an onerous task; in fact, I can make it pretty easy!" An Earful of Words English can be a tricky language. Just look at all the words that are spelled differently but sound exactly the same! Learn the difference between homophones: to, too, and two; write and right; there, their, and they're; accept and except. Make up reminders to help you tell the difference between the meanings as well as what meanings go with what spellings of the words. Here's one for the set of to, too, and two: - "To" is usually used when you go somewhere or do something, like running to the store or getting to the point. Just remember that to has the same number of letter as "do" and "go" - and substitute the "t" for either the "d" or the "g." - "Too" indicates "as well" or "more," like "Me, too!" or "There are too many vocabulary words!" The idea is that there's something extra, including and extra "o"! - "Two" is the spelling of the number 2. This is easy - ask yourself how many "v's" it takes to make the "w" in the middle of the word. The answer is, of course, 2 or "two". Get to the Root of It Most words are made up of mini words called roots that come from the Greek and Latin languages. These roots appear in the middle of lots of different words but always mean the same thing. For example, the root "spec" means "to look at." Now think about all the words that have "spec" somewhere in it: - A spectator is someone who watches something, like an event. All those people at your little sister's dance recital are spectators. - When you inspect an object, you're looking very closely at it. And inspectors examine evidence when they're trying to solve a mystery. - Respecting another person means that you look at him with admiration. - A spectacle is an exhibition that people look at with great interest. - Speculation means looking at something you don't know a lot about and guessing its meaning. So basically, every time you speak these words, you're using a little bit of Greek or Latin. Pretty cool, huh? It's All in the Details Try to pinpoint the best word to use when you describe something. Think about specific colors, whether a common word works for a situation, or how something makes you feel. Is the chair best described as red, or is it more a like a deep maroon? Was it simply a good day because you got picked first for soccer, or was it fantastic because you also got an A+ on your math homework, your crush talked to you during lunch, and the weather was perfect for a bike ride after school? Did your brother simply make you mad, or are you furious about the mess he made in your room? Picking the word that's "just right" will help you write better and improve your vocabulary. Pick a Word of the Day Open the dictionary, close your eyes, and point to a word on the page. Whatever you pick becomes the word of the day. Once you know the definition of that word, use it at least once in conversation during the day. Your teacher will be in awe when you tell her you're disgruntled that the cafeteria ran out of pizza at lunch (it means you're not happy about it, annoyed, or displeased). If you don't want to pick the word yourself, there are other ways to get your word of the day. Bookstores often sell tear-off calendars with a different word for every day of the week. There's even Word of the Day toilet paper! Who knew that something like learning could be so fun? Word scrambles, Mad-Libs, crossword puzzles, and word-find games all help expand vocabulary, believe it or not. Board games like Scrabble, Boggle, and Scattergories also offer some pretty entertaining ways to discover new words. It's very simple: The more you read, the more you'll build up your vocabulary. So pick up a book today and work on advancing your reading level. Recommended Products for Your Child Ages 8-10 Tell me the story that goes with these pictures. Becoming best friends with someone on Animal Jam takes time, effort, and genuine care for the other person. By following these steps, you can build a strong friendship that will last on Animal Jam. **Step 1: Create an Account and Choose Your Animal** Before you can start making friends on Animal Jama, you need to create an account and choose your animal. Pick an animal that represents who you are or who you want to be in the game. This is the first impression others will have of you, so make sure it's a good one! Key Tips and Guidelines: -Choose an animal that reflects your personality. -Make sure your username is appropriate and easy to remember. -Create a strong password and keep it safe. **Step 2: Customize Your Character** Once you have chosen your animal, it's time to customize your character. You can change its color, add patterns, and accessorize with clothes and items from the store. A well-designed character will attract more people to talk to you. Key Tips and Guidelines: -Use colors and patterns that stand out. -Accessorize with items that reflect your interests. -Keep your outfit age-appropriate and respectful. **Step 3: Join a Den or Community** To meet new people, join a den or community where players gather and chat. Here, you can find like-minded individuals and strike up conversations about shared interests. Key Tips and Guidelines: -Join dens and communities related to your hobbies or favorite topics. -Follow all rules and regulations set by the den owners and moderators. -Respect everyone's opinions and avoid arguments. **Step 4: Start Conversations** Don't wait for others to approach you; take initiative and begin conversations yourself. Ask open-ended questions and show genuine interest in getting to know them better. Key Tips and Guidelines: -Ask questions relevant to their current activity or appearance. -Share information about yourself without oversharing personal details. -Listen actively and respond thoughtfully. **Step 5: Engage in Activities Together** Participating in activities together strengthens bonds between players. Attend parties, play mini-games, go on adventures, and trade rare items as tokens of friendship. Key Tips and Guidelines: -Organize events for you and your friends to enjoy. -Trade fairly and responsibly; don't scam or cheat others. -Show appreciation for each other through gifts and kind gestures. **Step 6: Build Trust** Building trust takes time, but being honest and consistent goes a long way. Share stories, confide secrets (when appropriate), and support each other during challenging times. Key Tips and Guidelines: -Never break promises or lie to your friend. -Stand up for your friend when they aren't around. -Encourage positive behavior and discourage negative actions. **Step 7: Communicate Regularly** Stay in touch with your friend both inside and outside the game. Send messages, write letters, or arrange voice chats if allowed by parents or guardians. Consistent communication fosters stronger relationships. Key Tips and Guidelines: -Always obtain parental permission before communicating outside the game. -Set boundaries regarding what subjects are appropriate to discuss. -Limit sharing personal contact information such as email addresses or phone numbers. By following these steps, you'll be well on your way to becoming best friends on Animal Jam. Remember, building lasting friendships requires patience, understanding, and mutual respect. Avoiding Common Errors This worksheet discusses the differences between adjectives and adverbs. It defines adjectives and adverbs, shows what each can do, and offers several examples of each in use. Click here for some examples. Contributors:Paul Lynch, Allen Brizee Last Edited: 2011-02-02 03:58:26 - Bad or Badly? When you want to describe how you feel, you should use an adjective So you'd say, "I feel bad." Saying "I feel badly" would be like saying you play football badly. "I feel badly" would mean that you are unable to feel, as though your hands were numb. Here are some other examples: - "The dog smells badly." Here, badly means that the dog does not do a good job of smelling. - "The dog smells bad." Here, "bad" means that dog needs a bath. N.B. Sometimes people say "I feel badly" when they feel that they have done something wrong. Let's say you dropped your friend's favorite dish, and it broke into a million pieces. You might say, "I feel really badly about what happened." - Good or Well? Good is an adjective, so you do not do good or live good, but you do well and live well. Remember, though, that an adjective follows sense-verbs and be-verbs, so you also feel good, look good, smell good, are good, have been good, etc. So: "My mother looks good." This does not mean that she has good eyesight; it means that she appears healthy. "I feel really good today." Again, this does not mean that I touch things successfully. It means rather that I am happy or healthy. N.B. Many people confuse this distinction in conversation, and that's okay. You will hear people say, "I feel well" when they mean that they feel good. However, if you're talking about action verbs, you'd say "well." "I did well on my exam." "She plays tennis well." - Sure or Surely? Sure is an adjective, and surely is an adverb. For example: - "He is sure about his answer." Sure describes he. - "The Senator spoke out surely." Here, surely describes how the senator spoke. N.B. Surely can also be used as a sentence-adverb. For example, "Surely, you're joking." Here, surely describes the entire sentence "you're joking." The sentence more or less means, "You must be joking." - Near or Nearly? Near can function as a verb, adverb, adjective, or preposition. Nearly is used as an adverb to mean "in a close manner" or "almost but not quite." Here are some examples that demonstrate the differences between various uses of near and nearly. - "I'll be seeing you in the near future." Here, near describes the noun "future." - "The cat crept near." Near is an adverb that describes where the cat crept. - "Don't worry; we're nearly there." Here, nearly describes how close we are. Near can also be used as a verb and a preposition. - "My graduation neared." Here, neared is the verb of the sentence. - "I want the couch near the window." Near is a preposition at the head of the phrase "near the window." This done, we turned to other work. "The Minnesota lay aground in the North Channel. To her aid hurrying up from Old Point came the Roanoke and the Saint Lawrence. Our own batteries at Sewell's Point opened upon these two ships as they passed, and they answered with broadsides. We fed our engines, and under a billow of black smoke ran down to the Minnesota. Once upon a time, there was a clumsy mole who lived in a big field. He was a very clumsy mole, and he was always tripping over his feet. One day, the mole's best friend, a squirrel, came over for a visit. The mole was excited and asked his friend, "What should we do?" The squirrel answered, "Let's cover things with leaves!" The mole thought it was a great idea, so they spent the day covering things like rocks, trees, and even a hole in the ground with colorful leaves. When they were done, the mole was tired and said, "This is fun! Let's do it again!" The squirrel laughed and said, "Sure, let's do it tomorrow!" The End. If you answer this one you're finished. Suddenly, he saw a big, fierce bird flying towards him. Buzz quickly flew back to the hive and told Buzzy what happened. Buzzy said, "I told you it's dangerous outside, Buzz. You need to be careful." Buzz realized that Buzzy was right and promised to be more careful in the future. From that day on, Buzz stayed inside the hive and never ventured outside again. And what are you going to put in? Be able to instead of can and could - He is able to do it on his own. (= He can do it on his own.) - The watchman was able to catch the thief. (= The watchman could catch the thief.) - She was able to solve the problem. (= She could solve the problem.) Be to instead of will/shall - He is to retire next year. (= He will retire next year.) - We are to have a test in English next week. (= We will have a test in English next week.) The structure 'be to' indicates simple future, like will or shall, but with a slight degree of uncertainty. The sentence 'He is to retire' means that 'He is likely to retire'. It is not quiet certain whether he will actually retire or not. Be to may also express a stronger meaning, almost like must, but it is not quite strong or blunt. - You are to take this medicine whether you like it or not. Had better instead of should or ought to - You had better consult a doctor. (= You should consult a doctor.) Had better may also express a threat. - He had better mend his ways. Have to and have got to instead of must - I have to report for duty within a week. (= I must report for duty within a week.) - I have got to talk to her. (= I have to/must talk to her.) - Did you have to do that? (was that necessary?) Have got to means the same as have to in most situations. It is more common in an informal style. People who use profanity do not always mean to make anybody feel bad, and tolerance for different forms of profanity can vary widely, from person to person. Most often, using profanity is a verbal outlet for strong feelings (usually unpleasant ones), that might otherwise cause a physical reaction. At other times, some people may use profanity as humor. He was not very friendly. Lily felt sad and angry. She wanted to have a car too. She thought of a plan. She saw a bottle on the table. It had a label that said "shrink". She did not know what that word meant, but she thought it might make things smaller. She took the bottle and sprayed some of it on Ben's car. Ben saw what Lily did and shouted. "Hey, what are you doing? Stop it! You are making my car shrink!" Lily laughed. She thought it was funny to see Ben's car get smaller and smaller. She picked up the tiny car and put it in her pocket. She said, "Now I have a car too. And you have nothing. Ha ha!" Ben was very upset. He cried and ran to his mom. He told her what Lily did. Mom was not happy. She scolded Lily and made her give back the car. She said, "Lily, that was not nice. You should not take things that are not yours. And you should not use things that you do not understand. You could have hurt Ben or yourself. You need to say sorry to Ben and share your toys." Lily felt sorry. She realized she was wrong. She gave back the car to Ben and said, "I'm sorry, Ben. I was mean. I should not have sprayed your car. Can we be friends again?" Ben was still sad, but he forgave Lily. He said, "It's okay, Lily. I'm sorry too. I should have been more friendly. Anna and Tom were going on a big trip with Mom and Dad. They were very happy and excited. They had to pack their bags with clothes and toys and books. Mom and Dad helped them pack. They also gave them a passport each. A passport is a book with your name and picture that you need to go on a plane. "Look at your passport, Anna. It is very pretty. You are a big girl now. I am very proud of you," Mom said. "Thank you, Mom. I like my passport. Can I hold it?" Anna asked. "Sure, but be careful. Don't lose it or drop it. You need it to go on the plane," Mom said. Anna nodded and held her passport tight. She wanted to show it to Tom. She ran to his room, where he was packing his bag. She opened the door and said, "Look, Tom. I have a passport. Do you have one too?" Tom looked up and saw Anna holding her passport. He was jealous. He wanted to have a passport too. He did not have one yet. Mom and Dad said he was too little. He was angry and sad. He said, "No, I don't have a passport. You are lucky. You get to go on the plane. I have to stay home with Grandma. It is not fair. Give me your passport. I want it." He grabbed Anna's passport and tried to take it from her. Anna did not want to give it to him. She said, "No, Tom. It is mine. You can't have it. Let go." They pulled and tugged and fought over the passport. They did not hear Mom and Dad coming. They did not see the scissors on the floor. They did not notice the passport tearing in half. Mom and Dad saw what happened. They were very angry and sad. They said, "Oh no. What have you done? You ruined Anna's passport. Now she can't go on the plane. Now we have to cancel the trip. Now we have to stay home. You are very naughty and mean. We are very disappointed in you." Anna and Tom started to cry. They were very sorry and sad. They wished they had not fought over the passport. They wished they had been nice and kind. They wished they could go on the big trip. But it was too late. The passport was broken. The trip was over. Title: How to Enable Dark Mode in Windows 10 Introduction: Dark mode is a display setting that uses darker colors for windows, menus, and taskbars, which can help reduce eye strain and improve visibility, especially when using your computer in low-light conditions. This tutorial will guide you through enabling dark mode in Windows 10 with clear, detailed steps and helpful tips along the way. Step 1: Access Settings Click on the Start button (Windows icon) located at the bottom left corner of your screen or press the Windows key on your keyboard. Once the Start Menu appears, click on the gear icon labeled as "Settings." If you cannot find the gear icon, type "settings" into the search bar at the bottom of the Start Menu and select the corresponding app from the results. Key tip: The Settings app allows users to customize various aspects of their Windows experience. Exploring its options can lead to discovering new features and improving overall usability. Step 2: Navigate to Personalization Options In the Settings window, look for the "Personalization" category and click on it. It should be listed among other categories such as System, Devices, Network & Internet, etc., towards the top left side of the window. Guideline: Always familiarize yourself with different sections within settings apps or software interfaces to make navigation easier and more efficient over time. Step 3: Select Colors from the Left Sidebar Once you are in the Personalization section, locate and click on "Colors" from the list of available subcategories displayed on the left sidebar. Step 4: Choose Dark under "Choose your default app mode" After clicking on "Colors," scroll down until you see the heading "Choose your default app mode." Here, there are two radio buttons - one for Light and another for Dark. Click on the circle next to "Dark" to enable this option. Explanation: By selecting the Dark radio button, you're instructing Windows 10 to utilize dark color schemes across supported applications. However, please note that some legacy programs may still retain their original appearance even after switching to dark mode. Step 5: Confirm Changes by Scrolling Up and Checking the Preview Pane Scroll back up to ensure that the changes have been applied correctly. On the right pane, you will notice a preview area showing what your selected theme looks like. Make sure it displays a predominantly black background. Additional Tip: To further enhance readability while using dark mode, consider increasing text size slightly. Go to Ease of Access > Display > Make text bigger and adjust the slider accordingly. Conclusion: Congratulations! You have successfully enabled dark mode in Windows 10. Enjoy improved visual comfort and reduced glare during late-night computing sessions. Don't forget to explore other personalization options to tailor your Windows experience further. She's takin' a bath looked there see her take her bath? "The things that draw young people to places of amusement are not at first gross things. Take the most notorious public place in Paris,--the Jardin Mabille, for instance,--and the things which give it its first charm are all innocent and artistic. Exquisite beds of lilies, roses, gillyflowers, lighted with jets of gas so artfully as to make every flower translucent as a gem; fountains where the gaslight streams out from behind misty wreaths of falling water and calla-blossoms; sofas of velvet turf, canopied with fragrant honeysuckle; dim bowers overarched with lilacs and roses; a dancing-ground under trees whose branches bend with a fruitage of many-colored lamps; enchanting music and graceful motion; in all these there is not only no sin, but they are really beautiful and desirable; and if they were only used on the side and in the service of virtue and religion, if they were contrived and kept up by the guardians and instructors of youth, instead of by those whose interest it is to demoralize and destroy, young people would have no temptation to stray into the haunts of vice. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/Interviews/07/5929LD.cha = = = You don't drink out of a bottle [child moving toys].
