Title: How to Delete Your Story on Instagram Using an iPhone or iPad Instagram is a popular social media platform that allows users to share photos and videos as stories which disappear after 24 hours. However, there may be instances when you want to delete your story before the 24-hour period expires. This tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting your Instagram story using an iPhone or iPad, providing clear, detailed, and practical steps with helpful explanations along the way. 1. Launch the Instagram app on your iPhone or iPad. The Instagram icon looks like a multicolored camera lens. If you haven't logged into your account recently, enter your username (or email address) and password when prompted. 2. Navigate to your profile by tapping the 'person' icon at the bottom right corner of the screen. Accessing your own profile provides an overview of all your recent activities, including uploaded posts and shared stories. 3. Locate and open your current active story. Stories are displayed in circles at the top of your profile page. They represent either a still photo, a video, or a collection of multiple pieces of content known as a "story highlights." Tap on any circle representing one of your recent stories. This action opens your story, preparing it for potential removal if necessary. Key tip: Stories appear in reverse chronological order - meaning newer ones show up first. To find older stories, tap on the circular profile picture located left of the current active story. Keep swiping left until you reach the desired story. 4. Once your chosen story has fully loaded, look for three small horizontal dots (also called an ellipsis menu) located at the bottom right corner of the screen. These dots indicate additional options related to the specific piece of content. Go ahead and give them a gentle tap! 5. A contextual popup menu should now be visible. Among various choices, select the red option labeled 'Delete.' It might seem intimidating given its color, but fear not; this button serves merely as a confirmation tool ensuring you truly intend to remove the story from your profile. 6. Confirm your decision by tapping 'Delete' again when asked, 'Do you want to delete this story?' After confirming the deletion, two things happen simultaneously: - Firstly, your story vanishes instantly from public view, no longer accessible to friends, followers, or random passersby. - Secondly, a message appears briefly saying, 'Story deleted successfully,' reinforcing the fact that your content is indeed gone forever. Congratulations! You have successfully removed your unwanted Instagram story. Remember, once a story is deleted, it cannot be retrieved. So always double-check your intentions before hitting that big red button. Happy sharing, editing, and managing your digital presence responsibly! yeah, there's not too many of them. ah, here's some honey they said, and they took it to the giant. Title: How to Evolve Glalie in Pok mon Games Pok mon is a popular role-playing game series developed by Nintendo, where players collect, train, battle, and evolve various creatures called Pok mon. One such Pok mon is Glalie, an Ice-type Pok mon that can be found in several games within the franchise. This article will guide you through the process of evolving Glalie, providing clear, concise steps along with helpful tips and guidelines. Step 1: Catch a Snorunt To start your journey towards obtaining a Glalie, first, you need to catch its pre-evolved form - Snorunt. You can find Snorunt in colder regions or areas within select Pok mon games like Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, Omega Ruby, Alpha Sapphire, Diamond, Pearl, Platinum, Black, White, Black 2, White 2, X, Y, Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon. The encounter rate may vary depending upon the game version and location. Utilize Repel items to avoid unnecessary battles and focus on finding this specific Pok mon. Key Tips: * Be prepared with suitable Pok Balls (e.g., Great Ball, Ultra Ball) and status-inducing moves (e.g., Sleep Powder, Thunder Wave) to increase the chances of capturing Snorunt. * Check the wild Pok mon's level; if it's too high, try lowering its health using other Pok mon before engaging in battle. Step 2: Level Up Your Snorunt Once you have caught a Snorunt, train it until it reaches level 42. At this point, Snorunt will undergo evolution and transform into Glalie. To gain experience points efficiently, participate in trainer battles or engage wild Pok mon in combat while ensuring your Snorunt survives. Keep track of Snorunt's current level using the summary screen accessible via the party menu. Key Guidelines: * Grinding levels might take time, so patience is crucial during this stage. * Make sure your SnorUNT participates in most fights to maximize gained experience points. * Use Rare Candies sparingly as they provide instant level up but are rare resources better saved for later stages. Step 3: Verify Evolution After reaching level 42, Snorunt should automatically evolve into Glalie. However, double-check by opening the party menu and viewing Snorunt's details. If successful, you should see "Glalie" displayed instead of "Snorunt." Congratulations! You now own a fully evolved Glalie. Additional Information & Precautions: * Remember that some factors like happiness, stones, or special conditions may affect evolution processes in certain games. For Glalie, none of these additional requirements exist, making the evolution straightforward. * Always save your progress before attempting evolution to prevent losing any valuable data due to unexpected errors or accidents. * Lastly, enjoy training and battling with your new Glalie. Experiment with different movesets, abilities, and strategies to optimize its performance in competitions or casual playthroughs. After we put the XXX in can I XXX? Title: How to Date a Boy Who Is Unfaithful Disclaimer: This article does not endorse or encourage dating someone who is unfaithful. It aims to provide guidance for those already in such relationships, offering ways to cope with the challenges that come with it. However, we strongly advise seeking healthier relationship options. Introduction: Dating a boy who is unfaithful can be an emotional rollercoaster filled with heartbreaks and confusion. Despite his infidelities, some individuals may still want to continue the relationship due to various reasons like love, shared history, children involved, etc. If you find yourself in this situation, here's our comprehensive guide on navigating through it. Remember, every person and relationship is unique; therefore, adapt these steps as necessary. Step 1: Understand Why You Want To Stay Before proceeding further, reflect upon your feelings and understand why you are willing to stay despite his unfaithfulness. Are you hoping he will change? Does the good outweigh the bad for you? Knowing your motivations can help you make informed decisions moving forward. Key Tip: Be honest with yourself about what you're prepared to deal with emotionally. Step 2: Open Communication Lines Have an open conversation about his infidelity. Express your concerns and emotions without blaming or accusing him. Listen carefully to his responses and try to empathize with his perspective. Guideline: Use "I" statements instead of "you" statements to avoid sounding accusatory (e.g., say "I felt hurt when..." rather than "You made me feel...). Step 3: Seek Professional Help Consider couples therapy or counseling to work through trust issues and learn communication strategies. A professional mediator can offer impartial advice and tools to manage conflicts productively. Key Tip: Don't hesitate to seek individual therapy too if needed - understanding your own reactions and coping mechanisms is crucial. Step 4: Establish Boundaries & Expectations Clearly define what behaviors are acceptable and unacceptable in your relationship going forward. For instance, insist on transparency regarding whereabouts, contacts, etc. Discuss consequences if boundaries are crossed again. Guideline: Make sure these expectations are realistic and mutually agreed-upon to prevent future disagreements. Step 5: Rebuild Trust Gradually Trust takes time to rebuild after infidelity. Demonstrate reliability consistently by keeping promises, being truthful, and showing genuine remorse. Over time, small actions can contribute to restoring faith. Key Tip: Patience is vital during this process; remember rebuilding trust won't happen overnight. Step 6: Practice Self-care Be kind to yourself throughout this challenging journey. Engage in activities that bring joy and relaxation. Maintain friendships and support networks outside of the relationship. Guideline: Avoid isolating yourself; talking openly about your experiences with trusted friends or family members can alleviate stress. Step 7: Monitor Progress Regularly check in with yourself and assess whether progress is being made towards healing and regaining trust. Keep track of improvements and setbacks. Key Tip: Regular reassessment allows adjustments to be made if things aren't working as planned. Conclusion: While dating a boy who is unfaithful presents numerous obstacles, following these steps thoughtfully can facilitate dealing with them effectively. Ultimately, though, only you can decide whether staying in such a relationship aligns with your personal values and wellbeing. Remember you USTA drink out a bottle too, huh? Weak & Strong Both weak and strong verbs were used in Old English. The past and past participles of weak verbs were usually formed by adding dental suffixes to the present tense stem (as in the Modern English 'laugh', 'laughed', 'laughed'). For the strong verbs, the stem vowels are changed (as in 'sing', 'sang', 'sung'). The strong Anglo-Saxon verbs still in use today are easily recognized by the vowel changes. (They also make English difficult to learn as a foreign language!) They are somewhat complicated by the seven different classes of vowel gradations that were used. Of these, some were regular and some were not. The following are examples of each class still in use: Class 1: 'shine' Class 2: 'creep' Class 3a: 'thresh' Class 3b: 'fight' Class 3c: 'help' (archaic past tense 'holp') Class 3d: 'yield' Class 3e: 'sing' Class 4: 'bear' Class 5: 'see' Class 6: 'slay' Class 7a: 'know' Class 7b: 'fall' Some weak verbs also are still in use, as well. Most verbs created since the Anglo-Saxon period, as well as those borrowed from other languages, are treated as weak. A few of them also changed their stem vowels in the past and past participle. Notable examples of these exceptions still in use include 'seek', 'sell', 'think', 'bring', 'think', and 'buy'. The important verb ' to be' is anomalous. The only two tenses of Old English verbs were present and past (preterite). Indicative, subjunctive, and imperative moods were used. The subjunctive mood has survived primarily with the verb " to be" Present and past participles, as well as two infinitives (one without 'to'; the other inflected and with "to') were also used. Singular and plural verbs were used. Verbs in three persons were used in the singular of the present and past indicative. All plurals and the subjunctives were the same throughout. With but one exception, only the active voice was used in Old English. Then he showed them their mistake, and they applauded the justice of the execution. MORAL. On a fine morning in May, two Bees set forward in quest of honey; the one, wise and temperate; the other, careless and extravagant. They soon arrived at a garden enriched with aromatic herbs, the most fragrant flowers, and the most delicious fruits. They tried to find the woodchuck, but lost their way, and wandered deeper into the great forest till they came to a rocky place and could go no farther. They climbed up and tumbled down, turned back and went round, looked at the sun and knew it was late, chewed sassafras bark and checkerberry leaves for supper, and grew more and more worried and tired as hour after hour went by and they saw no end to woods and rocks. Once or twice they heard the hunter's gun far away, and called and tried to find him. Tommy scolded Billy for not going with the man, who knew his way and was probably safe in the valley when the last faint shot came up to them. Billy cried, and reproached Tommy for proposing to run away; and both felt very homesick for their mothers and their good safe beds at Farmer Mullin's. Elvis Presley was born on January 8, 1935 in Tupelo, Mississippi to 18-year-old Vernon Elvis Presley and 22-year-old Gladys Love Presley (born Smith), in the two-room shotgun house built by Vernon's father in preparation for the child's birth. Jesse Garon Presley, his identical twin brother, was delivered stillborn 35 minutes before him. As an only child, Presley became close to both parents and formed an unusually tight bond with his mother. The family attended an Assembly of God church, where he found his initial musical inspiration. Presley's ancestry was primarily a Western European mix: on his mother's side, he was Scots-Irish, with some French Norman; one of Gladys' great-great-grandmothers was Cherokee. According to a third cousin of Presley's, one of Gladys's great-grandmothers was Jewish. There is no evidence that Presley or his mother shared this belief in a Jewish heritage. Syndicated columnist Nate Bloom has challenged the cousin's account, which he calls a "tall tale". Presley's father's forebears were of Scottish and German ancestry. Gladys was regarded by relatives and friends as the dominant member of the small family. Vernon moved from one odd job to the next, evidencing little ambition. The family often relied on help from neighbors and government food assistance. The Presleys survived the F5 tornado in the 1936 Tupelo-Gainesville tornado outbreak. In 1938, they lost their home after Vernon was found guilty of altering a check written by the landowner. He was jailed for eight months, and Gladys and Elvis moved in with relatives. He moved to Memphis, Tennessee with his family when he was 13 years old. Presley was one of the first musicians to play a type of music called rockabilly, which combined country music with rhythm and blues. His first No.1 hit, "Heartbreak Hotel", from 1955, and was one of the first popular rock and roll songs. Do you wanna play with the baby or animals? Open the Google Play Store app on your Android device. This pre-installed app allows you to browse, download, and install apps from the official marketplace. By opening this app, you will have access to Ask My Buddy and other useful tools. 2. Tap on the search bar at the top of the screen and type in "Ask My Buddy" or "Evi". As you begin typing, related suggestions should appear below the search bar. Selecting one of these may speed up the process. Once you've entered the full name, tap 'Search'. 3. Look for the correct app among the results displayed. The developer name for Ask My Buddy is "Speechbridge." Make sure the logo matches too - it features a blue circle with a white letter 'B' inside. After finding the right app, tap on its title or icon to proceed to its store page. 4. Familiarize yourself with the app details before installation. On the app's page, read through the description, user reviews, screenshots, and additional information provided. Pay attention to any permissions requested, such as access to your contacts or microphone, so you know what data the app can handle. 5. When ready, press the green 'Install' button found near the top of the page. A popup window will then ask for confirmation; if comfortable with granting necessary permissions, select 'Accept'. Downloading might take several moments depending on your internet connection speed. 6. Wait until the installation completes automatically after downloading. You can monitor progress via the spinning animation next to the 'Installing' text under the app's title. Once complete, the word 'Installed' will replace the previous message, accompanied by an 'Open' option. 7. Press 'Open' to launch Ask My Buddy directly from the Play Store, or locate it within your app drawer or home screen later. If you chose not to open it immediately, find it amongst your other apps instead. 8. Set up your account once launched. To get started, you must sign up using either Facebook, Google, or email registration. Follow the prompts to provide required info like name and contact methods (email, phone number). Completing this step enables sending alerts when needed. 9. Customize your alert settings based on preference and necessity. Within the app, configure who receives alerts - family members, friends, emergency services, etc. Also specify whether alerts are sent via SMS, voice call, tweet, or all three simultaneously. 10. Test the system thoroughly post-setup. Before relying solely on Ask My Buddy during critical situations, ensure everything works correctly. Trigger test alerts and verify receipt across selected communication channels. Adjust settings accordingly if issues arise. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Regularly update the app to benefit from bug fixes, improvements, and new features. Check for updates manually periodically or enable automatic updates through your device's settings. * Keep personal information secure by only sharing essential details with trusted individuals. Limit sensitive data exposure where possible. * Explore available help resources within the app itself or online to maximize usage and understanding. * Always double-check recipients and messages prior to sending alerts to avoid confusion or false alarms. The Phoenicians spoke a Semitic language, usually called Canaanite. The Semitic group of languages includes Arabic, Maltese, Hebrew and also Aramaic, the language spoken by Jesus. We do not know much about how the alphabetic idea arose, but the Phoenicians, a trading people, came up with letters which were adapted by the early Greeks to produce their alphabet. The one big difference is that the Phoenician script had no pure vowels. Arabic script has vowels which may, or may not, be shown by diacritics (small marks above or below the line). [MOT hands the ball magnets for Chi to try on the big magnet] Filtering unwanted email quickly and safely is an essential skill for managing your inbox and improving productivity. This tutorial will guide you through setting up filters in Mozilla Thunderbird, a popular open-source email client. By following these steps, Thunderbird will automatically filter incoming messages based on specific criteria, helping you maintain a cleaner and more organized inbox. **Table of Contents:** 1. Introduction 2. Understanding Filters 3. Creating a New Filter 4. Setting Filter Criteria 5. Performing Actions on Filtered Emails 6. Testing Your Filter 7. Saving and Applying the Filter 8. Managing Existing Filters 9. Best Practices and Key Tips 10. Conclusion --- **1. Introduction** Email clients like Thunderbird provide users with powerful tools to manage their emails effectively. One such tool is the ability to create customizable filters that can sort, categorize, or delete incoming mail according to user-defined rules. In this tutorial, we'll learn how to: * Create new filters in Thunderbird * Set filter criteria * Define actions to perform on filtered emails * Save and apply filters to future incoming messages --- **2. Understanding Filters** A *filter* in Thunderbird is essentially a set of predetermined conditions (criteria) matched against incoming emails. If an email matches any condition(s), specified action(s) are executed automatically by the software. For example, if you want all newsletters from a particular sender to be moved into a separate folder named 'Newsletters,' you would define such a rule using filters. --- **3. Creating a New Filter** To begin creating a new filter, click the menu button at the top right corner of the window, then select **Message Filters...**. Alternatively, press `Ctrl + Shift + F` (Windows/Linux) or `Cmd + Opt + F` (Mac). This opens the Message Filters dialog box where existing filters may already reside. Click on the **New ** button located near the bottom left side of the dialog box to start configuring a brand new filter. ![Creating a new message filter](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/e/eb/Filter-Unwanted-Email-Quickly-and-Safely-in-Thunderbird-Step-3-Version-2.jpg/aid1193250-v2- She got a boo, boo didn't she? Creating an imaginary mind palace is a powerful technique that can help you remember information more effectively by associating it with specific locations within a mental construct of a building or environment. This method has been used for centuries by memory champions and scholars, including ancient Greek philosopher Simonides and Sherlock Holmes in Sir Arthur Conan Doyle's stories. Here's a step-by-step guide to creating your own mind palace: ### Step 1: Choose Your Mind Palace Location First, select a familiar location that you know well, such as your childhood home, current residence, or a frequently visited place like a school or office. The reason for choosing a familiar location is that it will be easier to navigate mentally and visualize details vividly. Key tip: Select a complex location with multiple rooms or areas to store more memories. ### Step 2: Familiarize Yourself With the Layout Spend time walking through your chosen location in your mind, paying close attention to every detail. Note architectural features, furniture placements, colors, textures, and any other distinctive elements. Mentally take note of entryways, hallways, stairs, doors, windows, and distinct objects. As you become increasingly comfortable navigating this space, you will find it easier to associate new memories with different parts of your mind palace. ### Step 3: Create Memory Stations Identify strategic points within your mind palace where you can store memories. These could be corners, walls, pieces of furniture, or unique items. Assign each station a specific category or topic, making it simpler to recall related information when needed. For example, if your mind palace is your childhood home, designate the living room for general knowledge, the kitchen for historical events, the bedroom for literature, and so forth. Key guideline: Make sure there are enough stations to accommodate all the information you wish to memorize. ### Step 4: Associate Memories With Objects Now it's time to start placing memories into your mind palace. To do this, choose an object at one of your designated memory stations and create a strong, memorable image related to the piece of information you want to remember. For instance, if you're trying to memorize the capital city of France (Paris), imagine a large Eiffel Tower made of flaming sparklers on top of your coffee table. Use exaggeration, humor, and vivid imagery to make these associations as striking as possible. ### Step 5: Reinforcement and Practice To ensure that your mind palace remains effective over time, regularly revisit each memory station and reinforce the connections between objects and information. Practicing retrieval-the act of deliberately attempting to bring stored memories back into consciousness-will strengthen neural pathways and improve long-term retention. You can also expand your mind palace by adding new memory stations and associations as needed. Key Tips for Successful Mind Palace Creation: 1. Be creative: Utilize exaggerated, humorous, or bizarre images to create stronger associations. 2. Visualize clearly: The more vividly you can picture each element of your mind palace, the easier it will be to recall information. 3. Space out practice: Instead of cramming all your studying into one session, review your mind palace periodically over an extended period. 4. Connect concepts: When possible, link related ideas together within your mind palace to form chains of association. 5. Personalize your palaces: Tailor your mind palaces to suit your interests and hobbies, making the learning process enjoyable and engaging. Title: Draw a Cat Face - A Comprehensive Step-by-Step Guide Introduction: Drawing a cat face can be both fun and rewarding! This guide will walk you through every detail necessary to create an adorable feline friend using just paper and pencil. We'll start with simple shapes before gradually adding more complex features. By following these steps carefully, even beginners should find this task achievable. Materials Needed: 1. Paper (smooth surface recommended) 2. Pencil (soft lead such as 2B or 4B is ideal) 3. Eraser Estimated Time Required: Approximately 30 minutes to one hour depending on your skill level and speed. Step 1: Outline The Basic Shape Start by drawing an oval shape towards the top half of your page. This forms the basic structure of the cat's head. It doesn't have to be perfect; slight irregularities add character! Step 2: Mark Key Features Divide the oval vertically into three equal parts. Make two marks along the horizontal midpoint - one near the top for the forehead, another closer to the bottom for the nose bridge. These guides help position eyes and nose accurately. Step 3: Sketch Eyes At about two-thirds up from the bottom line, draw two almond shapes leaning slightly inward. They represent the upper eyelids. Add smaller curves within them to indicate lower lids. Remember, cats' eyes are usually narrower than human ones. Step 4: Refine Eye Details Within each eye, sketch a small circle intersected by a vertical line - these are the pupils surrounded by reflections. Also, add tiny curved lines at the outer corners to suggest a sleepy or content expression. Step 5: Position Nose & Mouth Place a small triangle right below the center dividing line. Extend its base outwards symmetrically to form the mouth outline. Unlike humans, cats don't smile openly, so keep the curve gentle but closed. Step 6: Create Whiskers On either side of the nose, extend short perpendicular lines that taper off after a few millimeters. Then branch off shorter strokes from these initial whisker lines. Be sure they're unevenly spaced for natural realism. Step 7: Define Cheeks & Chin Gently shade around the jawline down to where the neck would connect. Don't forget subtle shadows under the chin too. Cats often appear chubby due to their furry cheeks, which we'll mimic later when shading. Step 8: Form Ears Either side of the head needs pointed triangular ears positioned roughly halfway between the eyes and top edge of the head. Their inner edges should align with the vertical division mark made earlier. Vary ear sizes and angles for different breeds if desired. Step 9: Start Shading Use light pressure to fill areas like the ears, noses, and surrounding darker regions. Keep checking against your reference image to ensure correct placement and intensity. Soften transitions between light and shadow for smooth gradients. Step 10: Final Touches Add final details like additional hairs around the face, further defining the eyes, or minor adjustments to make your cat unique. After ensuring everything looks good, gently erase any unnecessary construction lines. Conclusion: Congratulations! You've now drawn a beautiful cat face. Practice regularly to improve proportions and techniques. Soon enough, creating realistic felines becomes second nature. Happy drawing! So she did have a night light after all, huh? The purpose of this activity is to help your child compare different items and put them in order, estimate (guess) their sizes, and then measure to find out. These are all important steps toward understanding measurement.From the Virtual Pre-K: Ready For Math toolkit Safety note: Small items can be choking hazards for young children. Be sure this activity is closely supervised by you. On recycled paper, trace the foot or shoe of each family member. If you have a small family, invite friends to join in. With your child's help, write each person's name inside his/her foot outline. Cut out and compare the paper feet - first two at a time and then the whole group. Who has the biggest foot? Smallest? Help your child put the paper feet in order from smallest to biggest. Use small household items, such as pennies or paperclips, to measure each foot. First guess how many of the item each foot will be, and then find out (e.g., baby's foot is five pennies long and Dad's foot is 15 pennies long). Help your child write the number on each paper foot. When all the feet have been measured ask your child, "Whose foot is longest?" and "Who has the shortest foot?" Next, compare the paper feet with household items by asking questions such as, "Is your foot longer or shorter than the telephone?" "Is Daddy's foot wider or narrower than this straw?" "Is baby's foot bigger or smaller than this cup?" You can also use the paper feet to measure household objects, "How many of Daddy's feet is the rug? How many of baby's feet is the rug? Why?" Hindus believe that all living creatures have a soul. This soul or true "self" of every living being is called the " tman". The soul is believed to be eternal. According to the monistic/pantheistic (non-dualist) theologies of Hinduism (such as Advaita Vedanta school), this Atman is indistinct from Brahman. The goal of life, according to the Advaita school, is to realise that one's soul is identical to supreme soul, that the supreme soul is present in everything and everyone, all life is interconnected and there is oneness in all life. Dualistic schools (see Dvaita and Bhakti) sees Brahman as a Supreme Being separate from individual souls. They worship the Supreme Being variously as Vishnu, Brahma, Shiva, or Shakti, depending upon the sect. God is called "Ishvara", "Bhagavan", "Parameshwara", "Devadu" or "Devi", and these terms have different meanings in different schools of Hinduism. Devi is typically used when refereeing to a female goddess. The most formal kimono have all five of these crests on them. The least-formal formal kimono have just one. Cleaning a bathroom sink drain is an essential part of maintaining good hygiene and preventing blockages or foul odors. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process of cleaning your bathroom sink drain, providing detailed explanations and helpful tips along the way. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Gloves (rubber or latex) 2. Old toothbrush or small brush 3. Baking soda 4. White vinegar 5. Boiling water 6. Plunger (optional) 7. Screwdriver (optional) 8. Wire hanger or plumbing snake (optional) 9. Rag or paper towels ### **Step 1: Gather Your Materials** Before starting the cleaning process, make sure you have all necessary materials within reach. Protect your hands with gloves to avoid direct contact with any residue inside the drain. ### **Step 2: Remove the Stopper** Most bathroom sink drains come with a stopper that can be removed manually or by unscrewing it from beneath the sink. If there's no screw holding the stopper in place, simply lift it up and pull it out. Otherwise, locate the screw underneath the sink and use a screwdriver to remove it before lifting out the stopper. ![Remove the Stopper](<https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/aa/Clean-a-Bathroom-Sink-Drain-Step-2.jpg/aid405550-v4- May I have a little sip too? It was the official name for the new planet. In Roman mythology, Neptune was the god of the sea, identified with the Greek god, Poseidon. What did I do with the cup? At the middle of a black hole, there is a gravitational center called a singularity. It is impossible to see into it because the gravity prevents any light escaping. Around the tiny singularity, there is a large area where light which would normally pass by gets sucked in as well. The edge of this area is called the event horizon. The area beyond the event horizon is the black hole. The gravity of the black hole gets weaker at a distance. The event horizon is the place farthest away from the middle where the gravity is still strong enough to trap light. Once upon a time, there was a brave prince. He loved to learn swordfighting. You like to sit up there and drink! You caught a lot of fishes right? Most plants lose their leaves in the fall when it turns colder. This strategy protects the rest of the plant. Plants usually lose their leaves when conditions in the environment, such as drought or cold, become too harsh for the plant. Plants lose a lot of water through their leaves. The leaves basically sweat, losing water into the air. In drought conditions, losing the leaves helps the tree keep water in other parts of the plant. The tree doesn't dry out as much. Other plants have leaves that can't handle the cold. When the first frost comes, leaves die and drop off. The plants suck back all the sugars and other nutrients, or food, that the leaves were using. Plants store these nutrients through the winter until they need the stored food to grow new leaves in the spring. A different way Evergreens have adapted a different way of surviving. They keep their leaves on. As the weather turns colder, chemicals, or substances, in evergreens begin to change. Special parts of their leaves change fats, proteins and sugars into a kind of antifreeze. This antifreeze substance allows the leaves to stay on the tree during the cold winter. Changing with the times Evergreen trees change their chemistry in other ways too. The whole plant, right down to the fragile twigs, becomes hardier in the cold. Evergreens that grow high in the mountains may need even more protection. These mountain trees build up even more antifreeze chemicals in their leaves. They don't grow tall. That way, they limit their exposure to the wind and cold. Evergreens can grow in high altitudes. They are often the only trees able to grow high in the mountains. There are also equations for this type of kinematics. However, there are different equations for the x-direction and different equations for the y-direction. Galileo proved that the velocity in the x-direction does not change through the whole run. However, the y-direction is affected by the force of gravity, so the y-velocity does change during the run. Sports activity stations will help the student observe and understand that the greater the force applied to an object, the greater the change in speed or direction of the object. Main Curriculum Tie: Science - 3rd Grade Standard 3 Objective 2 Demonstrate that the greater the force applied to an object, the greater the change in speed or direction of the object. Background For Teachers: This activity will be done after all other teaching of 3rd Grade Science Standard III has been taught. Students will already understand push and pull. They will understand that force affects speed and Intended Learning Outcomes: 1. Use Science Process and Thinking Skills 2. Manifest Scientific Attitudes and Interests 3. Understand Science Concepts and Principles 4. Communicate Effectively Using Science Language and Reasoning. Invitation to Learn Set a soccer ball on the floor and stare at it. When someone asks you what you are doing, tell them that you are playing soccer. If no one asks, wait until someone says anything at all and say, "Shhhh, I'm doing something." Then they'll ask what you're doing. Of course they will make some sort of argument. Launch into a discussion about what else you would need to do to play soccer. - Beforehand, setup 10 stations for the students to go to, each with one sport activity. The equipment can be real or toys. You must include at least two sports from each category on the Sports that Push and Pull sheet. You may set up inside or outside. Make sure the stations are numbered in some way, so students know where to go next. Leave instructions for any sport that you think the kids might not be familiar with. - In the classroom, divide the students into 10 groups (2- 3 students per group). Pass out a pencil, a clipboard, and one Forces and Motion in Sports paper to each group. Have the students put their names at the top and listen while you explain the directions. - Explain that when they get to a station, they are to write the name of the sport in the left column before they play with the equipment. Then, they are to use the equipment to do the sport. While they are playing, they need to pay attention to what kind of force(s) they use to play. Tell them that they will have three minutes to play with the equipment. When you blow the whistle, they are to take one minute to finish filling out the rest of the row for their station. Then you blow the whistle one more time, and they are to go to the next - Explain that to fill out the sheet, they must mark either the pull or push box, or both, in the middle column. Then they must write a simple phrase to explain the pull/push force that they used. Example for volleyball: push when my hand hit the ball OR my hand pushed the ball. - Take the students to where you've setup the stations. Send one group to each station and tell them to start. Time them for three minutes, and then blow the whistle. Make sure they are writing. Give them about one minute to write, and then blow the whistle again so they go to the next station. - When each group has been to all the stations, clean up and go back to the classroom. Put Class Survey of Forces and Motion in Sports on the overhead projector. Write the sport name in the left column and ask who thought it was push, pull, or both. Tally for each sport. - Discuss the results with the class. - Make a list of all the Summer Olympics events. This is especially powerful if it is an Olympics year. Then sort them into push, pull, or both. - Disabled athletes are able to compete in various sports using assistive devices or other technologies. Have students do research and share their findings. Disabled athletes could be invited to the classroom to talk to students. - Send home an assignment as homework where the student and their family get together outside and each throws a ball as far as they can. If they don't have older/younger brothers and sisters, other neighborhood folks can join in. Have them record the age of the thrower, and then how far they threw it. Then they must explain to their family how the littler kids didn't throw the ball with as much force as the bigger kids, so it didn't go as far. - Send home a copy of the Forces and Motion in Sports paper for the students to fill out with their family. They may not include any that you had as a station at school. - Give the students each a copy of Bob Goes Adventuring, a green crayon, and a purple crayon. Tell them to read the story and put a green circle around the words every place they read about a push, and a purple rectangle every place they read about a pull. Remind them that some places might have both a green circle and purple rectangle. For example, when they read about Bob swimming, they will have a green circle for his legs pushing his body, and a purple rectangle for his hands pulling his body. - Have the students choose three of the sports they participated in during the activity to write about. In one paragraph for each sport, have students explain how different weight and different amount of force affects the play of that sport. Lewis, V. K., & Shaha, S. H. (2003). Maximizing learning and attitudinal gains through integrated curricula. Education. 123, 537-547. Three studies were done comparing integrated curriculum versus single subject curriculum. It was shown that integrated curriculum was significantly better for learning and student attitude. Students were engaged, cared about the lesson subjects, and were able to retain learning better. Aft throws herself to the floor] Title: How to Delete Your Search History on wikiHow wikiHow is an excellent resource for learning new skills and gaining knowledge on various topics. However, if you're concerned about your privacy or want to clean up your search history, deleting your searches on wikiHow can be helpful. This step-by-step guide will walk you through the process with clear instructions and additional information to help you understand why each step is important. ### Access your wikiHow account settings 1. Open your web browser and go to www.wikihow.com. 2. Log into your wikiHow account by clicking the 'Log in' button at the top right corner of the page. Enter your email address and password when prompted. 3. Once logged in, click on your username (top right) and select 'Settings' from the dropdown menu. 4. You are now in your Account Settings. Proceed to the next steps to delete your search history. Key tip: Keep track of your login credentials as you navigate to your account settings. ### Clear your search history 5. From your Account Settings page, locate the section labeled 'History.' Click on 'Search History.' 6. On the following page, you'll see all your past searches displayed as links. To remove a single search query, simply click on the 'X' icon beside it. A confirmation box will appear; click 'OK' to finalize removal. 7. If you wish to delete all search queries simultaneously, look for the 'Clear All' link near the bottom left side of the page. After clicking this option, another confirmation message will pop up-click 'Yes' to erase all entries. 8. Take note that once deleted, there is no way to recover individual items within your search history. Make sure you have saved any necessary information before clearing your entire history. 9. Double-check that your search history has been cleared by refreshing the page. The list should either display only recent searches after removing specific ones or show no previous records upon clearing everything. Guideline: Be cautious while using the 'Clear All' function since it permanently removes all stored data without any possibility of recovery. ### Sign out of your wikiHow account 10. After successfully deleting your search history, remember to log out of your account. Hover over your username again and choose 'Sign Out' from the options presented. By following these 10 steps, you can confidently manage and maintain your privacy on wikiHow. Regularly checking and cleaning your search history ensures that others cannot access your browsing activities and safeguards sensitive information. Mom I'm not going to put salt on this. Title: How to Change the Name of an iPhone in iOS 5 Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through changing the name of your iPhone using Apple's mobile operating system, iOS 5. This process is helpful if you want to personalize your device, differentiate between multiple iPhones in a shared environment, or when preparing the phone for sale or gifting. By following these steps closely, you can accomplish this task with ease. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Make sure that your iPhone is updated to iOS 5 or later versions. * Use clear and concise names without special characters which could cause issues during syncing processes. * Keep the name short but descriptive for easy identification. Step-by-step Instructions: 1. **Unlock your iPhone**: To begin, ensure that your iPhone is unlocked and at its home screen. Unlocking prevents any accidental changes made while securing the device. 2. **Access Settings App**: Tap on the gray 'Settings' app icon, usually located on one of the home screens. The settings app allows access to various configurations and preferences available within the iOS ecosystem. 3. **Navigate to General Section**: From the list presented after opening the Settings app, scroll down until you find the 'General' option (usually near the top) and tap on it. It has a gear icon next to it. This section contains fundamental device settings such as network connections, software updates, and more. 4. **Select About Option**: After tapping 'General', look towards the bottom where you will see an entry labeled 'About'. Selecting this reveals information about your iPhone including model number, serial number, storage capacity, etc. 5. **Edit Name Field**: Once inside the 'About' menu, locate the first field named 'Name'. Beneath it should be displayed the current name assigned to your iPhone. Tap anywhere within this field. Upon doing so, the keyboard should appear prompting you to edit the text. 6. **Input New Device Name**: Delete the existing name by pressing and holding over it till 'Delete' appears then swipe left across the word. Now type in the new preferred name using the virtual keyboard provided. Remember our Key Tips above regarding naming conventions. 7. **Confirm Changes**: Check spelling and formatting before proceeding further. When satisfied, press 'Home' button located either below the screen (for older models) or hidden within the power button (on newer ones). 8. **Exit Settings Application**: Locate the 'Settings' app icon again from any home screen and tap on it. Confirm that the altered name is correctly shown under 'Name' field in the 'About' menu. 9. **(Optional) Verify Syncing Devices**: If applicable, test whether other devices like computers, tablets, or speakers recognize the changed name. For instance, connect your iPhone via USB cable to a computer previously synced; check if the new name shows up correctly. Conclusion: Congratulations! You have successfully changed the name of your iPhone running iOS 5. With these simple yet effective steps, customizing your device becomes straightforward and hassle-free. Always remember to consider readability, brevity, and uniqueness when renaming your devices. Title: How to Get the Legendary Dogs in FireRed Introduction: In Pokemon FireRed, there are three legendary dogs that you can catch - Entei, Raikou, and Suicune. These dogs have unique abilities and appearances, making them highly desirable among trainers. This tutorial will guide you through the process of getting all three legendary dogs in FireRed. Step 1: Defeat Team Rocket at the Radio Tower Key Tip: Make sure your team is strong enough to defeat the Rockets. The first step towards obtaining the legendary dogs involves defeating Team Rocket at the Radio Tower in Goldenrod City. Once inside, navigate through the tower until you reach the top floor where Giovanni awaits. After defeating him, return to the second floor, speak with Eusine, and then head outside. The legendary dog that appeared before you when you entered the tower (Entei, Raikou or Suicune) has now fled. Step 2: Obtain the National Pokedex Key Guideline: Complete Professor Oak's tasks after beating the Elite Four. To capture all three legendary dogs, you need the National Pokedex. To get this, beat the Elite Four and champion, then visit Professor Oak in Pallet Town who will upgrade your Pokedex. Step 3: Track Down the First Legendary Dog Key Information: Each dog appears randomly in different locations across Kanto. Now comes the challenging part; tracking down the dogs! They appear randomly throughout Kanto, so keep an eye out for shaking grass patches which indicate their presence. When you find one, engage it in battle but do not catch it yet. Instead, weaken it significantly (around 50% HP), flee from the fight, then save your game. Step 4: Activate the Dog's Second Appearance Key Strategy: Trigger another encounter by entering certain buildings or areas. After saving, enter any building or area (like going into a house or cave) and exit immediately. Check the map again for shaking grass. If the dog doesn't reappear right away, don't worry-it might take several attempts before it respawns. Keep trying until you see the shaking grass again. Step 5: Catch the Weakened Dog Key Advice: Use status conditions like sleep or paralysis to increase your chances of catching it. Once the dog reappears, approach it carefully. Since you already weakened it earlier, you should have no problem finishing it off. However, instead of doing so, try putting it to sleep or inducing paralysis using moves like Thunder Wave or Sleep Powder. Then throw Ultra Balls until you successfully capture it. Step 6: Repeat Steps Three Through Five For Other Two Dogs Key Reminder: Be patient as these dogs roam freely around Kanto. Unfortunately, capturing one dog does not guarantee immediate encounters with others. You must repeat steps three through five for each of the remaining two dogs. Remember to be patient, persistent, and observant during this process. Conclusion: Capturing the legendary dogs in FireRed requires time, effort, and lots of patience. But following these steps correctly increases your odds of adding these powerful creatures to your collection. Good luck, trainer! When your child is studying alone make sure he/she creates 2 piles: the "I already know pile" and the "I need to learn pile". Don't spend too much time on the "I already know pile". Shuffle the pile every once in a while in order to change the order. 2. Chew - Chew your way to the top! Some students just need help to become activated, to focus a little movement can go a long way! Something as simple as chewing gum might help him/her focus. 3. Walk - Encourage your child to walk while he/she reads/reviews his/her cue cards. Again, a little movement can go a long way! 4. Rainbow - Colour code notes or cue cards. Be creative. Make them look great! Create and follow a pattern or strategy. 5. Draw - draw, doodle, illustrate concepts Have fun! Cut pictures from magazines and make a collage or two (or ten!). The more meaningful, clever, silly, creative the illustrations the more the concepts will "stick". 6. Teacher - Ask your child to create, administer and mark a test it for you to take. Let them be "the teacher"! 7. Sing - Take a familiar tune (e.g. "Happy Birthday", "Twinkle, Twinkle") and write new words to the song based on the study notes/concepts to be covered. 8. Create - Use well known, or help your child create his/her own mnemonics to help him/her to memorize facts. [Row to MOT about positioning the table with reply] Venus has been a beautiful sight in the evening sky for months, but at the end of April it's reaching a brightness peak, magnitude -4.7. By then, if you look at it in a telescope or even good binoculars, you'll see it has waned to a crescent. That's a bit non-obvious: when the moon is a crescent, it's a lot fainter than a full moon. So why is Venus brightest in its crescent phase? It has to do with their orbits. The moon is always about the same distance away, about 385,000 km or 239,000 miles (I've owned cars with more miles than that!), though it varies a little, from 362,600 km at perigee to 405,400 km at apogee. When we look at the full moon, not only are we seeing the whole Earth-facing surface illuminated, but the central part of that light is reflecting straight up off the moon's surface. When we look at a crescent moon, we're seeing light that's near the moon's sunrise or sunset point -- dimmer and more spread out than the concentrated light of noon -- and in addition we're seeing less of it. Venus, in contrast, varies its distance from us immensely. We can't see Venus when it's "full", because it's on the other side of the sun from us and lost in the sun's glow. It'll next be there a year from now, in April of 2013. But if we could see it when it's full, Venus would be a distant 1.7 AU from us. An AU is an Astronomical Unit, the average distance of the earth from the sun or about 89 million miles, so Venus when it's full is about 170 million miles away. Its disk is a tiny 9.9 arcseconds (an arcsecond is 1/3600 of a degree) -- about the size of Mars this month. In contrast, when we look at the crescent Venus around the end of this month, although we're only seeing about 28% of its surface illuminated, and that only with glancing twilight rays, it's much closer to us -- less than half an AU, or about 45 million miles -- and its disk extends a huge 37 arcseconds, bigger than Jupiter this month. Of course, eventually, as Venus pulls between us and the sun, its crescent gets so slim that even its huge size can't compensate. So its peak brightness happens when those two curves cross, when the disk is somewhere around 27% illuminated, as happens at the end of this month and the beginning of May. Exactly when? Good question. The RASC Handbook says Venus' "greatest illuminated extent" is on April 30, but PyEphem and XEphem say Venus is actually brighter from May 3-8 ... and when it emerges from the sun's glare and moves into the morning sky in June, it'll be slightly brighter still, peaking at magnitude -4.8 in the first week of July.) Tracking Venus with PyEphem When I started my Shallow Sky column this month, I saw the notice of Venus's maximum brightness and greatest illuminated extent in the RASC Handbook. But I wanted more details -- how much did its distance and size really change, when would the brightness peak again as it emerged from the sun's glare, when would it next be "full"? PyEphem made it easy to calculate all this. Just create an calculate its values for any date of interest, then print out parameters like phase, mag, earth_distance and size. In just a few minutes of programming, I had a nice table of Venus data. import ephem venus = ephem.Venus() print '%10s %6s %6s %6s %6s' % ('date', '%', 'mag', 'dist', 'size') def print_venus(when) : venus.compute(when) fmt = '%02d-%02d-%02d %6.2f %6.2f %6.2f %6.2f' trip = when.triple() print fmt % (trip, trip, trip, venus.phase, venus.mag, venus.earth_distance, venus.size) # Loop from the beginning of 2012 through the middle of 2013: d = ephem.date('2012') end_date = ephem.date('2013/6/1') while d < end_date : print_venus(d) # Add a week: d = ephem.date(d + ephem.hour * 24) I've found PyEphem very handy for calculations like this -- and it's great to be able to double-check listings in other publications. Title: How to Cope When You and Your Friend Both Like the Same Guy Who May Like You Introduction: Navigating romantic feelings can be challenging, especially when those feelings are directed towards the same person as your friend. This situation may bring up complex emotions such as jealousy, confusion, and fear of losing a friendship. However, by following these steps, you will learn how to cope with this predicament while maintaining self-respect and preserving your relationship with your friend. Step 1: Acknowledge Your Feelings Recognize that having conflicting emotions is normal. It's essential to accept and understand what you feel before proceeding further. By acknowledging your feelings early on, you prevent them from escalating into uncontrollable reactions later. Key Tip: Be honest with yourself about your true feelings without judgment or shame. Step 2: Communicate Openly With Your Friend Discuss the matter with your friend openly but tactfully. Express your concerns and listen attentively to their thoughts. Maintain empathy throughout the conversation; remember, they might also experience similar mixed feelings. Guideline: Use phrases like, "I value our friendship, so I thought it would be best if we talked about..." or "I care about your feelings, just as much as mine." Step 3: Establish Boundaries Define clear boundaries regarding interactions with the guy in question. Agree upon mutually respectful rules to avoid any unnecessary tension between you two. For instance, decide whether you should both attend social gatherings where he'll be present. Key Tip: Reiterate the importance of your friendship during boundary setting. Say something along the lines of, "Our friendship matters more than anything else," to ensure neither party feels betrayed. Step 4: Evaluate His Interest Level Observe his behavior carefully to gauge if he reciprocates your affections. Look out for signs such as prolonged eye contact, frequent conversations, shared smiles, physical touch (if appropriate), and genuine interest in knowing more about you. Important Note: Remember, interpreting signals incorrectly could lead to misunderstandings. So, tread cautiously and try confirming your suspicions through casual conversations rather than jumping to conclusions. Step 5: Consider Professional Help If managing these emotions becomes overwhelming, consider seeking help from a therapist or counselor. They provide an objective perspective and guide you through coping strategies effectively. Step 6: Prioritize Self-Care During stressful times, taking care of mental health becomes even more critical. Engage in activities that rejuvenate you, maintain a healthy lifestyle, get enough sleep, eat nutritious food, exercise regularly, and practice mindfulness meditation. Step 7: Respect His Decision Once you have all facts, respect whatever decision he makes concerning your feelings toward him. If things don't go as planned, remind yourself that there are plenty of other opportunities waiting ahead. Final Thought: While dealing with such situations isn't easy, keeping communication open, prioritizing self-care, and showing respect for everyone involved can make handling complicated emotional landscapes smoother. Always bear in mind that every challenge presents an opportunity for growth - personally and within relationships. Autumn Leaf Walk How you can help at home: In this fun and educational activity your preschooler explores leaves she has collected outside. By Jeanie McLoughlin Go outdoors and collect fallen leaves and seeds from different trees. Bring the leaves and seeds indoors and have your child sort them. What You'll Need - Leaves and seeds from a variety of trees - A bag or basket to hold the leaves and seeds you collect Here's How To Do It Take a walk with your child in your neighborhood or a park and collect fallen leaves from a variety of trees. If you can find them, also collect seeds (such as acorns, pine cones or eucalyptus pods) from the trees. Talk to your child about the trees and the seasonal change. Discuss how deciduous trees lose their leaves while evergreen trees keep their leaves year-round. Enjoy the outdoors and exercise. (Note: Make sure to avoid poison oak, sumac, etc.) Once you get home, sort the leaves and seeds by color, shape and size. This helps your child develop observational skills such as sorting and classifying. Help your child build her vocabulary by learning the names of a variety of trees, moving from the general (tree) to more specific (oak, maple, ash, pine.) Ask her questions such as: - "What can you tell me about these leaves?" - "Which ones are alike? How are they alike?" - "Which ones are different?" "How are they different?" - Help your child link each seed to its leaf (e.g. oak leaf and acorn; pine cone and pine needle; walnut leaf and walnut; maple and winged seed.) - Create an autumn leaf collage. Help your child glue the leaves and seeds on to a piece of paper. Then you can label the names of the types of leaves and seeds. - Trace a leaf onto a piece of paper. Have your child color in the leaf. - Go to the library and take out a picture book on trees to learn more. Mercury may be seen as a transition metal, but it is normally seen as a post-transition metal. It is in Group 12 of the periodic table. Mercury has seven stable (nonradioactive) isotopes. Hg is the most common isotope. Mercury makes a blue to ultraviolet color in a tube when a spark is passed through it. The ultraviolet light can kill germs or light fluorescent lamps. Title: How to Get the 121st and Final Star in Super Mario Galaxy Super Mario Galaxy is a beloved 3D platformer game developed by Nintendo for the Wii console. The game features 120 regular stars and an additional secret star, which can be quite challenging to obtain. This comprehensive guide will walk you through every step necessary to acquire that elusive final star. **Step 1:** Complete the Game's Main Story To start your journey towards getting the 121st star, you must first complete the main story of Super Mario Galaxy. You need to rescue Princess Peach from Bowser and collect all 120 Power Stars available throughout the game. These are scattered across various galaxies, so explore thoroughly! *Why this step matters:* Completing the main story unlocks new areas and challenges required to find the last star. It also ensures you have acquired enough skills and abilities needed for more complex levels. **Step 2:** Access Grandmaster Galaxy After rescuing Princess Peach, return to the Comet Observatory where she resides. Speak with her, then head back to Rosalina's room. Here, you should see a new galaxy - Grandmaster Galaxy - among the selection. Enter it. *Key tip*: Make sure you save your progress before entering Grandmaster Galaxy as there's no turning back once you begin. **Step 3:** Prepare for Extreme Challenges Grandmaster Galaxy consists of six individual stages, each one progressively harder than the last. To reach the final stage, you must earn at least two purple coins in each previous level. Be prepared for some tough trials ahead! *Guideline*: Take your time learning each course layout, remembering enemy locations, and mastering tricky jumps. Patience and practice are crucial here. **Step 4:** Collect Purple Coins In each level of Grandmaster Galaxy (excluding the last), search high and low for hidden purple coins. There are always exactly two per stage; collecting them all advances you further into the galaxy. *Tip*: Some coins may require advanced techniques or power-ups to reach. Don't hesitate to experiment with different strategies. **Step 5:** Navigate Through Dreadnought Galaxy Once you've collected all twelve purple coins, you'll gain access to the final stage: Dreadnought Galaxy. This grand finale pits you against numerous obstacles and enemies, including a boss fight against Megaleg. *Guidepoint*: Stay calm under pressure. Remember what you've learned thus far about controlling Mario and utilize those skills to navigate through this intense level. **Step 6:** Defeat Megaleg Megaleg is the final boss in Super Mario Galaxy. He has three forms, each requiring specific tactics to defeat. First, avoid his attacks while waiting for him to expose his weak spot. Then, when he does, hit him with spin attacks. Repeat until he changes form. Keep attacking until he's defeated. *Key strategy*: Use Luma to help target Megaleg's weak points during the battle. Also, remember that you can jump off walls to dodge certain attacks. **Step 7:** Claim Your Reward Upon defeating Megaleg, you'll receive the 121st Power Star. Congratulations! You've completed Super Mario Galaxy 100%! Remember, persistence pays off. If you find yourself struggling with any part of these steps, take a break, come back later with fresh eyes, and try again. The US wet pint, or more commonly 'pint', is the unit used to measure volume in the United States. It is more common than the dry pint which is used for non-liquid volume measurements. 1 US pint is exactly equal to 473,176.473 mm, defined by the international yard and pound agreement. Beginning Reading Lesson Beginning Reading Lesson by Amanda Talley Rationale: This lesson will help children identify /A/, the phoneme represented by a_e. Students will learn to recognize /A/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation (waving while saying "aaaayy!"; think Fonzie from Happy Days) and the grapheme a_e, practice finding /A/ in words, and apply phoneme awareness with /A/ in phonetic cue reading by distinguishing words with similar vowel sounds. Materials: Primary paper and pencil; letter boxes, letter tiles: a,b,c,d,e,f,k,l,m, p,r,s,t. Tongue twister on chart: "Abe the ape ate Amy's acorn.", Jane and Babe (Educational Insights, 1990) for all students; word cards with MADE, ACE, LAKE, GATE, and BRAVE, paper and crayons, assessment worksheet identifying pictures with /A/ (URL below). 1. Say: In our language there are times when two letters sound a lot a like, for example 'c' and 'k'. The tricky part is learning what letters stand for--the mouth moves we make as we say words. Today we're going to work on spotting the mouth move /A/. We can spell /A/ with letters a_e. The blank stands for another letter which comes between the a and e. /A/ makes the same sound as if you were saying hey to someone without pronouncing the /h/ sound, "/A/!". 2. Practice with me, /A/, /A/, /A/. [Wave to one another] Notice how you shaped your mouth. When we say /A/, we are actually saying the letter name A. 3. Let me show you how to find /A/ in the word pail. I'm going to stretch fade out in super slow motion and listen for me saying the letter A. Ff-aa-dd-e. Slower: Ff-aaaaaa-dd-e. There it was! I felt my mouth say the letter A. I can feel the /A / in fade. 4. Let's try a tongue twister [on chart]. "Abe the ape ate Amy's acorn." Everybody say it three times together. Now say it again, and this time, stretch the /A/ at the beginning of the words. " Aaaa-b-e the aaaa-p-e aaaa-t-e Aaaa-m-y's aaaa-c-o-r-n " Try it again, and this time break it off the word: " /A/be the /A/pe /A/te /A/my's /A/corn." 5. [Have students take out primary paper and pencil]. We use letter a_e to spell /A/. Let's write the lowercase letters a_e. Start just below the fence and make a curve like you are about to write the letter o. When you get back to where you started on the fence, draw a straight line down. That's the letter a, but to make the /A/ sound, we must imagine another letter following the a, then an e. For this, leave enough space after the letter a for an imaginary letter. Start in between the fence and the sidewalk, then draw a straight line out to the right. Next, without picking your pencil up, draw a c by curving up to the fence, down to the sidewalk, and around stopping before you complete a circle. That is a_e. I want to see everybody's a_e. After you are done, I will come around and give you a sticker. Then practice writing five more. 6. Call on students to answer and tell how they knew: Do you hear /A/ in maze or mad? Ball or bake? Age or at? Fall or fade ? Face or fad? Say: Let's see if you can spot the mouth move /A/ in some words. Put your thumb up if you hear /A/: The, able, snatch, tailed, him, pain, plate. 7. Pass out letter tiles and letterboxes. Say, "We are going to practice spelling the sounds we say." Model first word, pale. /p/ /A/ /l/ e. Pale. Instruct the students to practice along with the first word. Next call out each word as they spell it out using their materials: 2-- [ate], 3--[fate, male, tame], 4--[ place, spade, brake, blame], and 5--[scrape]. 8. Say: "We are going to read a book called, Jane and Babe. In this book, we will learn about a woman Jane, who works as a zookeeper and takes care of a lion named, Babe. One day, while babe is sleeping, Jane wakes him up! Will Babe be mad? Read to find out! Put your thumbs up if you hear /A/ as you read." Ask the students to read it individually. Come around and ask children to say words with a_e and draw out the /A/ sound. Ask children if they can think of other words with /A/. Ask them to think of something that has the /A/ sound . Then have each student write the name of the object and draw a picture of their /A/ object. Have the students exchange papers and read other students /A/ words. 9. Show MADE and model how to decide if it is made or mall: /A/, so this word is mm-aaa-dd-e, made. You try some: ACE: ace or at? LAKE: lamp or lake? GATE: gate or gab? BRAVE: brat or brave? 10. For assessment, distribute the worksheet. Students are to color the pictures that contain long a blue, and those with short a gray to discover what Ann and Abe are looking for. Call students individually to read the phonetic cue words from step #8. Bruce Murray. Emergent Literacy Lesson. "Brush Your Teeth with F". http://www.auburn.edu/academic/education/reading_genie/sightings/murrayel.html "Jane and Babe" Educational Insights, Carson, CA (USA). 1990. They will write a story about what the character Amelia the Pigeon would see if she flew over their school or home. Students will be introduced to aerial photography and the idea of different points of view. Imagine our school. You know what it looks like when you walk in the front door, right? Now imagine that you are sitting on a cloud high above our school and looking down on it. How does it look now? What's different? What can you see that you couldn't see if you were just walking on the ground? As you sit on that cloud high above, take a look around. Can you see the neighborhood the school is in? Can you see your house? What else can you see from this point of view? [Show students an aerial map of the school on Bing maps. Type the school address in the Search box. When the road map of your school neighborhood appears, point to the Globe icon at the bottom of the map, and then select Aerial. Next, zoom in.] Here is a photograph of the school neighborhood taken from an airplane. This is called an aerial photograph. What can you see from this point of view? What can't you see from this point of view? [If no aerial photograph of your neighborhood is available, show students an aerial photograph of a famous location.] Let's take a closer look from this point of view. Right now we're looking at a basic aerial map, which is made of photos taken from the air. [Point to the Aerial View icon on the bottom of the map, and then select Bird's-Eye.] Here's another aerial map. This one is called a Bird's-Eye map. This is also made up of photos taken from the air, but instead of looking straight down, the camera looks from above at an angle. That little shift in the angle of the point of view can give you a better view of what's on the ground. What can you see from this aerial view that you couldn't see from the first one, looking straight down on our school? [Switch back to the aerial view to show them the difference.] Both of these aerial views give us a lot of information that we don't get by looking straight ahead at the things around us. Can you think of any reasons why someone might need or want an aerial photograph of a neighborhood or a location? In this activity you will look at your world from a new point of view-an aerial view from up above in the sky-and write a story about what you see. Follow the steps below to guide your students through this lesson plan. See student handout link at right. Ask students to draw pictures of what Amelia the Pigeon would see if she flew over their school or home. See student handout link at right. Ask students to explore perspective further by doing the following: Take three digital photographs of an object: one from directly in front of the object, one from directly above the object (aerial), and one from above the object but at an angle (Bird's-Eye View), and then comparing what they see. What features and dimensions of the object appear in each perspective? They can insert the photos and their descriptions into a Microsoft Word document. Draw one object from each of the three perspectives and then write about what features and dimensions of the object appear from each perspective. Ask students to read some or all of the remaining chapters of "Amelia the Pigeon." They can use Bing maps to look at some of the places described in those chapters, such as a park or a zoo. Ask students to research the life of Amelia Earhart and write a story about something she might have seen from her airplane cockpit. Assess students on the story they write describing what Amelia would see if she flew over their home or school. Viewing the pictures on the Bing maps website should help inspire some creative writing. Two photographers were accidentally hired to take pictures at the same birthday party. Will each photographer's pictures provide the same impression of the party? Why or why not? How are photos different from drawings or paintings? When there is no political order, more than one government or political authority might sometimes compete for the same food, oil, land, or groups of people. The word "Anarchy" to used to describe this. However, because there is "more than one" competing authority, a better word might be "polyarchy". The difference between "anarchy" and "polyarchy" is important to someone who thinks that true anarchy would work well. The word for someone who thinks this is "anarchist", and the word for this kind of thinking is "anarchism". Anarchism has been thought about for hundreds of years. The book called "The Politics of Individualism" attempts to teach its readers that anarchism is not always simply an opposition to the government, but a complicated political movement against the domination of few people. Title: How to Click a Computer Mouse Introduction: A computer mouse is a hand-held device that controls movement and selection of content on a computer screen. The most common action performed with a mouse is clicking, which can be done in various ways such as left click, right click, double click, and drag click. This tutorial will provide an in-depth guide on how to effectively click a computer mouse while offering key tips and guidelines. Step 1: Placement Place your dominant hand over the top of the mouse with your fingers slightly curved. Your index finger should rest lightly on the left button (also known as the primary or main button), middle finger on the scroll wheel, and ring finger on the right button (also known as the secondary button). Make sure your wrist is straight and relaxed, avoiding any unnecessary tension. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Proper placement ensures comfort during extended usage and reduces strain. - Keeping your wrist straight prevents repetitive strain injuries. Step 2: Left Click To perform a left click, gently press down on the left button using your index finger until you hear a soft click sound. Release the pressure immediately after hearing the click. Avoid pressing too hard or holding the button down for prolonged periods. Key Tips & Guidelines: - A gentle touch is sufficient; no need to exert excessive force. - Releasing the button promptly avoids accidental multiple clicks. Step 3: Right Click Perform a right click by applying similar pressure to the right button with your ring finger. It's important to maintain proper posture and alignment throughout this process. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Use the same light touch used for left clicking. - Remember to release the button quickly to prevent unintended actions. Step 4: Double Click For a double click, rapidly press the left button twice within a short time frame without moving the cursor between clicks. Most operating systems recognize two quick clicks as a single command. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Maintain consistent timing between clicks for optimal results. - Practice regularly to improve speed and accuracy. Step 5: Drag Click Drag clicking involves keeping the left button pressed while moving the mouse around. To do this, hold down the left button and move the cursor across the screen. Once complete, release the button. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Be mindful of maintaining correct posture even when performing multi-step operations like dragging. - Consistent pressure keeps the connection steady during dragging tasks. Conclusion: Congratulations! You now possess the knowledge required to proficiently utilize one of the fundamental functions of a computer mouse - clicking. Regular practice and adherence to these steps guarantee improved skills, efficiency, and overall user experience. Happy computing! It gets hot and it goes off. Delicious [pretends to eat] can I have some? Title: How to Communicate with a Difficult Spouse Introduction: Effective communication is crucial in any relationship, especially marriage. However, communicating with a difficult spouse can be challenging and may lead to misunderstandings, conflicts, or feelings of frustration. This guide will provide you with steps and techniques to help improve communication with your challenging partner. Remember that change takes time and patience, so don't expect instant results. Step 1: Choose the Right Time and Place Find a quiet, comfortable space where you both feel at ease. Avoid distractions such as TV, phones, or children. Schedule a dedicated time to talk when neither of you feels rushed or stressed. Choosing the right environment sets the stage for open and productive conversation. Key Tip: Make sure both partners feel prepared and calm before diving into sensitive topics. Step 2: Define Your Goals Before starting the conversation, clarify what you hope to accomplish. Are you seeking resolution, understanding, or compromise? Having clear goals will help keep the discussion focused and constructive. Key Guideline: Be specific about your objectives; ambiguity can lead to confusion and misinterpretation. Step 3: Use "I" Statements Instead of accusing your spouse ("You never listen to me"), express your own thoughts and emotions using "I" statements ("I feel unheard when I speak, and I would appreciate it if you could give me your full attention"). "I" statements foster self-expression and reduce blame, making it easier for your spouse to respond without feeling attacked. Key Tip: Practice active listening by repeating back what you heard in your own words to ensure mutual understanding. Step 4: Validate Their Feelings Acknowledge your spouse's perspective and validate their emotions, even if you disagree. Show empathy by saying things like "I understand why you might feel that way," or "That sounds really tough." Validation doesn't mean you agree but shows respect for their viewpoint. Key Guideline: Demonstrate appreciation for their willingness to share their thoughts and feelings. Step 5: Stay Calm and Composed Maintain a relaxed posture and use a gentle tone of voice. Deep breathing exercises can help manage anger and anxiety during heated discussions. By remaining composed, you model positive behavior and encourage a more civil dialogue. Key Tip: Take breaks if necessary; stepping away from an intense situation can prevent escalation and allow both parties to regroup. Step 6: Focus on Solving One Issue at a Time Tackling multiple problems simultaneously can overwhelm both partners and hinder progress. Instead, concentrate on resolving one issue before moving onto the next. Breaking down complex matters into smaller parts makes them more manageable. Key Guideline: Establish action items and deadlines for addressing each concern. Step 7: Seek Professional Help If communication remains consistently difficult despite your efforts, consider consulting a therapist or counselor specializing in relationships. A neutral third party can facilitate conversations, offer guidance, and teach new skills to enhance overall communication. Key Tip: Don't view therapy as a last resort; early intervention can prevent further damage to the relationship. Conclusion: Improved communication with a difficult spouse requires practice, patience, and persistence. Implementing these strategies can help create a healthier, more fulfilling partnership. Keep in mind that every individual and relationship is unique, so tailor these suggestions to fit your specific circumstances.
